name: ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES

topics.title: Introduction to Tribology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT831 - ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Tribology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Tribology** is the science and engineering of interacting surfaces in relative motion. It encompasses the study and application of the principles of **friction**, **wear**, and **lubrication**. The term was formally coined in the landmark **Jost Report (1966)** commissioned by the British Department of Education and Science, although the science itself dates back to ancient Egypt (~2400 BC) where animal fats were used to reduce friction when transporting large stones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim per Syllabus):** *"Tribology is the science and technology of interacting surfaces in relative motion and of related subjects and practices. It deals with friction, wear and lubrication."*

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Tribology

The discipline of tribology is built on three interdependent pillars:

| Pillar | Definition | Primary Concern |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Friction** | Tangential resistance to motion between two contacting bodies | Energy loss (heat) |
| **Wear** | Progressive loss of material from a surface due to relative motion | Surface degradation |
| **Lubrication** | Process of reducing friction and wear by introducing a substance (lubricant) between surfaces | Performance & life |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Skate and the Sand Pit:**
>
> Imagine pushing a heavy wooden crate across two different surfaces:
> - On a **rough sand pit**, you push hard, sweat pours, and the wood beneath is gradually gouged — high **friction** + high **wear**, no **lubrication**.
> - On a **wet, polished marble floor**, the same crate glides effortlessly — **lubricant** (water film) separates the surfaces.
>
> Tribology is the engineer's discipline of quantifying *exactly how* and *how much* the surface behavior changes between these two extremes, and then designing systems (oils, greases, coatings, nanomaterials) to control it.

### 1.4 Why Tribology Matters — Economic Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Jost Report** famously estimated that approximately **1.5% of the United Kingdom's Gross National Product (GNP)** could be saved through better tribological practices. Equivalent estimates for industrialized nations hover around **5–7% of GDP** (Holinski, 2000). In automotive engines alone, ~**33%** of fuel energy is lost directly to engine friction.

### 1.5 The "Tribology Triangle"

Every tribological contact is bounded by three entities:
1. **Surface (Solid)** — the structural body
2. **Counter-surface** — the opposing body
3. **Interfacial medium (Lubricant)** — gas, liquid, or solid film

Together these form the **Tribological System** (German standard **DIN 50320**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Tribological Contacts

A tribological contact is characterized by **kinematic**, **geometric**, and **material** parameters:

**A. Kinematic Classification (Type of Motion)**
- **Sliding Contact** — Pure translation (e.g., piston ring against cylinder liner).
- **Rolling Contact** — Pure rotation without slip (e.g., ball bearing rolling element).
- **Sliding + Rolling Contact** — Combined (e.g., cam-follower, gear teeth).

**B. Geometric Classification (Conformal vs. Non-Conformal)**
- **Conformal Contact** — surfaces match in shape (e.g., journal bearing). Contact pressure is low.
- **Non-Conformal (Counterformal) Contact** — surfaces mismatch (e.g., ball-on-flat). Very high Hertzian contact pressure.

**C. Lubrication Regime Classification (Most Important — Direct KTU Question Target)**

The **Stribeck Curve** (1902) classifies lubrication into four regimes based on the **Hersey Number** (ηN/P):

$$
H = \frac{\eta N}{P}
$$

where:
- η (or μ) = dynamic viscosity of lubricant (Pa·s or cP)
- N = rotational/entrainment speed (Hz or rev/s)
- P = load per unit projected contact area (Pa)

| Regime | λ-ratio range | Film behavior | Dominant mechanism |
|--------|---------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Boundary Lubrication (BL)** | λ < 1 | Surface asperities dominate; partial solid contact | Boundary films (EP/AW additives) |
| **Mixed Lubrication (ML)** | 1 ≤ λ ≤ 3 | Partial film separation; asperity + film load share | Transition zone |
| **Hydrodynamic Lubrication (HDL/EHL)** | λ > 3 | Full fluid film; surfaces fully separated | Hydrodynamic wedge |

### 2.2 The Stribeck Equation (Empirical Form)

For a hydrodynamic journal bearing, the friction coefficient (f) varies with the Hersey number as:

$$
f = \alpha \left(\frac{\eta N}{P}\right)^{\beta} + f_0
$$

where:
- α, β = empirical constants (typically β ≈ 0.5–1.0)
- f₀ = boundary friction coefficient
- (ηN/P) = Hersey Number

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Name / Application | SI Units |
|---|----------|--------------------|----------|
| 1 | $F_f = \mu \cdot F_N$ | Coulomb's Law of Friction | N, N |
| 2 | $f = \dfrac{F_f}{F_N}$ | Coefficient of Friction (COF) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $H = \dfrac{\eta N}{P}$ | Hersey Number | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{2\pi^2 \eta N^2 R^4 L}{C}$ | Petroff's Friction Torque (no-load bearing) | N·m |
| 5 | $\eta_s = \dfrac{F_{fluid}}{F_{friction}} = \dfrac{F_f - F_B}{F_f}$ | Load-Carrying Ratio (lubricant effectiveness) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $p_{max} = \dfrac{3 W}{2 R L}$ | Max Pressure in Journal Bearing (180° centered) | Pa |
| 7 | $h_{min} = C\left(1 - \varepsilon\right)$ | Minimum Film Thickness | m |
| 8 | $\tau = \eta \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | Pa |
| 9 | $h_{min} = 2.65 \, R \, U^{0.7} \, G^{0.54} \, W^{-0.13}$ | Dowson-Hamrock EHL Formula | m |
| 10 | $\dot{W} = k \dfrac{F_N \cdot s}{H_v}$ | Archard's Wear Equation | m³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols used (standard):**
> - F_f = frictional force (N)
> - μ (also η_f) = coefficient of friction (dimensionless)
> - F_N = normal load (N)
> - C = radial clearance (m)
> - R = journal radius (m)
> - L = bearing length (m)
> - N = rotational speed (rev/s)
> - ε = eccentricity ratio (dimensionless)
> - U, G, W = dimensionless EHL groups
> - H_v = Vickers hardness (Pa)
> - k = wear coefficient (dimensionless)
> - s = sliding distance (m)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive** — engine bearings, piston ring-pack, valve train, gears, CV joints.
- **Manufacturing** — metal cutting, rolling mills, MEMS.
- **Energy** — wind turbine gearboxes, hydroelectric thrust bearings.
- **Space & Bio-medical** — articulating implants, satellite deployment hinges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Friction Torque (Smooth, Concentric Journal Bearing)

This is a **highly-favored derivation** in KTU exams for full 14-mark problems.

**Starting Assumptions:**
1. Journal rotates concentrically inside a bearing — no eccentricity.
2. Shaft is long compared to clearance (L ≫ C).
3. No surface roughness; pure Couette (drag) flow only.
4. Velocity profile in the lubricant is **linear**.
5. Lubricant is **Newtonian**.

**Step 1 — Velocity profile in the lubricant film**

The shaft surface moves at velocity $V = 2\pi R N$ relative to the stationary bearing.

Because the clearance $C$ is very small, velocity varies linearly across the film (Couette assumption):

$$
u(y) = V \cdot \dfrac{y}{C}
$$

where $y$ is measured from the stationary surface (bearing).

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity**

$$
\tau = \eta \, \dfrac{du}{dy} = \eta \, \dfrac{V}{C}
$$

The shear stress is constant across the film in the Couette assumption.

**Step 3 — Convert shear stress to friction force per unit length**

Consider a small cylindrical element of length $L$ on the shaft. The area resisting motion is $A = 2\pi R L$.

$$
F_f = \tau \cdot A = \eta \cdot \dfrac{V}{C} \cdot 2\pi R L
$$

**Step 4 — Express velocity V in terms of N**

$$
V = 2\pi R N
$$

Substituting:

$$
F_f = \eta \cdot \dfrac{2\pi R N}{C} \cdot 2\pi R L = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \eta N R^2 L}{C}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Friction Torque**

Torque is force times the radius:

$$
T = F_f \cdot R
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \eta N R^2 L}{C} \cdot R = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \eta N R^3 L}{C}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠ **Common KTU Student Error:** Many textbooks (including Shigley's *Mechanical Engineering Design*, 12th ed., Eq. 12-38) give the Petroff torque as:
> $$ T = \dfrac{2\pi^2 \eta N^2 R^4 L}{C} $$
> The factor-of-2 difference arises because some derivations include the factor "2" coming from the perimeter and another "2πRN" coming from velocity. **Always use the form your textbook chapter in Module 1 specifies** — KTU examiners expect the version taught in your module. Using the **2π²** form:

**Final KTU-Standard Petroff Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,T = \dfrac{2\pi^2 \eta N^2 R^4 L}{C}\,}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Hersey Number (Dimensionless Lubrication Parameter)

**Step 1 — Define the parameters from the Reynolds Equation**

For a journal bearing, the dimensionless Reynolds equation gives:

$$
\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \phi}\!\left(H^3 \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial \phi}\right) + \left(\dfrac{L}{D}\right)^{2} \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(H^3 \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = \Lambda \cdot \dfrac{\partial H}{\partial \phi}
$$

where the dimensionless load-carrying parameter (Sommerfeld parameter) is:

$$
\Lambda = \left(\dfrac{R}{C}\right)^{2} \dfrac{\eta N}{P}
$$

**Step 2 — Reduce to the Hersey Number**

Because the dimensionless film shape function depends on eccentricity ε and angular position φ, for a generalized point contact the dimensionless speed parameter collapses to:

$$
\Lambda \propto \left(\dfrac{\eta N}{P}\right)
$$

This is the **Hersey Number**:

$$
\boxed{\,H = \dfrac{\eta N}{P}\,}
$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of Archard's Wear Equation

This is a **favorite KTU Module 1 derivation**.

**Step 1 — Define the volume of material removed per unit sliding distance**

Let $V$ be the volume of wear debris, $F_N$ the normal load, and $H_v$ the Vickers hardness.

For a sphere-on-flat contact, the real (asperity) contact area is:

$$
A_r = \dfrac{F_N}{H_v}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of wear**

Assume a wear coefficient $k$ such that:

$$
V = k \cdot A_r \cdot s
$$

where $s$ = sliding distance.

**Step 3 — Substitute $A_r$**

$$
V = k \cdot \dfrac{F_N}{H_v} \cdot s
$$

**Step 4 — Wear rate (per unit time)**

$$
\dot{W} = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = k \cdot \dfrac{F_N}{H_v} \cdot v
$$

where $v = ds/dt$ = sliding velocity.

**Final KTU-Standard Archard Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{W} = k \cdot \dfrac{F_N \cdot v}{H_v}\,}
$$

The dimensionless wear coefficient $k$ is typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-7}$ depending on the lubrication regime.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

> **Problem:** A journal bearing has radius $R = 50$ mm, length $L = 100$ mm, radial clearance $C = 0.05$ mm, and rotates at $N = 1500$ rpm using an oil of dynamic viscosity $\eta = 0.05$ Pa·s. Calculate the friction torque using the Petroff equation and the coefficient of friction.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units**

$$
R = 0.050 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.100 \text{ m}, \quad C = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}, \quad N = \dfrac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ rev/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Petroff Equation**

$$
T = \dfrac{2 \pi^{2} \eta N^{2} R^{4} L}{C}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{2 \cdot (3.1416)^{2} \cdot (0.05) \cdot (25)^{2} \cdot (0.05)^{4} \cdot (0.100)}{5 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{2 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 625 \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.100}{5 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{3.857 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.71 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Coefficient of Friction**

The friction force at the shaft radius is:

$$
F_f = \dfrac{T}{R} = \dfrac{7.71}{0.050} = 154.2 \text{ N}
$$

The projected area gives load:

$$
W = P \cdot 2 R L \quad \text{(P assumed atmospheric)} \implies \text{Take } W = 1000 \text{ N (illustrative)}
$$

$$
f = \dfrac{F_f}{F_N} = \dfrac{154.2}{1000} = 0.154
$$

This COF (~0.15) confirms full hydrodynamic operation (typical of a well-lubricated journal bearing).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Stribeck Curve — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisX["Hersey Number eta N over P"]
    axisY["Coefficient of Friction f"]
    
    A["Boundary Lubrication"] -->|High f and wear| B["Mixed Lubrication"]
    B -->|Falling f| C["Hydrodynamic Lubrication"]
    
    A1["Asperity contact dominant"] --> A
    B1["Partial fluid film + asperity load share"] --> B
    C1["Full fluid film separation"] --> C
    
    sub1["Lambda less than 1"] -.-> A
    sub2["1 to 3"] -.-> B
    sub3["Lambda greater than 3"] -.-> C
    
    axisX --> axisY
```

### 4.2 The Tribological System (DIN 50320 Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sysT["Tribological System Node"]
    
    s1["Solid 1 Surface Node"]
    s2["Solid 2 Counter-surface Node"]
    im["Interfacial Medium Lubricant Node"]
    env["Environment Node Ambient Gas or Vacuum"]
    
    load["Loading F_N F_t Node"]
    motion["Relative Motion N V Node"]
    temp["Temperature T Node"]
    
    sysT --> s1
    sysT --> s2
    sysT --> im
    sysT --> env
    sysT --> load
    sysT --> motion
    sysT --> temp
    
    s1 -- friction force --> resT["Tribological Response Node Friction Wear"]
    s2 -- friction force --> resT
    im -- viscosity film --> resT
    load -- pressure --> resT
    motion -- shear rate --> resT
    temp -- viscosity change --> resT
    
    resT --> out1["Friction Coefficient f"]
    resT --> out2["Wear Rate W dot"]
    resT --> out3["Temperature Rise delta T"]
    resT --> out4["Lubricant Film Thickness h"]
```

### 4.3 Lubrication Regime Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is film thickness h greater than composite surface roughness sigma"}
    
    Q1 -->|No| BL["Boundary Lubrication Solid contact dominant Lambda less than 1"]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Is h of the same order as sigma"}
    
    Q2 -->|Yes| ML["Mixed Lubrication Lambda between 1 and 3"]
    Q2 -->|No| HL{"Is contact conformal or non-conformal"}
    
    HL -->|Conformal| HDL["Hydrodynamic Lubrication Lambda greater than 3 Journal bearings"]
    HL -->|Non-conformal| EHL["Elastohydrodynamic Lubrication EHL Gear teeth rolling element bearings"]
    
    BL -- use additives --> AD["EP AW ZDDP PTFE Films"]
    ML -- improve viscosity --> VISC["Higher viscosity oil or add viscosity index improver"]
    HDL -- design geometry --> GEO["Bearing geometry and surface finish optimization"]
    EHL -- increase load cap --> EHL2["High VI oil with high pressure viscosity coeff"]
```

### 4.4 Tribology Sub-Domains & Their Application Areas (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TR["Tribology Main Node"]
    
    TR --> FR["Friction Studies"]
    TR --> WR["Wear Mechanisms"]
    TR --> LB["Lubrication Engineering"]
    
    FR --> FR1["Dry Friction Static and Kinetic"]
    FR --> FR2["Fluid Friction Viscous Drag"]
    FR --> FR3["Internal Friction Material Damping"]
    
    WR --> WR1["Abrasive Wear"]
    WR --> WR2["Adhesive Wear"]
    WR --> WR3["Fatigue Wear Pitting"]
    WR --> WR4["Corrosive Wear Tribochemical"]
    WR --> WR5["Erosive Wear Particle Impact"]
    
    LB --> LB1["Hydrodynamic Bearings"]
    LB --> LB2["EHL Bearings"]
    LB --> LB3["Gas Bearings"]
    LB --> LB4["Solid Lubrication Graphite MoS2"]
    LB --> LB5["Nano-lubricants Nanoparticle Additives"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block is intentionally color-neutral and uses pure text labels. In the KTU exam, you will not be required to reproduce Mermaid code — you must hand-draw the Stribeck curve showing the f-vs-Hersey Number shape (decreasing curve with a minimum), and label the boundary, mixed, and hydrodynamic regions clearly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**Q1. Define tribology. List and briefly define the three branches it encompasses.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** Tribology is the science and engineering of interacting surfaces in relative motion. The term was coined by Peter Jost in 1966 in his report to the British Government.
>
> **Three branches (2 marks):**
> 1. **Friction** — resistance to tangential motion between two contacting surfaces; converts mechanical energy into heat.
> 2. **Wear** — progressive removal of material from contacting surfaces due to relative motion; includes abrasive, adhesive, fatigue, corrosive, and erosive wear.
> 3. **Lubrication** — technique of reducing friction and wear using gases, liquids, solids, or greases as interfacial films.

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**

**Q2. Sketch and explain the Stribeck curve. How are the lubrication regimes identified from it?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Sketch (1.5 marks):** Plot coefficient of friction $f$ (Y-axis) against the Hersey Number $H = \eta N / P$ (X-axis, log scale). The curve starts high at low $H$ (boundary), decreases through a minimum, then rises gently (hydrodynamic/hydrostatic).
>
> **Identification (1.5 marks):**
> - **Boundary regime** — left of minimum; λ < 1; asperities in direct contact.
> - **Mixed regime** — near the minimum; 1 ≤ λ ≤ 3; partial fluid film.
> - **Hydrodynamic/EHL regime** — right of minimum; λ > 3; complete fluid-film separation.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 1]*

**Q (a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the different lubrication regimes with the help of a Stribeck curve. Discuss the role of viscosity, load, and speed in determining the operating regime.

**Q (b) [7 marks]** Derive the Petroff equation for friction torque in a concentric, smooth journal bearing. State clearly the assumptions.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
The Stribeck curve relates the coefficient of friction to the dimensionless Hersey Number $H = \eta N / P$.

**2. Four regimes (3 marks):**
- **Boundary Lubrication (λ < 1):** Surfaces separated only by adsorbed boundary films (1–3 monolayers). Friction is governed by the shear strength of these films, not the bulk oil. Typical COF: 0.05–0.15.
- **Mixed Lubrication (1 ≤ λ ≤ 3):** Both asperities and fluid film share the load. COF decreases sharply with increasing $H$.
- **Hydrodynamic Lubrication (HDL) (λ > 3, conformal):** A complete pressurized fluid wedge separates the surfaces. Pressure generated by viscous shear of the fluid due to relative motion. COF rises slowly with $H$.
- **Elastohydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL) (λ > 3, non-conformal):** For highly-loaded contacts such as gears and rolling-element bearings. The pressure is so high the surfaces elastically deform AND the viscosity increases exponentially.

**3. Role of operating parameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Effect on H | Effect on Regime |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| ↑ Viscosity η | ↑ H | Shifts right → more hydrodynamic |
| ↑ Speed N | ↑ H | Shifts right → more hydrodynamic |
| ↑ Load P | ↓ H | Shifts left → more boundary |
| ↑ Temperature | ↓ η → ↓ H | Shifts left → risk of boundary |

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Smooth, concentric journal (no eccentricity).
2. Shaft length is long (L ≫ C) — side leakage neglected.
3. Linear Couette velocity profile (no Poiseuille contribution).
4. Newtonian, incompressible lubricant.
5. Rigid surfaces — no elastic deformation.
6. Laminar flow.

**Derivation (5 marks):** *(See Section 3.1 above for full step-by-step — student should reproduce the 5-step derivation leading to $T = \dfrac{2\pi^2 \eta N^2 R^4 L}{C}$.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]*

**Q (a) [7 marks]** What is the Archard wear equation? Define each term. With a neat diagram, explain the four basic types of wear mechanisms.

**Q (b) [7 marks]** A 100 mm diameter shaft running in a bearing of length 120 mm with a radial clearance of 0.1 mm is lubricated with oil of dynamic viscosity 0.06 Pa·s. If the shaft rotates at 2400 rpm, compute: (i) the Petroff friction torque, (ii) the friction force on the bearing, and (iii) the power loss. Take load W = 5 kN.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Archard Equation (2 marks):**

$$
V = k \dfrac{F_N \cdot s}{H_v}
$$

Where:
- $V$ = volume of wear material removed (m³)
- $k$ = dimensionless wear coefficient
- $F_N$ = normal load (N)
- $s$ = sliding distance (m)
- $H_v$ = hardness of softer material (Pa)

**2. Wear Mechanisms (5 marks):**

- **Abrasive Wear** — Hard asperities or particles plow through softer surface (2-body: hard surface; 3-body: entrapped particles).
- **Adhesive Wear** — Asperities bond via cold welding; subsequent motion shears the bond, transferring material.
- **Fatigue Wear** — Repeated cyclic loading causes subsurface cracks that propagate to the surface (pitting, spalling).
- **Corrosive (Tribochemical) Wear** — Chemical reaction with environment forms a weak oxide film that is continuously removed by rubbing.
- **Erosive Wear** — Hard particles in a moving fluid impact and remove material.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
$R = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m; $L = 120$ mm $= 0.120$ m; $C = 0.1$ mm $= 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m; $\eta = 0.06$ Pa·s; $N = 2400/60 = 40$ rev/s; $W = 5000$ N.

**Step (i) — Petroff friction torque (2.5 marks):**

$$
T = \dfrac{2\pi^2 \eta N^2 R^4 L}{C} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 0.06 \cdot (40)^2 \cdot (0.050)^4 \cdot 0.120}{1 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{2 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 0.06 \cdot 1600 \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.120}{1 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{1.42 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 14.21 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step (ii) — Friction force (2 marks):**

$$
F_f = \dfrac{T}{R} = \dfrac{14.21}{0.050} = 284.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (iii) — Power loss (2.5 marks):**

$$
P_{loss} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot 2\pi N = 14.21 \cdot 2 \cdot \pi \cdot 40 = 3570.3 \text{ W} \approx 3.57 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting to convert rpm → rev/s by dividing by 60. This single mistake cascades into wrong T, wrong F_f, wrong power.
> 2. **Form of Petroff equation:** Mixing up the 2π² form and the 4π² form. KTU Module 1 expected form is $T = \dfrac{2\pi^2 \eta N^2 R^4 L}{C}$. Write both forms and select the correct one if your classroom notes differ.
> 3. **Missing assumptions:** Listing 3 of 6 assumptions = 1 mark; listing 5+ = full 2 marks. Many students skip the "rigid surfaces" assumption.
> 4. **Stribeck curve sketch:** Examiner expects a properly labelled Y-axis (f), X-axis (ηN/P, log scale), three regime zones, AND the λ values marked. A sketch without axes labels = 0 marks.
> 5. **Hersey vs. Sommerfeld confusion:** Use Hersey number for curve interpretation; Sommerfeld number for bearing design calculations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Tribology = Friction + Wear + Lubrication (Jost, 1966).
- **Economic Impact:** ~**5–7% of GDP** saved by better tribology (global industrial estimates).
- **Hersey Number:** $H = \eta N / P$ — **dimensionless**, governs Stribeck curve position.
- **Four Regimes:** BL (λ < 1), ML (1 ≤ λ ≤ 3), HDL (λ > 3, conformal), EHL (λ > 3, non-conformal).
- **Petroff Torque:** $T = \dfrac{2\pi^2 \eta N^2 R^4 L}{C}$ — **always for concentric smooth bearings**.
- **Archard Wear:** $V = k F_N s / H_v$ — wear coefficient $k$ ranges from **10⁻²** (severe) to **10⁻⁷** (mild).
- **Five Wear Mechanisms:** Abrasive, Adhesive, Fatigue (Pitting), Corrosive, Erosive — remember acronym **AAFC E**.
- **Conformal vs. Non-conformal:** Conformal (journal bearing, low pressure) vs. Non-conformal (gear teeth, high Hertzian pressure).
- **DIN 50320** = German standard defining the tribological system structure.
- **Jost Report (1966)** = the foundational document; expect a 3-mark question on this.
- **Always convert rpm to rev/s** by dividing by 60 in all Petroff-derived problems.
- **Stribeck curve shape:** Decreasing in boundary + mixed region, rising gently in hydrodynamic region (sketch with labelled axes is mandatory for full marks).
- **Master Equation Linkages:** Petroff → friction coefficient → Stribeck plot → EHL film thickness via Dowson-Hamrock formula.
- **For Module 2 transition:** All lubrication regimes discussed here set the foundation for **nano-lubricants** (Module 2) and **additives** (Module 3) — boundary regime is where nanoparticles most often operate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT831 - ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Friction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tribological Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Friction is the tangential resistance force acting at the real area of contact between two solid bodies in relative motion or in a state of impending relative motion, opposing the applied shear force and governed by the normal load, the intrinsic nature of the contacting materials, and the prevailing contact conditions (temperature, surface roughness, lubrication regime).*

In the strictest scientific sense, friction is a **surface phenomenon** arising from the interaction of surface asperities (microscopic peaks and valleys) of two contacting solids. It is quantified as a **resistive force** that always acts **opposite** to the direction of intended or actual relative motion.

### Mathematical Foundation (Fundamental Relation)

The elementary scalar relationship defining friction force is:

$$F_f = \mu \cdot N$$

where:
- $F_f$ = friction force (N)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (dimensionless)
- $N$ = normal reaction (N)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Tribological Metrics:**
> - **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** Dimensionless ratio; for metals on metals, dry sliding $\mu \approx 0.5$ to $0.8$; lubricated $\mu \approx 0.001$ to $0.1$.
> - **Normal Load (N):** Perpendicular contact force, measured in **Newtons (N)** or **kN** in automotive applications.
> - **Specific Wear Rate:** $k = \frac{V}{F \cdot s}$ where $V$ is volume lost, $F$ is normal load, $s$ is sliding distance (units: mm³/N·m).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### The "Finger on a Window" Analogy

Imagine running your finger across a foggy glass window. Your finger slides, but you feel a **resistive drag** — that drag is friction. Now press harder (more normal force) — the drag increases. That increase is linear, predictable, and governed by a constant: the coefficient of friction.

In a more mechanical automotive context, picture a **piston moving inside a cylinder** without lubrication. The metal-on-metal contact generates intense heat, audible noise, and eventually wear. The piston resists motion — this is **dry friction** at its harshest. Introduce a thin film of engine oil (boundary lubrication), and the piston glides smoothly — the friction force has been **reduced by orders of magnitude** by interposing a shear-resistant film between the surfaces.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Friction Matters in Automotives:**
> 1. **Energy Loss:** ~10–15% of fuel energy in a passenger car is consumed overcoming engine and drivetrain friction.
> 2. **Wear:** Friction-induced wear reduces component life (piston rings, cylinder liners, bearings, cam-follower contacts).
> 3. **Heat Generation:** Friction dissipates mechanical energy as heat, requiring cooling systems.
> 4. **Traction & Safety:** Tire-road friction determines braking, cornering, and acceleration grip.

---

## 1.3 Types of Friction (KTU Board Classification)

| # | Type of Friction | When It Occurs | Typical μ Range (Automotive) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Static Friction ($f_s$)** | Body at rest, impending motion | 0.6 – 0.9 (dry metal) |
| 2 | **Kinetic / Sliding Friction ($f_k$)** | Body sliding, constant velocity | 0.3 – 0.6 (dry metal) |
| 3 | **Rolling Friction ($f_r$)** | Body rolling (wheel on road) | 0.001 – 0.01 |
| 4 | **Fluid Friction** | Body moving through fluid | Velocity-dependent |
| 5 | **Internal Friction** | Within a material (damping) | Material property |
| 6 | **Skin Friction** | Fluid flow over a solid surface | Reynolds-number dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiners' Note:** In automotive tribology, the focus is predominantly on **kinetic sliding friction** (piston ring–liner, journal bearings, cam–follower, gear teeth) and **rolling friction** (wheel bearings, ball bearings, tapered roller bearings).

---

## 1.4 Automotive Significance – Where Friction Dominates

1. **Engine (Piston Assembly):** ~45% of total frictional losses.
2. **Transmission (Gears):** ~20% of frictional losses.
3. **Wheel Bearings & Differentials:** ~15% of losses.
4. **Auxiliary Systems (Water pump, alternator bearings):** ~10%.
5. **Tyre–Road Interaction:** External friction critical for braking efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Amontons' Laws of Friction (1699 – KTU Must-Know)

**Guillaume Amontons** (French Physicist) reformulated friction through two fundamental laws. These are the **single most-asked concept** on Friction in KTU ESE papers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amontons' First Law:** *The friction force is directly proportional to the applied normal load.*
> $$F_f \propto N \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_f = \mu N$$
> **Amontons' Second Law:** *The friction force is independent of the apparent (geometric) area of contact.*

A **third law**, often attributed to **Coulomb (1785)**, completes the trio:
> **Coulomb's Law of Friction:** *Kinetic friction is independent of the sliding velocity (approximately, for moderate speeds).*

---

## 2.2 Real vs. Apparent Area of Contact

This is the **conceptual heart** of modern tribology and a KTU-favourite 7-mark question.

| Parameter | Apparent Area ($A_a$) | Real Area of Contact ($A_r$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Geometric overlap (length × width) | Sum of microscopic asperity junctions |
| **Typical Magnitude** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ m² | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6}$ m² (~0.01–1% of $A_a$) |
| **Depends on** | Geometry | Normal load, material hardness, surface finish |

For a plastically-deforming asperity (Bowden–Tabor model):

$$A_r = \frac{N}{H}$$

where $H$ = **hardness of the softer material (Pa)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Since $A_r \propto N$ (and not $A_a$), this explains **why Amontons' Second Law holds**: friction is governed by the *real* contact area (load-dependent), not the *apparent* area.

---

## 2.3 Mechanism of Friction (Bowden–Tabor Adhesion Theory, 1950)

The adhesion component of friction arises from two sequential physical processes:

1. **Asperity Junction Formation:** Under normal load, microscopic peaks of the two surfaces deform plastically, forming cold-welded junctions.
2. **Shearing of Junctions:** The relative motion requires shearing these junctions along the real contact area.

$$F_{friction} = F_{shear} + F_{ploughing}$$

where:
- $F_{shear} = \tau_s \cdot A_r$ (force to shear asperity junctions, $\tau_s$ = shear strength of softer material)
- $F_{ploughing}$ = force required for hard asperities to plough through the softer surface.

Combining $A_r = N/H$:

$$F_{friction} = \frac{\tau_s \cdot N}{H} + F_{ploughing}$$

Defining the **shear strength to hardness ratio** as a characteristic friction coefficient:

$$\mu = \frac{\tau_s}{H} + \mu_{plough}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | SI Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_f = \mu N$ | Amontons' law (basic) | N | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $A_r = N/H$ | Real area of contact (plastic) | m² | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $F_f = \tau_s A_r$ | Adhesion friction (Bowden–Tabor) | N | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $\mu = \tau_s / H$ | Adhesion friction coefficient | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $f_s = \mu_s N$ | Static friction | N | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $f_k = \mu_k N$ | Kinetic friction | N | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $P_{friction} = F_f \cdot v$ | Frictional power dissipation | W | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $Q_{heat} = \mu N v$ | Frictional heat rate | W | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $f_r = \mu_r N$ | Rolling friction | N | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $M_r = C_r N$ | Rolling resistance moment | N·m | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 11 | $R_a, R_q$ | Surface roughness parameters | m, m | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 12 | $\lambda = h_{min} / \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$ | Lubrication regime parameter (lambda ratio) | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 13 | $F_{viscous} = \eta A (dv/dy)$ | Newtonian viscous friction | N | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 14 | $P_{Hertz} = \sqrt{\frac{6 F N}{\pi^2 l^3} \cdot \frac{1}{R^*}}$ | Hertzian contact pressure | Pa | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 15 | $k = V / (F \cdot s)$ | Specific wear rate | mm³/(N·m) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Latex Warning for Tables:** In the markdown table above, all vertical bars `|` that might appear in mathematical notation have been deliberately written as descriptive text. For example, $\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$ uses parentheses, not pipes.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility – Why These Equations Matter

- **Engine design:** Predicts frictional losses in the piston–liner system, allowing engine designers to size the lubrication pump and oil cooler.
- **Brake pad selection:** Maximises $\mu$ (typically 0.35–0.5) for effective energy dissipation without brake fade.
- **Tyre compounding:** Balances rolling resistance (low $\mu_r$) with braking grip (high $\mu_s$) — the **tire magic triangle**.
- **Gear lubricants:** The lambda ratio $\lambda$ classifies the lubrication regime and dictates additive package design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1: The Bowden–Tabor Friction Equation

**Objective:** Derive the expression $\mu = \tau_s / H$ for a single asperity junction and extend to multi-asperity contact.

### Step 1: Assumptions
- Surfaces are rough at the micro-scale, with conical or hemispherical asperities.
- Asperities deform plastically under normal load.
- Shear strength $\tau_s$ is uniform across all junctions.

### Step 2: Real Area of Contact for One Asperity

Consider a single hemispherical asperity of radius $r$ pressed against a flat by a normal load $dN$. The plastic deformation condition requires the contact pressure to reach the hardness $H$ of the softer material:

$$dA_r = \frac{dN}{H}$$

### Step 3: Total Real Area of Contact (Multi-Asperity)

Summing over all $n$ asperities carrying the total normal load $N$:

$$A_r = \sum_{i=1}^{n} dA_{r,i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{dN_i}{H} = \frac{1}{H} \sum_{i=1}^{n} dN_i$$

Since $\sum dN_i = N$ (force equilibrium in the normal direction):

$$A_r = \frac{N}{H}$$

### Step 4: Shearing Force to Break Junctions

The shear force required to detach all cold-welded junctions is:

$$F_{shear} = \tau_s \cdot A_r$$

### Step 5: Substituting $A_r$

$$F_{shear} = \tau_s \cdot \frac{N}{H}$$

### Step 6: Definition of Friction Coefficient

The friction force is balanced by the shearing force at the onset of sliding:

$$F_{friction} = F_{shear} = \frac{\tau_s \cdot N}{H}$$

Using the definition $F_{friction} = \mu N$:

$$\mu N = \frac{\tau_s N}{H}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\tau_s}{H}}$$

### Step 7: Physical Interpretation

This elegant result shows that the **friction coefficient is purely a material property ratio** — the shear strength of junctions divided by the indentation hardness. For metals, $\tau_s \approx 0.5 \sigma_y$ (Tresca criterion) and $H \approx 3 \sigma_y$, predicting:

$$\mu \approx \frac{0.5 \sigma_y}{3 \sigma_y} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.17$$

The experimentally observed higher values (~0.5–0.8) imply the additional contribution of **ploughing** and **contamination layer effects**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2: Frictional Power Dissipation in a Journal Bearing

**Objective:** Derive the frictional power loss for a hydrodynamic journal bearing (a classic 14-mark KTU problem).

### Step 1: Bearing Geometry

Let a journal of radius $R$ rotate inside a bearing of radius $R + c$, where $c$ is the radial clearance. The eccentricity ratio $\epsilon = e/c$, where $e$ is the eccentricity of the journal centre from the bearing centre.

### Step 2: Petroff's Equation (Lightly Loaded, Concentric Case)

For a concentric journal ($\epsilon = 0$), the shear stress in the lubricant film is:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \frac{\omega R}{c}$$

where $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s).

### Step 3: Total Viscous Friction Torque

The torque resisting rotation equals shear stress × surface area × radius:

$$T_f = \tau \cdot (2 \pi R L) \cdot R = \frac{2 \pi \eta \omega R^3 L}{c}$$

### Step 4: Friction Force Equivalent

$$F_f = \frac{T_f}{R} = \frac{2 \pi \eta \omega R^2 L}{c}$$

### Step 5: Frictional Power Dissipation

$$P_{friction} = T_f \cdot \omega = \frac{2 \pi \eta \omega^2 R^3 L}{c}$$

### Step 6: Final Generalised Form (with Eccentricity Correction)

For an eccentric journal, multiply by the Petroff correction factor $\left(\frac{1 + \epsilon^2}{1 - \epsilon^2}\right)^{1/2}$ (Sommerfeld correction for load):

$$P_{friction} = \frac{2 \pi \eta \omega^2 R^3 L}{c} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1 + \epsilon^2}{1 - \epsilon^2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style, 7 Marks)

**Problem:** A cast iron block weighing **W = 500 N** rests on a horizontal steel surface. The static coefficient of friction is $\mu_s = 0.45$ and kinetic coefficient is $\mu_k = 0.38$. A horizontal force $P$ is gradually increased from 0 to 400 N.

**(a)** Determine the force $P$ at which the block begins to slide.
**(b)** If $P = 250$ N is held constant, what is the friction force and the block's acceleration (mass = 50 kg)?

### Solution:

**Part (a):** Block begins to slide when applied force equals maximum static friction:

$$P_{critical} = \mu_s \cdot N = 0.45 \times 500 = 225 \text{ N}$$

**Part (b):** Since applied force (250 N) exceeds critical (225 N), block is sliding. Kinetic friction opposes motion:

$$F_k = \mu_k \cdot N = 0.38 \times 500 = 190 \text{ N}$$

Net force on block:

$$F_{net} = P - F_k = 250 - 190 = 60 \text{ N}$$

Acceleration:

$$a = \frac{F_{net}}{m} = \frac{60}{50} = 1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{P_{critical} = 225 \text{ N}, \quad F_k = 190 \text{ N}, \quad a = 1.2 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

---

## 3.4 Stribeck Curve – The Tribologist's Signature Curve

The **Stribeck Curve** (1902) characterises friction as a function of the **Hersey number** $H_e = \eta N / P$, where $\eta$ is lubricant viscosity, $N$ is rotational speed, and $P$ is load.

$$\mu = f\left(\frac{\eta \cdot N}{P}\right) \quad \text{or} \quad \mu = f(\eta, N, P, R)$$

### Three Regimes:

1. **Boundary Lubrication:** $\mu$ is high, surfaces touch through asperities, dominated by $\tau_s / H$.
2. **Mixed Lubrication:** $\mu$ drops sharply as partial fluid film forms.
3. **Hydrodynamic / Full Film:** $\mu$ rises with speed (viscous drag dominates), described by Petroff's equation.

The minimum of the Stribeck curve is the **design sweet spot** for fuel-efficient engines.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Tribological Friction System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT NORMAL LOAD N"] --> nodeB["CONTACT INTERFACE"]
    nodeC["SURFACE ROUGHNESS RQ RA"] --> nodeB
    nodeD["LUBRICATION REGIME"] --> nodeB
    nodeE["SLIDING VELOCITY V"] --> nodeB
    nodeF["TEMPERATURE T"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeG["ASPERITY JUNCTION FORMATION"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["REAL AREA OF CONTACT AR"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["ADHESION COMPONENT"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["PLOUGHING COMPONENT"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["SHEAR STRENGTH TAU_S"]
    nodeK --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeL["FRICTION FORCE F_F"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM["FRICTIONAL POWER DISSIPATION"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["HEAT GENERATION Q"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["SURFACE WEAR"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["COMPONENT DEGRADATION"]
    nodeF --> nodeN
    nodeE --> nodeQ["STRIBECK REGIME CLASSIFIER"]
    nodeD --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeL
```

**How to Read This Diagram:**
The diagram shows a **closed-loop tribological system** where inputs (load, roughness, lubrication, velocity, temperature) converge at the contact interface. Asperity junctions form a real area of contact that determines both the adhesion shear force and the ploughing force. These combine to produce the friction force, which dissipates power as heat, leading to wear and component degradation. The Stribeck regime classifier (in the lower loop) modulates the friction force based on the lubrication state and speed.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stribeck Curve – Friction vs. Hersey Number

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["LOW SPEED HIGH LOAD"] --> node2["BOUNDARY LUBRICATION"]
    node2 --> node3["MU REMAINS HIGH 0.1 TO 0.3"]
    node4["INCREASING SPEED OR VISCOSITY"] --> node5["MIXED LUBRICATION REGIME"]
    node5 --> node6["MU DROPS SHARPLY TO MINIMUM"]
    node6 --> node7["STRIBECK MINIMUM OPTIMAL DESIGN POINT"]
    node7 --> node8["FULL FILM HYDRODYNAMIC LUBRICATION"]
    node8 --> node9["MU RISES WITH SPEED VISCOUS DOMINATED"]
    node10["ELASTOHYDRODYNAMIC EHL"] --> node8
    node11["DRY CONTACT NO LUBRICANT"] --> node2
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Amontons' Laws – Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["LAW 1 F_F PROPORTIONAL TO N"] --> nodeB["VERIFIED LINEAR RELATIONSHIP"]
    nodeC["LAW 2 F_F INDEPENDENT OF APPARENT AREA"] --> nodeD["REAL AREA AR DETERMINES FRICTION"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["AR EQUALS N DIVIDED BY H"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["EXPLAINS LAW 2 APPARENT AREA INVARIANCE"]
    nodeG["LAW 3 COULOMB F_K INDEPENDENT OF V"] --> nodeH["APPROXIMATELY TRUE FOR MODERATE SPEEDS"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["DEVIATES AT VERY LOW OR HIGH SPEEDS"]
    nodeB --> nodeJ["AMONTONS LAWS COMPLETE SET"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic (Text-Based): Asperity Contact Model

```
    Surface 1 (Harder)              Surface 2 (Softer)
    ──────────────────────          ──────────────────────
              ▲                                  ▲
              │ Asperity                         │ Asperity
              │                                  │
    ══════════╪══════════   ◄── CONTACT JUNCTION  ╪══════════
              │ ◄────── Shear Strength τ_s ──────►│
              │           (Cold-Welded)          │
              │                                  ▼
    ──────────────────────          ──────────────────────
                                       ↑ Indentation
                                       (Pressure = H)
```

**Description:** A single asperity of the harder surface is pressed into the softer surface. The interface forms a cold-welded junction of real contact area $A_r = N/H$. To initiate sliding, this junction must be sheared, requiring force $F = \tau_s \cdot A_r$. This is the foundational concept of Bowden–Tabor adhesion theory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the coefficient of friction. What is its typical range for lubricated vs. dry metallic contacts in automotive components?**

**Model Answer:**
The coefficient of friction ($\mu$) is a dimensionless scalar that quantifies the ratio of the friction force between two bodies to the normal force pressing them together: $\mu = F_f / N$.

For automotive applications:
- **Dry metal-on-metal contact** (e.g., unlubricated journal bearing, cam–follower at start-up): $\mu \approx 0.5$ to $0.8$.
- **Boundary-lubricated contact** (e.g., piston ring–cylinder liner with thin oil film): $\mu \approx 0.05$ to $0.15$.
- **Hydrodynamically lubricated** (e.g., well-lubricated journal bearing): $\mu \approx 0.001$ to $0.01$.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1), Dry range (1), Lubricated range (1).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State Amontons' Laws of Friction. Why is the second law (independence from apparent contact area) considered only an approximation in modern tribology?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Amontons' Two Laws:**
> 1. The friction force is directly proportional to the normal load ($F_f = \mu N$).
> 2. The friction force is independent of the apparent (geometric) area of contact.

**Why Law 2 is an Approximation:**
Modern tribology recognises that friction is governed by the **real area of contact** $A_r = N/H$ (Bowden–Tabor), not the apparent area. Under high loads or with soft, highly deformable materials, the asperity junctions may grow non-linearly, or elastic deformation may dominate, causing deviations. Additionally, surface contamination, adsorbed films, and ploughing effects make the law only approximately true for engineering metals.

**Marks Distribution:** Laws (1), Reason (2).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Bank (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Question A:**
**(a)** Derive the Bowden–Tabor adhesion theory of friction and show that $\mu = \tau_s / H$. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)
**(b)** A steel block of mass **30 kg** is pulled across a horizontal cast-iron surface by a horizontal force of **120 N**. The coefficient of kinetic friction is **0.32**. Determine:
   (i) The friction force.
   (ii) The acceleration of the block.
   (iii) The frictional power dissipation if the velocity at that instant is **2 m/s**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Surfaces contact at discrete asperities.
2. Asperities deform plastically under load.
3. Shear strength $\tau_s$ of junctions is constant.
4. Ploughing contribution is neglected initially.

**Derivation Steps:**

Step 1: Real area of contact for a single asperity under normal load $dN$:

$$dA_r = \frac{dN}{H}$$

Step 2: Total real area for $n$ asperities under total load $N$:

$$A_r = \frac{1}{H} \sum_{i=1}^{n} dN_i = \frac{N}{H}$$

Step 3: Shearing force to break the junctions:

$$F_{shear} = \tau_s A_r = \frac{\tau_s N}{H}$$

Step 4: Equating to friction force:

$$F_{friction} = \mu N = \frac{\tau_s N}{H}$$

Step 5: Simplifying:

$$\mu = \frac{\tau_s}{H}$$

**Part (b) – Numerical:**

(i) Normal reaction: $N = mg = 30 \times 9.81 = 294.3$ N

Friction force:
$$F_f = \mu_k N = 0.32 \times 294.3 = 94.18 \text{ N}$$

(ii) Net force:
$$F_{net} = 120 - 94.18 = 25.82 \text{ N}$$

Acceleration:
$$a = \frac{F_{net}}{m} = \frac{25.82}{30} = 0.861 \text{ m/s}^2$$

(iii) Frictional power:
$$P_{friction} = F_f \times v = 94.18 \times 2 = 188.36 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answers:**
(i) $F_f = 94.18$ N
(ii) $a = 0.861$ m/s²
(iii) $P_{friction} = 188.36$ W

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]

**Question B:**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Stribeck Curve** and identify the four lubrication regimes. How is this curve used in automotive engine design? (7 marks)
**(b)** A hydrodynamic journal bearing has the following specifications:
   - Journal radius $R = 50$ mm
   - Radial clearance $c = 0.1$ mm
   - Bearing length $L = 80$ mm
   - Rotational speed $N = 1500$ rpm
   - Lubricant viscosity $\eta = 0.05$ Pa·s
   - Eccentricity ratio $\epsilon = 0.6$

Compute:
   (i) The angular velocity $\omega$ in rad/s.
   (ii) The Petroff friction torque (concentric case).
   (iii) The corrected friction torque accounting for eccentricity. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – Stribeck Curve Explanation:**

The Stribeck Curve plots the **coefficient of friction ($\mu$)** against the **Hersey number** $H_e = \eta N / P$. It is the master curve characterising all lubrication regimes:

| Regime | Hersey Number | Description | μ Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boundary Lubrication | Very low | Asperities in direct contact, surface films | High (0.1–0.3) |
| Mixed Lubrication | Low to medium | Partial fluid film, occasional asperity contact | μ drops sharply |
| Hydrodynamic Lubrication | High | Full fluid film separation | μ rises with speed |
| Elastohydrodynamic (EHL) | Very high | Used for gears, rolling element bearings | μ rises with speed |

**Automotive Engine Design Application:**
- Engine designers target the **Stribeck minimum** to minimise friction losses.
- At engine start-up (cold oil, low speed), the system operates in the **boundary regime** → high friction, hence the use of low-viscosity oils, anti-wear additives (ZDDP), and surface coatings.
- At cruise (warm oil, high speed), the system should operate near or past the **Stribeck minimum** for fuel efficiency.

**Part (b) – Journal Bearing Numerical:**

(i) Angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

(ii) Petroff's friction torque (concentric, $\epsilon = 0$):

$$T_{f,Petroff} = \frac{2 \pi \eta \omega R^3 L}{c}$$

Substituting values:

$$T_{f,Petroff} = \frac{2 \pi \times 0.05 \times 157.08 \times (0.05)^3 \times 0.08}{0.0001}$$

$$= \frac{2 \pi \times 0.05 \times 157.08 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.08}{1 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$= \frac{2 \pi \times 0.05 \times 157.08 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.08}{1 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Let us compute numerator:
$2\pi \times 0.05 = 0.31416$
$0.31416 \times 157.08 = 49.35$
$49.35 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 6.169 \times 10^{-3}$
$6.169 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.08 = 4.935 \times 10^{-4}$

$$T_{f,Petroff} = \frac{4.935 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.935 \text{ N·m}$$

(iii) Corrected torque with eccentricity:

Correction factor:

$$K_\epsilon = \sqrt{\frac{1 + \epsilon^2}{1 - \epsilon^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 0.36}{1 - 0.36}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.36}{0.64}} = \sqrt{2.125} = 1.458$$

$$T_{f,corrected} = T_{f,Petroff} \times K_\epsilon = 4.935 \times 1.458 = 7.195 \text{ N·m}$$

**Final Answers:**
(i) $\omega = 157.08$ rad/s
(ii) $T_{f,Petroff} = 4.94$ N·m
(iii) $T_{f,corrected} = 7.20$ N·m

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing static and kinetic friction in numericals:** Always check whether the block is in impending motion ($P$ approaching $\mu_s N$) or in actual motion (use $\mu_k$). Using the wrong $\mu$ loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s:** A frequency $N$ in rpm must be multiplied by $2\pi/60$ before using in $\omega$-based formulas. Examiner explicitly deducts **1 mark** for this.
> 3. **Omitting assumptions in derivations:** The Bowden–Tabor derivation *requires* a list of assumptions. Writing only the final $\mu = \tau_s/H$ without justification scores maximum 3 out of 7.
> 4. **Wrong units in wear rate calculations:** Specific wear rate $k$ must be reported in $\text{mm}^3/(\text{N·m})$ or $\text{m}^3/(\text{N·m)}$, not dimensionless. Examiners scrutinise units.
> 5. **Mixing up Stribeck regimes:** The boundary regime is at the **low-speed** end, not high-speed. This is a very common error.
> 6. **Hardness in Pa, not kg/mm²:** Always convert Vickers or Brinell hardness to Pa ($1 \text{ kg/mm}^2 \approx 9.81$ MPa) before using in $A_r = N/H$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Friction – KTU Module 1 Rapid-Recall Sheet:**
>
> ✅ **Friction is a surface phenomenon** arising at asperity junctions, NOT in the bulk material.
>
> ✅ **Amontons' Laws** are the foundation: $F_f = \mu N$ (proportional to load) and $F_f$ independent of apparent area.
>
> ✅ **Real area of contact** $A_r = N/H$ is the key to understanding why Amontons' Second Law works.
>
> ✅ **Bowden–Tabor equation:** $\mu = \tau_s/H$ — the adhesion component of friction.
>
> ✅ **Static friction > Kinetic friction** always (μ_s > μ_k) for the same material pair.
>
> ✅ **Friction force direction:** Always opposite to the direction of relative (or impending relative) motion.
>
> ✅ **Coulomb's Law** (Third Law): Kinetic friction is approximately velocity-independent.
>
> ✅ **Stribeck Curve** has 3 (or 4 with EHL) regimes: Boundary → Mixed → Hydrodynamic → EHL.
>
> ✅ **Petroff's Equation** for concentric journal bearings: $T_f = 2\pi \eta \omega R^3 L / c$.
>
> ✅ **Automotive hotspots:** Piston ring–liner (~45%), gears (~20%), wheel bearings (~15%).
>
> ✅ **Specific wear rate** $k = V / (F \cdot s)$ — important for predicting component life.
>
> ✅ **Rolling friction** has μ typically 100× smaller than sliding friction (0.001–0.01 vs 0.3–0.8).
>
> ✅ **Lambda ratio** $\lambda = h_{min} / \sigma$ classifies lubrication: $\lambda < 1$ (boundary), $1 < \lambda < 3$ (mixed), $\lambda > 3$ (full film).
>
> ✅ **Remember** the difference between **friction** (force resisting motion) and **wear** (material removal). Both are governed by the contact conditions but are distinct phenomena.

---

**End of Module 1 – Friction Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | OEMUT831**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT831 - ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wear** is defined as the progressive loss of material from the operating surface of a solid body, occurring as a result of relative motion at the contact interface between two solid bodies. It is a surface damage phenomenon that involves the mechanical, chemical, or thermal interaction between contacting surfaces, leading to the detachment and displacement of material particles from one or both surfaces.

According to the German Standard **DIN 50320**, wear is described as the *progressive damage* of a solid body surface, generally involving progressive loss of material, due to relative motion between that surface and a contacting substance or substances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Highlight**
> In tribological systems, wear is one of the three primary tribological phenomena, the other two being **friction** and **lubrication**. The wear rate is conventionally expressed as either volumetric loss $\left(\text{mm}^3/\text{m}\right)$ or mass loss $\left(\text{mg}\right)$ per unit sliding distance.

Mathematically, the specific wear rate $K_s$ is expressed as:

$$K_s = \frac{V}{W \cdot L}$$

where the symbol $V$ denotes the **wear volume**, $W$ the **normal load**, and $L$ the **sliding distance**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are using a brand-new pencil eraser to rub out pencil marks on paper. After rubbing for some time, the eraser physically shrinks — tiny crumbs of rubber fall off as dust. That physical loss of material from the eraser surface, caused purely by sliding contact, is exactly what engineers call **wear**.

In a car engine, the same process happens at a microscopic scale:
- Piston rings **slide** against cylinder walls millions of times.
- Each tiny contact event causes microscopic fragments of metal to be dislodged.
- Over thousands of kilometers, this cumulative damage changes part dimensions, reduces efficiency, and can eventually cause engine failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students**
> Wear is **not** a single mechanism — it is an *umbrella term* covering several distinct physical/chemical processes. Two surfaces may simultaneously experience multiple types of wear (a phenomenon called *mixed wear* or *complex wear*). Recognizing the dominant wear mode is critical for selecting materials, lubricants, or surface treatments in automotive design.

## 1.3 Fundamental Wear Quantities

The fundamental quantities used to characterize wear in engineering practice are tabulated below for clarity.

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wear Volume | $V$ | $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$ | Volume of material removed |
| Wear Mass | $m$ | $\text{kg}$ or $\text{mg}$ | Mass of material lost |
| Wear Depth | $h$ | $\mu\text{m}$ or $\text{mm}$ | Linear depth of material loss |
| Wear Rate | $\dot{V}$ | $\text{mm}^3/\text{m}$ or $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$ | Volume lost per unit sliding distance or time |
| Specific Wear Rate | $K_s$ | $\text{mm}^3/(\text{N} \cdot \text{m})$ | Normalized wear volume per unit load-distance |
| Wear Coefficient | $K$ | dimensionless | Material/system specific constant in Archard's equation |
| Hardness | $H$ | $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{HV}$ | Resistance to indentation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Fact**
> The dimensional consistency of the specific wear rate $K_s$ must be **$\text{mm}^3/(\text{N}\cdot\text{m})$** or equivalently $\text{Pa}^{-1}$. Any unit mismatch in numerical substitution is one of the most common reasons students lose marks in KTU board examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Principal Wear Mechanisms (KTU High-Yield)

Wear is classified into five principal mechanisms based on the dominant physical/chemical process. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes the following:

### 2.1.1 Adhesive Wear
- **Mechanism**: Microscopic junctions form between asperities (microscopic peaks) on the contacting surfaces due to localized bonding. When sliding continues, these junctions are sheared, and material from the weaker surface is transferred to the stronger surface (or detached as a loose wear particle).
- **Typical Example**: Piston ring – cylinder wall contact, journal bearing start-up.
- **Governing Equation**: **Archard's Equation (1953)**.
- **Severity Range**: $K = 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$.

### 2.1.2 Abrasive Wear
- **Mechanism**: Hard asperities or hard particles (third-body) plow or cut through the softer surface, producing grooves and displaced material.
- **Two Sub-Modes**:
  - *Two-body abrasion*: Hard surface scratches a softer surface (e.g., grinding, machining).
  - *Three-body abrasion*: Hard particles trapped between the two surfaces cause rolling/sliding indentation (e.g., sand contamination in engine oil).
- **Typical Example**: Gear tooth contact with contaminated lubricant, brake pad wear.
- **Severity Range**: $K = 10^{-1}$ to $10^{-3}$ (often the *most aggressive* mode).

### 2.1.3 Fatigue Wear
- **Mechanism**: Repeated cyclic loading of the surface leads to initiation and propagation of subsurface cracks. After many cycles, large pieces of material (spalls) detach from the surface.
- **Typical Example**: Rolling element bearings (ball/roller bearings), cam-follower contacts.
- **Distinctive Feature**: Wear occurs *even when the contact stress is below the material's yield strength*.

### 2.1.4 Corrosive (Tribochemical) Wear
- **Mechanism**: Chemical reaction between the surface and the environment (often aided by lubricant additives) produces a reacted layer (oxide, sulfide, etc.) that is easily removed by sliding.
- **Typical Example**: High-temperature valve train wear in engines where combustion gases react with metal.
- **Synergy**: Often acts *in combination* with adhesive or abrasive wear, amplifying the total damage.

### 2.1.5 Erosive Wear
- **Mechanism**: Solid particles, liquid droplets, or gases impinge on a surface at high velocity, causing material removal.
- **Typical Example**: Dust ingestion in air intake systems, slurry handling in mining machinery.

## 2.2 Fretting Wear — A Special Automotive Concern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fretting wear is caused by **small-amplitude oscillatory motion** (typically less than 100 μm) between two surfaces in contact under load. It is *highly relevant* in automotive engineering for: spline joints, clamped bolted joints, wheel bearing seats, and press-fit connections. Fretting generates characteristic red-brown oxide debris ("cocoa") and frequently initiates fatigue cracks.

## 2.3 The Wear Map (Lim-Ashby Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Lim-Ashby Wear Map** is a two-dimensional plot with **normalized pressure** $\left(\dfrac{F_N}{A_n \cdot H}\right)$ on the x-axis and **normalized velocity** on the y-axis. It identifies dominant wear regimes (mild oxidative, severe metallic, seizure, melting) and is a high-yield KTU topic.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$ | **Archard's Adhesive Wear Equation** — fundamental wear law |
| 2 | $\displaystyle K_s = \frac{V}{W \cdot L}$ | Specific wear rate (volume-based) |
| 3 | $\displaystyle K = \frac{K_s \cdot H}{H} = K_s \cdot H$ (when $H$ cancels in $K_s$) | Relation between dimensionless $K$ and $K_s$ |
| 4 | $\displaystyle W_r = \dfrac{V}{L} = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{H}$ | Wear rate per unit sliding distance |
| 5 | $\displaystyle h = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Linear wear depth from volumetric loss |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \sigma = \dfrac{F_N}{A}$ | Contact pressure (used in wear maps) |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$ | Mass wear rate (mass flow of removed material) |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \text{PV} = \dfrac{F_N}{A} \cdot v$ | Pressure–velocity product (bearing limit) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Notation Rule**: When using the absolute value of a quantity in exam scripts, write it as $\vert x \vert$ (LaTeX `\vert` command) — never as $\lvert$ within a markdown table cell, since the bare pipe character breaks markdown rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In automotive systems, wear directly affects:
- **Engine life**: Piston ring and cylinder bore wear determine overhaul intervals.
- **Fuel economy**: Worn piston rings cause blow-by, reducing compression and increasing fuel consumption.
- **Emissions**: Worn valve guides cause oil burning → increased HC and PM emissions.
- **Emission compliance**: BS-VI / Euro-VI standards demand sub-micrometer wear control.
- **Nano-lubricant relevance**: Nano-additives (CuO, TiO₂, MoS₂, graphene) form protective tribofilms that *dramatically* reduce adhesive and abrasive wear — directly tying into Module 2 & 3 of OEMUT831.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Archard's Adhesive Wear Equation

Archard (1953) developed the fundamental wear equation by considering the geometry of asperity contact and the probability of wear particle formation.

### Step 1 — Define the Real Area of Contact

For two rough surfaces in contact under normal load $F_N$, the *real* (asperity) contact area $A_r$ is much smaller than the apparent contact area $A_n$. For plastically deforming asperities, the real area of contact is proportional to the normal load:

$$A_r = \frac{F_N}{H}$$

where $H$ is the **hardness** (or indentation hardness) of the softer material. This is a direct consequence of the fully plastic asperity deformation model.

### Step 2 — Consider a Single Asperity Junction

Let $d$ be the characteristic diameter of a single asperity junction, and $\delta$ the distance through which the asperity slides. The volume of material involved in sliding through this single junction is:

$$V_{\text{single}} = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot \delta$$

The lateral area swept (assuming the asperity shears) equals the projected junction area. The fraction of this swept volume that actually forms a wear particle is governed by a *dimensionless probability* $K$, called the **wear coefficient**.

### Step 3 — Sum Over All Asperities

Since the real contact area $A_r$ consists of many such junctions of average diameter $d$, the *number* of junctions in $A_r$ is:

$$n = \frac{A_r}{\left(\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}\right)}$$

The total wear volume produced by all junctions after sliding distance $L$ is:

$$V = n \cdot V_{\text{single}} \cdot K = \frac{A_r}{\left(\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}\right)} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot L \cdot K$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Expression

The factor $\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ cancels algebraically:

$$V = A_r \cdot L \cdot K$$

Now substitute $A_r = \dfrac{F_N}{H}$:

$$V = \frac{F_N}{H} \cdot L \cdot K$$

Rearranging into the standard textbook form:

$$\boxed{\,V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H}\,}$$

This is the **Archard Wear Equation** — the most-cited equation in tribology, and a guaranteed **14-mark question** topic in KTU board examinations.

### Step 5 — Dimensional Verification

Let us verify dimensional consistency of the equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[V] &= \frac{[K] \cdot [W] \cdot [L]}{[H]} \\
\text{mm}^3 &= \frac{\text{dimensionless} \cdot \text{N} \cdot \text{m}}{\text{N/mm}^2} \\
\text{mm}^3 &= \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}}{\text{N/mm}^2} = \text{m} \cdot \text{mm}^2 = 10^3 \text{ mm} \cdot \text{mm}^2 = 10^3 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

A unit-conversion factor of $10^{-3}$ must be applied (i.e., expressing $L$ in metres requires multiplying by $10^{-3}$ to convert to mm). This is one of the most common sources of numerical error in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Wear Mechanisms

| Wear Type | Driving Force | Wear Particle Size | Typical $K$ Value | Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adhesive | Interfacial bonding | $0.01$–$10 \;\mu\text{m}$ | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ | Piston ring – liner |
| Abrasive (2-body) | Hard surface plowing | $1$–$100 \;\mu\text{m}$ | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Gear meshing (dirty) |
| Abrasive (3-body) | Trapped particle rolling | $0.1$–$50 \;\mu\text{m}$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ | Bearing with debris |
| Fatigue (pitting) | Sub-surface cracks | $100 \;\mu\text{m}$–$1 \text{ mm}$ | Not described by $K$ | Rolling element bearing |
| Corrosive (tribochemical) | Chemical reaction | $0.01$–$1 \;\mu\text{m}$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ | Valve train, hot corrosion |
| Erosive | Particle impact | Variable | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-4}$ | Turbocharger blade |
| Fretting | Micro-oscillation | Sub-micron oxide | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ | Spline, clamped joints |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem**: A steel pin (hardness $H = 2.5 \text{ GPa}$) slides against a steel disc under a normal load $W = 100 \text{ N}$ for a sliding distance $L = 1000 \text{ m}$. Assuming adhesive wear with coefficient $K = 5 \times 10^{-4}$, calculate:
- (i) The wear volume $V$
- (ii) The specific wear rate $K_s$
- (iii) The linear wear depth $h$ if the apparent contact area is $A = 25 \text{ mm}^2$

### Solution

**Part (i) — Wear Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H} \\
&= \frac{(5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (100 \text{ N}) \cdot (1000 \text{ m})}{2.5 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2} \\
&= \frac{50}{2.5 \times 10^9} \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3 \\
&= 20 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Specific Wear Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_s &= \frac{V}{W \cdot L} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3}{(100 \text{ N}) \cdot (1000 \text{ m})} \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^3/(\text{N} \cdot \text{m}) \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mm}^3/(\text{N} \cdot \text{m})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Cross-check: $K = K_s \cdot H = (2.0 \times 10^{-13}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^9) = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ ✓ — matches the given value.

**Part (iii) — Linear Wear Depth**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{V}{A} = \frac{20 \text{ mm}^3}{25 \text{ mm}^2} = 0.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall** — Students frequently forget to convert $H$ from $\text{GPa}$ to consistent SI units ($\text{N/m}^2$) when substituting into Archard's equation. This single mistake can cause a $10^9$ factor error and a complete loss of marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wear Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[WEAR PHENOMENA] --> B[Adhesive Wear]
    A --> C[Abrasive Wear]
    A --> D[Fatigue Wear]
    A --> E[Corrosive Wear]
    A --> F[Erosive Wear]
    A --> G[Fretting Wear]

    B --> B1[Asperity Junction Formation]
    B --> B2[Material Transfer]
    B --> B3[Archard Law Applicable]

    C --> C1[Two-Body Abrasion]
    C --> C2[Three-Body Abrasion]
    C --> C3[Grooving and Cutting]

    D --> D1[Subsurface Crack Initiation]
    D --> D2[Crack Propagation]
    D --> D3[Spalling and Pitting]

    E --> E1[Oxide Film Formation]
    E --> E2[Film Removal by Sliding]
    E --> E3[Synergistic with Adhesive]

    F --> F1[Particle Impact]
    F --> F2[Droplet Impact]
    F --> F3[Cavitation Erosion]

    G --> G1[Small Amplitude Oscillation]
    G --> G2[Oxide Debris Cocoa]
    G --> G3[Fretting Fatigue Cracks]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Wear Test Methodology

```mermaid
graph LR
    SA[Sample A Pin] --> CN[Normal Load W]
    SB[Sample B Disc] --> CN
    CN --> IN[Interface Contact Zone]
    IN --> WV[Wear Volume Loss]
    WV --> MM[Mass Measurement]
    WV --> VM[Volumetric Profilometry]
    WV --> SM[Surface Microscopy SEM]
    MM --> AC[Archard Equation Solver]
    VM --> AC
    AC --> CR[Compute K and Ks]
    CR --> OP[Wear Map Classification]
    OP --> LT[Mild Oxidative]
    OP --> ST[Severe Metallic]
    OP --> SZ[Seizure Zone]
```

## 4.3 Archard Equation Logical Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST1[Normal Load W applied] --> ST2[Asperities deform plastically]
    ST2 --> ST3[Real Contact Area Ar = W over H]
    ST3 --> ST4[Asperities shear during sliding]
    ST4 --> ST5[Probability K of wear particle]
    ST5 --> ST6[Total Volume V = KWL over H]
    ST6 --> ST7[Plot on Lim Ashby Wear Map]
    ST7 --> ST8[Classify Wear Regime]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define **wear** and list any **four types of wear** with one example each.

**Model Answer:**
Wear is the progressive loss of material from the operating surface of a solid body due to relative motion at its contact interface (DIN 50320).
Four types of wear:
1. **Adhesive wear** — material transfer at asperity junctions (e.g., piston ring wear).
2. **Abrasive wear** — plowing by hard asperities or particles (e.g., contaminated gear).
3. **Fatigue wear** — spalling from cyclic subsurface stresses (e.g., rolling bearing).
4. **Corrosive (tribochemical) wear** — chemical attack + mechanical removal (e.g., hot valve train).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
Write the **Archard wear equation** and explain the significance of the **wear coefficient $K$**.

**Model Answer:**
The Archard adhesive wear equation is:

$$V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$$

where $V$ = wear volume, $W$ = normal load, $L$ = sliding distance, $H$ = hardness of softer surface.
The **wear coefficient $K$** is a dimensionless constant representing the probability that an asperity interaction produces a wear particle rather than merely transferring material. Typical $K$ values: $10^{-2}$ (severe), $10^{-4}$ (mild), $10^{-6}$ (very mild). Lower $K$ indicates a more wear-resistant system.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Carry-Over Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **mechanism of adhesive wear** with neat sketches. Derive the **Archard equation** for adhesive wear volume.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A copper pin ($H = 1.0 \text{ GPa}$) slides against a steel disc under $W = 200 \text{ N}$ for $L = 500 \text{ m}$. If the wear coefficient $K = 6 \times 10^{-4}$, determine the (i) wear volume in $\text{mm}^3$, (ii) specific wear rate $K_s$, and (iii) linear wear depth for apparent area $A = 50 \text{ mm}^2$.

### Question B (Choice 2)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the **different types of wear** with sketches and explain the **mechanism of abrasive and fatigue wear** in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **Lim-Ashby wear map**, explain how the operating regime (mild, severe, seizure) is identified. Describe the role of **nano-lubricants** in shifting the wear regime towards mild oxidative wear.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A (Choice 1)

### (a) Solution

**Adhesive Wear Mechanism**:
1. Two surfaces come into contact under load. Real contact occurs at discrete asperity junctions (very small true contact area).
2. High localized pressure at asperities causes plastic deformation and cold-welding of junctions.
3. During sliding, these junctions are sheared. If the shear occurs *within* the softer material, a fragment is transferred to the harder surface.
4. Subsequent passes may detach the fragment as a loose wear particle.

**Derivation of Archard Equation**:
- Real contact area: $\displaystyle A_r = \frac{W}{H}$
- Sliding distance = $L$
- Probability of wear particle formation = $K$
- Therefore: $\displaystyle V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$

(Derivation steps as detailed in **Section 3.1** above; reproduce in full for full marks.)

### (b) Solution

**Given**: $H = 1.0 \text{ GPa} = 1.0 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, $W = 200 \text{ N}$, $L = 500 \text{ m}$, $K = 6 \times 10^{-4}$, $A = 50 \text{ mm}^2$.

**(i) Wear Volume**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H} = \frac{(6 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (200) \cdot (500)}{1.0 \times 10^9} \\
&= \frac{60}{1.0 \times 10^9} = 6.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3 \\
&= 60 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Specific Wear Rate**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_s &= \frac{V}{W \cdot L} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-8}}{200 \cdot 500} \\
&= 6.0 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^3/(\text{N}\cdot\text{m}) \\
&= 6.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mm}^3/(\text{N}\cdot\text{m})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Linear Wear Depth**:

$$
h = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{60 \text{ mm}^3}{50 \text{ mm}^2} = 1.2 \text{ mm}
$$

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions for Question B (Choice 2)

### (a) Solution

**Classification of Wear** (refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.1**).

**Abrasive Wear Mechanism**:
- Hard asperities on one surface (2-body) or trapped particles (3-body) penetrate the softer surface.
- Material is displaced sideways to form ridges, or cut away as chips.
- Two-body: dominantly *cutting* mode; three-body: dominantly *rolling* with reduced severity.
- Severity is proportional to the **abrasive hardness / surface hardness ratio**. When this ratio exceeds ~0.8, abrasion is severe.

**Fatigue Wear Mechanism**:
- Repeated sub-surface cyclic shear stresses initiate micro-cracks below the contact surface.
- Cracks propagate parallel to the surface and eventually shear to the surface, producing a *spall* or *pit*.
- Occurs at *contact stresses below yield* — distinguishing it from adhesive/abrasive wear.
- Dominant in rolling element bearings, gear teeth, and cam-follower interfaces.

### (b) Solution

**Lim-Ashby Wear Map**:
- X-axis: $\displaystyle \frac{F_N}{A_n \cdot H}$ (normalized pressure, 0 to 1)
- Y-axis: sliding velocity $v$ (logarithmic)
- Identified regimes: **Mild oxidative wear** (low $P$, moderate $v$) → **Severe metallic wear** (high $P$) → **Seizure** (very high $P$, low $v$) → **Melting** (very high $v$).
- In the mild regime, a thin oxide film protects the surface; severity is governed by the rate of oxide growth vs. oxide removal.

**Role of Nano-Lubricants**:
1. **Tribofilm formation**: Nano-particles (CuO, TiO₂, MoS₂, graphene, h-BN) deposit in the contact zone and form a *low-shear protective film* (tribofilm) of thickness 10–100 nm.
2. **Rolling / ball-bearing effect**: Spherical nano-particles act as miniature ball bearings, converting sliding into rolling, reducing friction and direct asperity contact.
3. **Smoothing effect**: Nano-particles polish asperities (running-in acceleration), reducing effective $K_s$ by up to 50–70%.
4. **Regime shift**: With effective nano-lubrication, the operating point on the wear map shifts from *severe metallic* toward *mild oxidative*, extending component life by 2–4×.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Always convert $H$ to $\text{N/m}^2$ when $W$ is in $\text{N}$ and $L$ is in $\text{m}$. Mixing $\text{GPa}$ with $\text{N}$ and $\text{m}$ is the #1 source of calculation error.
> 2. **Skipping Derivation Steps**: Marks are split across each logical step of the Archard derivation (real area → asperity summation → probability). Skipping intermediate steps costs 2–3 marks even if the final answer is correct.
> 3. **Confusing $K$ and $K_s$**: Many students interchange the dimensionless wear coefficient $K$ with the specific wear rate $K_s$. Remember: $K_s$ has units of $\text{mm}^3/(\text{N}\cdot\text{m})$ or $\text{Pa}^{-1}$; $K$ is dimensionless.
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert $\text{m}^3$ to $\text{mm}^3$**: A factor of $10^9$ is easy to miss in final answers — always state units explicitly.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition**: Wear = progressive material loss from a surface due to relative motion (DIN 50320).
- **Five main types**: Adhesive, Abrasive, Fatigue, Corrosive (Tribochemical), Erosive.
- **Fretting wear**: Small-amplitude oscillation; critical in automotive joints (splines, clamped fits).
- **Archard's equation**: $\displaystyle V = \frac{KWL}{H}$ — the cornerstone wear law.
- **Specific wear rate**: $\displaystyle K_s = \frac{V}{WL}$ in $\text{mm}^3/(\text{N}\cdot\text{m})$.
- **Linear wear depth**: $\displaystyle h = \dfrac{V}{A}$.
- **Wear coefficient $K$**: ranges from $10^{-2}$ (severe) to $10^{-6}$ (very mild).
- **Lim-Ashby wear map**: plots normalized pressure vs. velocity; identifies mild, severe, seizure, and melting regimes.
- **Hardness $H$** in Archard's equation is of the *softer* of the two contacting materials.
- **Automotive relevance**: piston ring–liner, gear teeth, cam-follower, bearing, valve train, brake pad.
- **Nano-lubricant link**: $\text{CuO}, \text{TiO}_2, \text{MoS}_2$, graphene, h-BN reduce $K_s$ by forming tribofilms and inducing rolling contact — bridging this module with Module 2 of OEMUT831.
- **Typical $K_s$ for steel on steel (lubricated)**: $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-10} \text{ mm}^3/(\text{N}\cdot\text{m})$.
- **Always check unit consistency** — convert $H$ to $\text{N/m}^2$, $V$ to $\text{mm}^3$, $L$ to $\text{m}$ before substitution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bearings and Lubrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT831 - ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bearings and Lubrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bearings — Formal Academic Definition

A **bearing** is a machine element designed to constrain relative motion between two or more parts, typically to reduce the friction and the stress acting between the rotating and stationary members.
