Bearings are broadly classified into two main families: **sliding-contact (plain) bearings** and **rolling-contact (antifriction) bearings**. In the context of automotive tribology, bearings support shafts, gears, camshafts, crankshafts, and wheels while transmitting loads with minimal power loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Plain Bearing):** A *plain bearing* is a tribological component that carries a load on a sliding surface, typically lubricated by a hydrodynamic or boundary film of lubricant. A *rolling bearing* carries the load on rolling elements (balls or rollers) immersed in a lubricant, where the primary motion is rolling rather than sliding.

## 1.2 Lubrication — Formal Academic Definition

**Lubrication** is the process of applying a substance (lubricant) between two contacting surfaces to minimize friction, wear, heat generation, and surface damage. In automotive systems, lubrication is essential to ensure long service life, high mechanical efficiency, and reliable operation of engines, gearboxes, and chassis components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Tribological Metrics in Bearings:**
> - **Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$):** $0.001$ to $0.1$ (rolling vs. sliding).
> - **Viscosity ($\eta$):** typically $0.001$ to $1$ Pa·s for engine oils.
> - **PV Limit:** Pressure × Velocity product indicating bearing performance.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **bicycle wheel axle**. If you put a dry metal rod into a metal tube and spin it, it grinds, heats up, and eventually seizes. Now, drop a few drops of oil into the gap, and the wheel spins smoothly for hours. That thin oil film is performing the miracle of *lubrication*. The axle inside the tube is the **journal bearing** — the simplest form of a sliding bearing. A ball bearing works differently: tiny steel balls roll between the inner and outer rings, converting sliding friction into much smaller rolling friction. **Bearings are the unsung heroes** that keep every rotating automotive component alive — from the turbocharger shaft spinning at 150,000 rpm to a humble wheel hub.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** A bearing's job is *not* to eliminate contact entirely (impossible in boundary lubrication) but to *manage* it — replacing solid-solid contact with fluid-fluid shear wherever possible.

## 1.4 Classification Overview

**By motion type:**
1. **Plain (Sliding) Bearings** — Journal, Thrust, Tilting-pad.
2. **Rolling Element Bearings** — Ball, Cylindrical roller, Tapered roller, Spherical roller, Needle.

**By load direction:**
- **Radial bearings** — load perpendicular to axis of rotation.
- **Thrust (Axial) bearings** — load parallel to axis of rotation.
- **Angular contact bearings** — combined radial + axial loads.

**By lubrication regime:**
- Boundary, Mixed, Hydrodynamic, Hydrostatic, Elastohydrodynamic (EHL).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sliding (Journal) Bearing — Operational Logic

A **journal bearing** consists of a shaft (journal) rotating inside a bearing shell (sleeve) with a thin film of lubricant separating the two surfaces. As the shaft begins to rotate from rest, it climbs up the lubricant film on one side due to viscous drag, and at steady state, the journal is offset from the bearing center by an *eccentricity* $e$. The minimum oil-film thickness $h_{min}$ determines whether the bearing operates in hydrodynamic regime or fails by metal-to-metal contact.

**Step-by-step logic of hydrodynamic film formation:**
- **Step 1 — At rest:** Shaft sits at the bottom of the bearing under load $W$. Metal-to-metal contact occurs.
- **Step 2 — Start-up:** Shaft begins to rotate. Viscous oil is dragged into the converging wedge between shaft and bearing.
- **Step 3 — Hydrodynamic lift:** A pressure peak develops in the converging region, generating a load-supporting force.
- **Step 4 — Equilibrium:** The shaft reaches a steady offset position where the hydrodynamic pressure exactly supports the external load $W$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in automobiles:** Crankshaft main bearings, connecting rod big-end bearings, and camshaft bearings in internal combustion engines all rely on this exact hydrodynamic wedge principle. Without it, the engine would seize within seconds.

## 2.2 Rolling Element Bearings — Operational Logic

A **rolling bearing** uses spheres or cylinders placed between two races. The relative motion is *rolling* rather than *sliding*, drastically reducing friction. The contact between ball and race is technically a small *elliptical contact area* under high Hertzian stress, lubricated by a thin EHL film.

**Components:**
- **Inner race** — rotates with the shaft.
- **Outer race** — fixed in the housing.
- **Rolling elements** — balls or rollers.
- **Cage (retainer)** — keeps rolling elements spaced uniformly.

## 2.3 Lubrication Regimes — The Stribeck Curve

The **Stribeck curve** is the single most important diagram in lubrication theory. It plots the coefficient of friction $\mu$ versus a dimensionless group (Hersey number) defined as $\frac{\eta N}{P}$, where $\eta$ is dynamic viscosity, $N$ is rotational speed, and $P$ is bearing load per unit projected area.

| Regime | Hersey Number | Film Condition | Typical $\mu$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boundary Lubrication | Very low | Surfaces in asperity contact | $0.05 - 0.15$ |
| Mixed Lubrication | Low to moderate | Partial asperity + partial film | $0.02 - 0.06$ |
| Hydrodynamic (Full-Film) | Moderate to high | Complete film separation | $0.001 - 0.005$ |
| Hydrostatic | Speed = 0 | Externally pressurized film | $0.0005 - 0.002$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hersey number** combines three operating variables into a single regime indicator, allowing engineers to predict lubrication behavior without knowing exact film thickness.

## 2.4 Boundary vs. Hydrodynamic Lubrication

- **Boundary lubrication:** When the load is high, speed is low, or viscosity is low, the film thickness becomes comparable to surface roughness. Asperities on opposing surfaces come into direct contact, and lubrication depends on chemical films (e.g., ZDDP additives in engine oil).
- **Hydrodynamic lubrication:** When speed, viscosity, and geometry are favorable, a full fluid film (typically $1-100$ µm thick) completely separates the surfaces, and friction depends only on the lubricant's viscous shear.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Petroff's friction: $F = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu \eta N L R^3}{c}$ | $F$=friction force (N), $\mu$=friction coeff., $\eta$=viscosity (Pa·s), $N$=speed (rev/s), $L$=length (m), $R$=radius (m), $c$=radial clearance (m) | Concentric journal, low eccentricity |
| 2 | Petroff's power loss: $P_{loss} = 4 \pi^3 \mu \eta N^2 L R^3 / c$ | $P_{loss}$ in watts | Heat generation estimation |
| 3 | Reynolds Eq. (1D): $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = 6U\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ | $h$=film thickness, $p$=pressure, $U$=surface velocity | Generates pressure in converging wedge |
| 4 | Sommerfeld Number: $S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P}\!\left(\frac{R}{c}\right)^{\!2}$ | Dimensionless | Bearing design chart input |
| 5 | Load capacity: $W = 2 \pi R L \eta U (R/c)^2 \cdot f(\epsilon)$ | $\epsilon=e/c$ = eccentricity ratio | Hydrodynamic journal bearing |
| 6 | Coefficient of friction: $\mu_f = (R/c) \cdot f_1(\epsilon)$ | Functional form from Raimondi-Boyd charts | Journal bearing design |
| 7 | Minimum film thickness: $h_{min} = c(1-\epsilon)$ | Critical for boundary vs. full-film check | Operating safety margin |
| 8 | Hersey number: $H = \eta N / P$ | Dimensionless | Stribeck curve classification |
| 9 | Specific film thickness: $\Lambda = h_{min} / \sigma$ | $\sigma$=combined RMS roughness | Regime prediction ($\Lambda<1$ boundary, $\Lambda>3$ full film) |
| 10 | Hertzian contact stress: $p_{max} = \frac{3F}{2\pi a b}$ | $a,b$ = contact ellipse semi-axes | Rolling bearing fatigue life |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Convention:** Note that all absolute values and vertical separators in formulas use plain text or $\vert$ notation to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **automotive engineering**, bearing-lubrication principles are used to:
- **Design** crankshaft main and rod bearings for 100,000+ km life.
- **Select** appropriate oil viscosity grade (SAE 5W-30, 10W-40) for climate and engine type.
- **Diagnose** failures such as oil starvation, cavitation, and white-etching cracks in wheel bearings.
- **Develop** nano-lubricants (e.g., MoS₂, graphene, h-BN nanoparticles dispersed in base oil) to extend boundary lubrication performance — the central theme of the OEMUT831 course.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Equation (Low-Speed, Concentric Journal Bearing)

**Assumptions:**
1. The journal is concentric with the bearing (no eccentricity).
2. The lubricant film is continuous, Newtonian, and isoviscous.
3. End-leakage effects are neglected.
4. The film is thin and velocity varies linearly across the gap (Couette flow).

**Step 1 — Velocity profile in the gap:**
For a journal rotating at angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N$ rad/s with surface velocity $U = \omega R$, the velocity at the journal surface equals $U$ and at the bearing surface equals $0$. With linear variation across gap $c$:

$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress in the fluid:**
For a Newtonian fluid, shear stress is:

$$\tau = \eta \frac{du}{dy} = \eta \cdot \frac{U}{c} = \frac{2 \pi \eta N R}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Frictional force on the journal surface:**
Friction force equals shear stress times the journal's wetted area $2 \pi R L$:

$$F = \tau \cdot (2 \pi R L) = \frac{2 \pi R L \cdot 2 \pi \eta N R}{c}$$

$$F = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^2}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient of friction:**
Dividing friction force by the normal load $W$:

$$\mu = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^2}{c \cdot W}$$

**Step 5 — Power loss:**
Power dissipated is friction force times surface velocity:

$$P_{loss} = F \cdot U = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^2}{c} \cdot 2 \pi N R = \frac{8 \pi^3 \eta N^2 L R^3}{c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Petroff's equation predicts that friction rises linearly with speed $N$ and viscosity $\eta$, and decreases with clearance $c$. It is valid only for very small eccentricities ($\epsilon \to 0$). Real bearings use the **Raimondi-Boyd charts** for $\epsilon > 0$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Reynolds Equation (1-D, Sliding Bearing)

**Assumptions:**
1. Newtonian, isoviscous lubricant.
2. Inertia and body forces are neglected.
3. The film is thin: $\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} \ll \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$.
4. No-slip at the surfaces.
5. Steady-state, laminar flow.

**Step 1 — Navier-Stokes in the film:**
For a 2-D film in the $x$-direction with film thickness $h(x)$, the momentum equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \eta \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate twice with boundary conditions:**
Boundary conditions: $u = 0$ at $y = 0$ and $u = U$ at $y = h$.

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} y + C_1$$

$$u(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply BCs:**
At $y=0$: $u = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.

At $y=h$: $u = U \Rightarrow C_1 = \frac{U}{h} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} h$.

**Step 4 — Velocity profile (full expression):**

$$u(x,y) = \frac{U y}{h} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}(h-y)y$$

The first term is **Couette flow** (drag), the second is **Poiseuille flow** (pressure-driven).

**Step 5 — Volume flow per unit width:**
Integrate $u$ from $y=0$ to $y=h$:

$$q = \int_0^h u \, dy = \frac{U h}{2} - \frac{h^3}{12 \eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

**Step 6 — Continuity (incompressible, steady):**
For no side leakage, $\frac{dq}{dx} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial q}{\partial x} = \frac{U}{2}\frac{\partial h}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{12 \eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$

**Step 7 — Final Reynolds Equation (1-D):**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Meaning:** The right-hand side $6U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ is **non-zero only in converging or diverging wedge regions**. A constant-thickness parallel gap produces no pressure. This is the foundation of why *shape* (wedges) creates load-bearing capacity in journal bearings.

## 3.3 Derivation of Load Capacity for a Journal Bearing

**Setup:** A journal of radius $R$ rotates at $N$ rev/s inside a bearing of radius $R + c$. Eccentricity is $e$, giving an eccentricity ratio $\epsilon = e/c$. The film thickness varies as:

$$h(\theta) = c(1 + \epsilon \cos\theta)$$

**Step 1 — Pressure distribution from Reynolds equation:**
Substituting $h(\theta)$ and integrating Reynolds equation around the bearing gives a pressure $p(\theta)$ that is non-zero only over the positive half-cycle (converging wedge from $\theta = 0$ to $\pi$).

**Step 2 — Load capacity integral:**
The vertical load component is:

$$W = -L \int_0^{2\pi} p(\theta) \cos\theta \, R \, d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Resulting Sommerfeld form:**

$$W = 2 \pi R L \eta U \left(\frac{R}{c}\right)^{\!2} \cdot f_W(\epsilon)$$

where $f_W(\epsilon)$ is a tabulated function (Raimondi-Boyd chart).

**Step 4 — Sommerfeld Number:**
Dividing both sides by $L D N \eta$ (a standard normalization):

$$S_o = \frac{W}{\eta N L D}\left(\frac{c}{R}\right)^{\!2} = f_W(\epsilon)$$

**Step 5 — Minimum film thickness:**

$$h_{min} = c(1 - \epsilon) = R \cdot \frac{1 - \epsilon}{\left(\frac{R}{c}\right)}$$

This is the **single most important design output** — it must exceed the combined surface roughness $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ for full-film lubrication.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Regime Selection

| Operating Condition | $h_{min}$ vs. $\sigma$ | Regime | Friction Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Lambda = h_{min}/\sigma > 3$ | $h_{min} \gg \sigma$ | Full hydrodynamic | Viscous shear only |
| $1 < \Lambda < 3$ | $h_{min} \approx \sigma$ | Mixed | Viscous + asperity |
| $\Lambda < 1$ | $h_{min} < \sigma$ | Boundary | Asperity + additive film |
| $\Lambda \to 0$ | Direct contact | Boundary/Boundary seizure | Solid-solid adhesion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bearing Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bearings] --> B[Sliding Contact Bearings]
    A --> C[Rolling Contact Bearings]
    B --> B1[Journal Bearing]
    B --> B2[Thrust Bearing]
    B --> B3[Tilting Pad Bearing]
    C --> C1[Ball Bearing]
    C --> C2[Roller Bearing]
    C2 --> C2a[Cylindrical Roller]
    C2 --> C2b[Tapered Roller]
    C2 --> C2c[Spherical Roller]
    C2 --> C2d[Needle Roller]
    C1 --> C1a[Deep Groove]
    C1 --> C1b[Angular Contact]
    C1 --> C1c[Self Aligning]
    C1 --> C1d[Thrust Ball]
```

## 4.2 Journal Bearing — Hydrodynamic Film Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Shaft at Rest] -->|Rotation begins| S1[Couette flow established]
    S1 -->|Viscous drag pulls oil| S2[Converging wedge formed]
    S2 -->|Pressure peak develops| S3[Hydrodynamic pressure lift]
    S3 -->|Equilibrium reached| S4[Shaft floats on oil film]
    S4 -->|Steady-state operation| S5[Load W supported by p theta]
    S0 -.->|Failure mode| F1[Metal to metal contact]
    F1 -.->|Seizure risk| F2[Boundary lubrication failure]
```

## 4.3 Lubrication Regimes — Stribeck Curve Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Hersey Number eta N over P] --> Y{Lambda Regime Check}
    Y -->|Lambda less than 1| Z1[Boundary Lubrication]
    Y -->|1 to 3| Z2[Mixed Lubrication]
    Y -->|3 to 10| Z3[Hydrodynamic Lubrication]
    Y -->|Greater than 10| Z4[Full Film Turbulent EHL]
    Z1 --> R1[Friction 0.05 to 0.15]
    Z2 --> R2[Friction 0.02 to 0.06]
    Z3 --> R3[Friction 0.001 to 0.005]
    Z4 --> R4[Friction 0.0005 to 0.002]
```

## 4.4 Rolling Bearing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Inner Race] -->|Press fit on shaft| A
    B[Outer Race] -->|Press fit in housing| B
    C[Rolling Element Ball] --> D[Cage Retainer]
    C --> A
    C --> B
    D -->|Spaced evenly| C
    A --> E[Lubricant Grease or Oil]
    B --> E
    C --> E
    E -->|EHL film at Hertzian contact| F[Load Transmission]
```

## 4.5 Bearing Failure Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Bearing Performance Issue] --> Q1{Visual Inspection}
    Q1 -->|Discoloration| F1[Overheating]
    Q1 -->|Cracks or pitting| F2[Fatigue spalling]
    Q1 -->|Scoring scratches| F3[Abrasive wear]
    Q1 -->|Material transfer| F4[Adhesive wear]
    Q1 -->|Cavitation pits| F5[Cavitation erosion]
    F1 --> C1[Check oil supply and viscosity]
    F2 --> C2[Check load and Hertzian stress]
    F3 --> C3[Check filtration and cleanliness]
    F4 --> C4[Check lubrication regime and additives]
    F5 --> C5[Check bearing geometry and air entrainment]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "hydrodynamic lubrication" and list the three essential conditions required for it to occur.**

**Model Answer:**
Hydrodynamic lubrication is the regime in which two moving surfaces are completely separated by a continuous fluid film whose pressure is generated solely by the relative motion and geometry of the surfaces (no external pressurization). The three essential conditions are:
1. **Relative motion** between the surfaces (sliding or rolling with slip).
2. **A converging wedge geometry** (film thickness must decrease in the direction of motion).
3. **A viscous fluid** (lubricant with sufficient viscosity at operating temperature).
*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for the three conditions.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the Stribeck curve? Explain its significance in bearing design.**

**Model Answer:**
The Stribeck curve is a plot of the coefficient of friction $\mu$ versus the Hersey number $H = \eta N / P$ for a lubricated bearing. It identifies four lubrication regimes: boundary, mixed, hydrodynamic, and (sometimes) turbulent/EHL. Its significance lies in allowing a designer to predict the operating regime of a bearing from speed, load, and viscosity alone — without computing the actual film thickness — and to design the bearing so it operates in the **hydrodynamic regime** (lowest friction, longest life).
*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for axes, 1 for design significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Confusing *coefficient of friction* with *viscosity* in Petroff's equation — these are unrelated. $\mu$ in Petroff's equation is dimensionless, while $\eta$ has units of Pa·s.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting that Reynolds equation produces pressure **only in converging regions**; writing $\partial p / \partial x$ for a parallel gap is a guaranteed zero mark.
> - **Common mistake 3:** In Raimondi-Boyd chart problems, students often confuse $\epsilon$ (eccentricity ratio) with $e$ (eccentricity) — always normalize to $c$.
> - **Mark loss zones:** Sketches without labeled axes/circles lose 1–2 marks; units not mentioned lose 1 mark; missing boundary conditions lose 2 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) Derive Petroff's equation for friction torque in a concentric journal bearing. State all assumptions clearly.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Assumptions (2 marks):*
1. The journal is concentric with the bearing (eccentricity $e = 0$).
2. The lubricant is Newtonian with constant viscosity $\eta$.
3. The film is thin, steady, and isothermal; flow is laminar and incompressible.
4. Velocity varies linearly across the film (Couette assumption).
5. End effects are neglected (infinitely long bearing approximation).

*Derivation (5 marks):*

For a journal of radius $R$ rotating at $N$ rev/s in a bearing of radius $R + c$, the linear surface velocity is $U = 2 \pi R N$.

Linear velocity profile across gap $c$:

$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{c}$$

Shear stress on the journal:

$$\tau = \eta \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{\eta U}{c} = \frac{2 \pi \eta R N}{c}$$

Friction force over the wetted area $A = 2 \pi R L$:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \frac{2 \pi \eta R N}{c} \cdot 2 \pi R L = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^2}{c}$$

**Therefore, Petroff's equation for friction force is:**

$$\boxed{F = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^2}{c}}$$

The friction torque is $T = F \cdot R = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^3}{c}$. Note that friction depends only on $N$, $\eta$, $L$, $R$, and $c$ — not on the applied load $W$ (a peculiarity of the concentric assumption that is corrected in real eccentric bearings).

**(b) A 60 mm diameter journal runs in a 60.05 mm diameter bearing at 1500 rpm. The bearing is 50 mm long. The lubricant has a viscosity of 0.05 Pa·s. Using Petroff's equation, calculate (i) the coefficient of friction, and (ii) the power loss, if the bearing supports a load of 5 kN.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Given:*
$R = 0.030$ m, $c = (60.05 - 60)/2 = 0.025$ mm $= 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m, $L = 0.050$ m, $N = 1500/60 = 25$ rev/s, $\eta = 0.05$ Pa·s, $W = 5000$ N.

*Part (i) — Coefficient of friction:*

Friction force (Petroff):

$$F = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^2}{c} = \frac{4 \pi^2 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times 0.050 \times (0.030)^2}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Numerator: $4 \pi^2 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times 0.05 \times 0.0009 = 4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times 0.05 \times 0.0009 = 0.0222$ N·m

$$\mu = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{0.0222}{5000} = 4.44 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Coefficient of friction: $\mu \approx 4.44 \times 10^{-6}$** *(extremely low — typical of well-lubricated bearings).*

*Part (ii) — Power loss:*

$$P_{loss} = F \cdot U = F \cdot 2 \pi R N = 0.0222 \times 2 \pi \times 0.030 \times 25$$

$$P_{loss} = 0.0222 \times 4.7124 = 0.1046 \text{ W} \approx 105 \text{ mW}$$

**Power loss: $P_{loss} \approx 0.105$ W**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a) With a neat sketch, describe the formation of a hydrodynamic oil film in a journal bearing. Explain the concept of eccentricity and minimum film thickness.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Sketch (3 marks):* Draw a circle representing the journal of radius $R$ inside a larger circle representing the bearing of radius $R+c$. Mark the bearing center $O$, the journal center $O'$, the eccentricity $e = OO'$, the line of action of load $W$, and the converging/diverging wedge regions. Show the minimum film thickness $h_{min}$ at the trailing (or leading) side, and the maximum thickness $h_{max}$ on the opposite side.

*Description (3 marks):*
At rest, the journal sits at the bottom of the bearing, touching it under load $W$. As the journal begins to rotate, viscous oil is dragged into the converging wedge between the journal and the bearing surface. This wedge generates a hydrodynamic pressure that lifts the journal. The journal reaches an equilibrium position offset from the bearing center by eccentricity $e$, with the load line passing through the points of maximum pressure.

*Eccentricity & $h_{min}$ (1 mark):*
The eccentricity $e$ is the offset between the journal and bearing centers. The **eccentricity ratio** is $\epsilon = e/c$. The **minimum film thickness** is:

$$h_{min} = c(1 - \epsilon)$$

This is the critical design parameter — for hydrodynamic operation, $h_{min}$ must exceed the combined RMS surface roughness of journal and bearing materials.

**(b) State and derive the 1-D Reynolds equation for hydrodynamic lubrication. Show that pressure is generated only in a converging wedge.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Statement (1 mark):*
The 1-D Reynolds equation for steady, isoviscous, thin-film flow is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

*Derivation (5 marks):* *(See Section 3.2 above for full step-by-step derivation.)*

*Key steps summary:*
1. From Navier-Stokes, $\partial p / \partial x = \eta \partial^2 u / \partial y^2$.
2. Integrate twice with BCs $u(0)=0$, $u(h)=U$:

$$u(x,y) = \frac{U y}{h} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}(h-y)y$$

3. Volume flow: $q = \frac{U h}{2} - \frac{h^3}{12\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$.
4. Continuity $\partial q / \partial x = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

*Pressure only in converging wedge (1 mark):*
If $h = $ constant (parallel gap), then $\partial h / \partial x = 0$, which means $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = 0$. This implies $\partial p / \partial x = 0$ (constant pressure, typically atmospheric), so **no load-supporting pressure is generated**. In a converging wedge, $\partial h / \partial x < 0$, which produces $\partial^2 p / \partial x^2 < 0$ — a pressure peak that can support external load. Hence hydrodynamic pressure generation is fundamentally a *geometric* phenomenon requiring wedge-shaped films.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Bearings and Lubrication (Module 1):**

- [x] **Bearing types:** Plain (journal, thrust, tilting-pad) vs. Rolling element (ball, roller subtypes).
- [x] **Petroff's equation** applies only to **concentric, isoviscous, Newtonian** journal bearings — its friction is independent of load.
- [x] **Reynolds equation** is the foundation of hydrodynamic theory — derived from Navier-Stokes with thin-film and laminar assumptions.
- [x] **Pressure generation requires a converging wedge** — flat gaps cannot support load hydrodynamically.
- [x] **Sommerfeld number** $S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P}(R/c)^2$ links operating variables to bearing geometry via Raimondi-Boyd charts.
- [x] **Eccentricity ratio** $\epsilon = e/c$ ranges from 0 (concentric) to 1 (metal contact at edge).
- [x] **Minimum film thickness** $h_{min} = c(1-\epsilon)$ — design rule: $h_{min} > 3\sigma$ for full-film hydrodynamic operation.
- [x] **Stribeck curve** plots $\mu$ vs. Hersey number $\eta N / P$ and identifies four regimes.
- [x] **Specific film thickness** $\Lambda = h_{min}/\sigma$: $\Lambda < 1$ boundary, $1 < \Lambda < 3$ mixed, $\Lambda > 3$ full film.
- [x] **Automotive applications:** crankshaft main bearings, rod bearings, camshaft bearings, wheel bearings — all rely on hydrodynamic or EHL lubrication.
- [x] **Lubricant additives** (ZDDP, MoDTC) protect during boundary lubrication regime.
- [x] **Nano-lubricants** (graphene, MoS₂, h-BN) — central to this course — extend boundary regime performance by forming protective tribofilms on asperities.
- [x] **Hertzian contact** governs stress in rolling element bearings — elliptical contact area, maximum shear below the surface.
- [x] **Failure modes:** fatigue spalling, adhesive wear, abrasive wear, cavitation, overheating.
- [x] **Units to memorize:** $\eta$ in Pa·s, $P$ in Pa, $N$ in rev/s, $c$ in m, $L$ in m, $R$ in m.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wear Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT831 - ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wear Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Wear** is defined as the progressive loss of material from the operating surface of a solid body as a result of relative motion at its contact interface with another solid, liquid, or gaseous counterface. In the context of automotive tribology, wear is *the unwanted displacement and removal of material from contacting surfaces* subjected to tangential motion, which directly governs the service life, efficiency, and reliability of engine, transmission, and braking components.

The **Wear Classification System** is the systematic taxonomy used in tribology to categorize the physical mechanisms by which material loss occurs. According to standard ISO 6601 / ASTM G40 nomenclature adopted in the KTU 2024 OEMUT831 syllabus, wear mechanisms are broadly classified into **eight primary modes** based on the dominant physical, chemical, or mechanical process responsible for material removal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** Wear is *not* a material property — it is a **system response** of the tribo-system (load, speed, environment, material pair, lubrication regime, temperature). Any change in one of these variables can shift the dominant wear mechanism.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **shredding a piece of cheese with a grater**. Now imagine doing it with a *smoother* grater versus a *rougher* one, against a *hard wooden board* versus a *soft plastic board*, and with a *cheese that is warm and oily* versus *cold and dry*. The "wear" of the grater and the board is what we study — and the type of wear that dominates depends entirely on these conditions.

In an automobile:
- **Piston rings rubbing against cylinder walls** → primarily *adhesive* and *abrasive* wear.
- **Gear teeth meshing in a gearbox** → *fatigue* (pitting) wear.
- **Brake pad against rotor** → *abrasive* + *adhesive* + *fretting* wear combined.
- **Diesel engine cylinder at high temperature** → *corrosive (tribochemical)* wear dominates.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Tribology

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Wear Metrics You Must Memorize for KTU:**
> - **Wear Rate (W):** volume of material removed per unit sliding distance, typically in **mm³/m** or **mm³/Nm** (specific wear rate).
> - **Wear Coefficient (K):** dimensionless number from Archard's equation.
> - **PV Limit:** Pressure–Velocity product beyond which lubrication breaks down.
> - **Wear Number:** Reciprocal of wear rate — used in bearing selection charts.
> - **ASTM G99 Standard Test:** Pin-on-disc configuration for wear measurement.
> - **ASTM G65 Standard Test:** Dry sand/rubber wheel test for abrasive wear.

---

**INTUITIVE SUMMARY:** Wear is material loss due to motion, and **classification** is the engineer's way of asking "WHY and HOW is the material being lost?" — because the *fix* (lubricant change, surface coating, material substitution) depends entirely on the *type* of wear occurring.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Primary Wear Mechanisms — Structured Logic

### 2.1.1 Adhesive Wear

- **Mechanism:** Microscopic junctions form at asperity contacts due to local welding under high contact pressure. When sliding continues, the weaker of the two materials fractures at a depth below the original interface, transferring material to the counterface.
- **Why it happens:** Real surfaces touch only at asperities, so even a small applied load creates *enormous* local pressures (GPa range) — enough to cause local cold-welding.
- **How to identify:** Smooth grooves, transferred material patches (pickup), and in severe cases *galling* or *scuffing* (visible plastic deformation).
- **Automotive example:** Piston skirt rubbing against cylinder bore during cold start.
- **Severity ranking:** *Galling → Scoring → Seizure* (progressive).

### 2.1.2 Abrasive Wear

- **Mechanism:** Hard asperities or hard particles (3rd body) plough or cut the softer surface.
- **Two sub-modes:**
  - **Two-body abrasion:** A hard surface slides against a softer one (e.g., grinding wheel on metal).
  - **Three-body abrasion:** Hard particles trapped between the two surfaces roll/slide, causing both surfaces to be worn (e.g., dirt in engine oil).
- **Why it happens:** When a particle's hardness ≥ 1.2 × the surface hardness, it can penetrate and abrade.
- **Automotive example:** Sand and dust in the cylinder liner; gear-tooth abrasion from contaminated lubricant.

### 2.1.3 Fatigue Wear (Surface and Subsurface)

- **Mechanism:** Repeated cyclic loading causes sub-surface micro-cracks to nucleate, propagate parallel to the surface, and finally shear off as flake-like debris (called *spalling* or *pitting*).
- **Why it happens:** Each load cycle plastically deforms a sub-surface volume; after N cycles, fatigue cracks coalesce and lift out.
- **Automotive example:** Bearing raceways, gear tooth flanks (pitting), cam-follower contacts.

### 2.1.4 Corrosive / Tribochemical Wear

- **Mechanism:** Chemical reaction with the environment (oxygen, acids in oil) forms a reaction product film on the surface. The next sliding contact mechanically removes this film, exposing fresh metal, which re-oxidizes. The net result is *synergistic* — material loss is greater than wear + corrosion added separately.
- **Why it happens:** High temperatures and chemically active environments (acidic oil, combustion byproducts) accelerate film formation.
- **Automotive example:** Cylinder liner at high temperature (acidic blow-by gases), valve guides.

### 2.1.5 Erosive Wear

- **Mechanism:** Solid particles, liquid droplets, or gas-borne contaminants impact the surface at high velocity, removing material by micro-cutting or fatigue.
- **Critical parameter:** **Impingement angle**:
  - *Ductile materials* wear most at 20°–30° (cutting mode).
  - *Brittle materials* wear most at 90° (cracking mode).
- **Automotive example:** Combustion gases carrying soot particles striking the cylinder wall (diesel particulate erosive wear).

### 2.1.6 Fretting Wear

- **Mechanism:** Small-amplitude oscillatory motion (typically < 100 µm) between two contacting surfaces, often combined with corrosion (called *fretting corrosion*).
- **Why it happens:** Vibration in a bolted joint or splined shaft, even when "nothing is moving."
- **Automotive example:** Spline couplings, bearing seats on shafts, exhaust manifold studs.

### 2.1.7 Cavitation Wear

- **Mechanism:** Pressure variations in a liquid cause vapor bubbles to form and collapse violently (implode) on a solid surface — the micro-jets and shockwaves from implosion pit and erode the surface.
- **Automotive example:** Diesel engine wet cylinder liners (vibrating cylinder bore), hydraulic pump housings.

### 2.1.8 Impact Wear

- **Mechanism:** Repeated impact loading causes plastic deformation, fatigue cracking, and finally material loss. Dominant in hammer mills, cam-follower systems at high lift rates.
- **Automotive example:** Valve seat inserts in high-performance engines.

## 2.2 The Wear Mechanism Map (Lim-Ashby Approach)

The Lim–Ashby wear mechanism map (a plot of normalized pressure vs. normalized velocity) is used to identify which mechanism dominates for a given tribo-system. **This is a high-yield KTU concept.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lim-Ashby Map Axes:**
> - X-axis: Normalized velocity $v^* = v \cdot a / \lambda$ (Peclet number analogue)
> - Y-axis: Normalized pressure $F^* = F / (A_s \cdot H)$
> - Boundaries between regions of mild oxidative wear, severe oxidation, seizure, and melt wear.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Archard's Adhesive Wear Equation** | $V = K \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{H}$ | $V$ = wear volume (mm³); $K$ = wear coefficient (dimensionless, 10⁻² to 10⁻⁷); $W$ = normal load (N); $L$ = sliding distance (m); $H$ = hardness of softer material (Pa) | Most fundamental wear equation; $K \le 10^{-4}$ → mild wear; $K \ge 10^{-2}$ → severe wear |
| 2 | **Specific Wear Rate** | $K_s = \frac{V}{W \cdot L} = \frac{K}{H}$ | $K_s$ in mm³/Nm | Used to compare materials |
| 3 | **Wear Rate by Mass Loss** | $\dot{W} = \frac{\Delta m}{\rho \cdot L}$ | $\Delta m$ = mass loss (g); $\rho$ = density (g/cm³); $L$ = sliding distance (m) | Experimental measurement |
| 4 | **Larsen-Bardon (Abrasive) Index** | $\text{Wear} = C \cdot \frac{P \cdot v \cdot t}{H}$ | $C$ = abrasivity factor; $P$ = pressure; $v$ = velocity; $t$ = time | For two-body abrasive wear |
| 5 | **Erosive Wear (Finnie Model)** | $E = \frac{m_p \cdot v_p^2}{\psi \cdot H} \cdot f(\alpha)$ | $m_p$ = particle mass; $v_p$ = impact velocity; $\psi$ = particle shape factor; $\alpha$ = impingement angle | $f(\alpha)$ = $\sin(2\alpha) - 3\sin^2(\alpha)$ for $\alpha < 45°$ |
| 6 | **Fatigue Life (Basquin) for Wear Cracking** | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$ | $\sigma_a$ = alternating stress; $N_f$ = cycles to failure; $\sigma_f', b$ = fatigue strength coefficient and exponent | Used in pitting life prediction |
| 7 | **Reynolds Equation (for boundary wear)** | $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ | $h$ = film thickness; $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity; $p$ = pressure; $U$ = entrainment velocity | Determines film thickness $\lambda$ → wear regime |
| 8 | **Lambda Ratio (Stribeck)** | $\lambda = \frac{h_{\min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}}$ | $h_{\min}$ = minimum film thickness; $R_q$ = RMS roughness | $\lambda < 1$: boundary lubrication (high wear); $1<\lambda<3$: mixed; $\lambda>3$: full film |
| 9 | **Dimensionless Wear Coefficient (K)** | $K = \frac{3 H \cdot V}{W \cdot L}$ | Same as Archard | Derived directly from #1 |
| 10 | **PV Limit (for bearings)** | $PV \le (PV)_{\text{limit}}$ | $P$ = bearing pressure (N/mm²); $V$ = sliding velocity (m/s) | Material property of bearing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of these formulas:** In automotive design, Archard's equation (#1) is used to predict the service life of cylinder liners, brake pads, and clutch plates. The Lim-Ashby map helps design engineers choose lubricant viscosity and material pairings. The lambda ratio is checked daily in the design of journal bearings and cam-follower systems.

## 2.4 The Stribeck Curve — Connecting Lubrication Regime to Wear

The Stribeck curve plots the **coefficient of friction (μ)** versus the **lambda ratio (λ)** or the Hersey number ($\eta N/P$). The shape is critical for KTU:

- **Boundary Lubrication (λ < 1):** Surfaces in asperity contact → **HIGH wear**, μ moderate.
- **Mixed Lubrication (1 < λ < 3):** Partial film → **MODERATE wear**.
- **Hydrodynamic / Elastohydrodynamic (λ > 3):** Full fluid film → **NEAR-ZERO wear**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Implication:** During cold start, oil viscosity is low → λ < 1 → boundary regime → this is why **90% of engine wear happens in the first 30 seconds after start-up**. Modern nano-lubricants (next modules of OEMUT831) directly target this regime.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Archard's Wear Equation (1953)

This derivation is the **single most important derivation in tribology** and a guaranteed KTU question.

### Step 1 — Define the Real Contact Area (From Asperity Theory)

Consider two rough surfaces in contact under normal load $W$. Since real contact occurs only at asperity tips, the **real area of contact** $A_r$ is much smaller than the apparent (geometric) area. By Bowden and Tabor (1939):

$$
A_r = \frac{W}{H}
$$

where $H$ is the indentation hardness (flow pressure) of the softer material. This is **Step 1 reasoning**: hardness is defined as load divided by projected real contact area, so rearranging gives the real contact area.

### Step 2 — Assume a Critical Junction Diameter for Adhesion

Assume each asperity junction has a critical diameter $d$ before fracture. The volume of a hemispherical wear particle formed at one junction is:

$$
v_{\text{particle}} = \frac{\pi d^3}{12}
$$

This is the volume of a hemisphere of diameter $d$.

### Step 3 — Count the Number of Junctions in the Sliding Path

When the surface slides a distance $L$, each point of contact sweeps through a path. The number of asperity interactions per unit length is proportional to the real contact area per unit length. Archard proposed that the total number of wear particles generated during sliding distance $L$ is:

$$
N = \frac{A_r \cdot L}{d} = \frac{W \cdot L}{H \cdot d}
$$

This step comes from: (real area) × (distance) / (junction diameter) gives the cumulative contact events.

### Step 4 — Multiply to Get Total Wear Volume

Total wear volume $V$ is the number of particles times the volume per particle:

$$
V = N \cdot v_{\text{particle}} = \frac{W \cdot L}{H \cdot d} \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{12}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify

The diameter $d$ cancels partially, leaving one $d^2$ in the numerator:

$$
V = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot W \cdot L}{12 \cdot H}
$$

### Step 6 — Group Into the Dimensionless Wear Coefficient $K$

Archard defined a dimensionless wear coefficient $K$ that absorbs all the geometric/probabilistic constants:

$$
K = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2}{12}
$$

Hence we obtain the **famous Archard equation**:

$$
V = K \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{H}
$$

### Step 7 — Dimensional Check

$$
[V] = \text{mm}^3; \quad [K] = \text{dimensionless}; \quad [W] = \text{N}; \quad [L] = \text{m}; \quad [H] = \text{N/mm}^2 = \text{MPa}
$$

$$
[V] = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}}{\text{N/mm}^2} = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{mm}^2}{\text{N}} = \text{m} \cdot \text{mm}^2 = (1000\,\text{mm}) \cdot \text{mm}^2 = 10^3 \cdot \text{mm}^3
$$

The equation needs unit adjustment factor; in SI practice we write:

$$
V \, [\text{m}^3] = K \cdot \frac{W \, [\text{N}] \cdot L \, [\text{m}]}{H \, [\text{Pa}]}
$$

This confirms dimensional consistency. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of $K$:**
> - $K = 10^{-10}$ to $10^{-6}$ → ultra-mild wear (well-lubricated, polished surfaces).
> - $K = 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ → mild wear (typical engine bearings).
> - $K = 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ → severe wear (boundary, no lubrication).
> - $K > 10^{-2}$ → galling / seizure imminent.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Wear Type vs. Diagnostic Features

| Wear Type | Dominant Cause | Diagnostic Surface Feature | Typical Debris Shape | Automotive Component | Lubricant Regime |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Adhesive** | Cold-welding at asperities | Transfer film, smearing, galling | Flake-like; composition matches one body | Piston ring / liner, gear teeth | Boundary (λ < 1) |
| **Abrasive (2-body)** | Hard asperity cutting | Parallel grooves, ploughing marks | Slivers / chips | Cutting tool, sand in liner | Any |
| **Abrasive (3-body)** | Trapped hard particles | Random scratches, pitting | Spherical or irregular | Gearbox with contaminated oil | Any |
| **Fatigue (Pitting)** | Cyclic sub-surface stress | Crater-like pits, spalling | Plate-like flakes | Bearing race, cam flank | EHL/Full film |
| **Corrosive (Tribo)** | Chemical reaction + mechanical | Etched surface, oxide color | Powdery, often red/black | Hot cylinder liner, valve stem | Thin-film/Boundary |
| **Erosive** | Particle impact | Directional pitting, "shark-skin" pattern | Angular fragments | Diesel cylinder (soot), turbine blade | N/A — gas/liquid stream |
| **Fretting** | Micro-vibration + oxidation | Red/brown oxide spots at contact | Fine red powder (Fe₂O₃) | Splined shaft, bearing seat | Boundary with trapped air |
| **Cavitation** | Bubble implosion | Honeycomb pits, rough texture | Irregular, melted edges | Wet cylinder liner, pump | Liquid film |
| **Impact** | Repeated shock loading | Plastic deformation, cracks | Chunks | Valve seat, cam nose | Boundary |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Archard's Equation)

> **KTU-Style Problem:** A steel pin (H = 2.5 GPa) slides against a steel disc under a normal load of 50 N at a speed of 0.5 m/s for 2 hours. The wear coefficient is $K = 5 \times 10^{-4}$. Calculate: (a) total wear volume, (b) linear wear depth on the pin (pin diameter = 8 mm), (c) specific wear rate.

**(a) Total wear volume**

Sliding distance:

$$
L = v \cdot t = 0.5 \, \text{m/s} \times 2 \times 3600 \, \text{s} = 3600 \, \text{m}
$$

Apply Archard's equation:

$$
V = K \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{H} = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot \frac{50 \times 3600}{2.5 \times 10^9}
$$

Step by step:

$$
V = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot \frac{1.8 \times 10^5}{2.5 \times 10^9} = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (7.2 \times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
V = 3.6 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^3 = 0.036 \, \text{mm}^3
$$

**(b) Linear wear depth on the pin**

Pin cross-section area:

$$
A_{\text{pin}} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.008)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2
$$

Linear depth $h$ (assuming uniform wear):

$$
h = \frac{V}{A_{\text{pin}}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-8}}{5.027 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.16 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.716 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

**(c) Specific wear rate**

$$
K_s = \frac{V}{W \cdot L} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-8}}{50 \times 3600} = 2.0 \times 10^{-13} \, \text{m}^3/\text{Nm}
$$

Convert:

$$
K_s = 2.0 \times 10^{-13} \times 10^9 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{mm}^3/\text{Nm}
$$

This falls in the **mild wear** range, as expected for steel-steel with reasonable lubrication. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wear Classification Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["WEAR CLASSIFICATION TREE"]:::title
    A1["Mechanical Wear"]:::branch
    A2["Chemical Wear"]:::branch
    A3["Thermal Wear"]:::branch

    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2
    A0 --> A3

    A1 --> B1["Adhesive Wear"]
    A1 --> B2["Abrasive Wear"]
    A1 --> B3["Fatigue Wear"]
    A1 --> B4["Fretting Wear"]
    A1 --> B5["Impact Wear"]
    A1 --> B6["Cavitation Wear"]

    A2 --> C1["Oxidative / Corrosive"]
    A2 --> C2["Tribochemical Wear"]

    A3 --> D1["Hot Erosion"]
    A3 --> D2["Melt Wear"]

    B2 --> B2a["Two-Body Abrasion"]
    B2 --> B2b["Three-Body Abrasion"]

    B3 --> B3a["Surface Initiated"]
    B3 --> B3b["Subsurface Initiated"]

    C1 --> C1a["Mild Oxidation lambda greater than 1"]
    C1 --> C1b["Severe Oxidation lambda less than 1"]

    classDef title fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#16213e,stroke:#0f3460,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef default fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Wear Severity vs. Lubrication Regime (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0["STRIBECK CURVE ZONES"]:::title
    L1["Boundary Lubrication Region"]:::redzone
    L2["Mixed Lubrication Region"]:::yellowzone
    L3["Full Film EHL Region"]:::greenzone

    L0 --> L1
    L0 --> L2
    L0 --> L3

    L1 --> S1["Lambda less than 1"]
    L1 --> S2["Severe wear, asperity contact"]
    L1 --> S3["Adhesive + Abrasive dominate"]

    L2 --> S4["Lambda 1 to 3"]
    L2 --> S5["Moderate wear, partial film"]
    L2 --> S6["Fatigue + mild abrasion"]

    L3 --> S7["Lambda greater than 3"]
    L3 --> S8["Negligible wear"]
    L3 --> S9["Hydrodynamic regime"]

    classDef title fill:#0d1b2a,stroke:#e0e1dd,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    classDef yellowzone fill:#e8a838,stroke:#1a1a1a
    classDef greenzone fill:#386641,stroke:#a7c957,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Wear Mechanism Identification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0["OBSERVED MATERIAL LOSS"]:::title
    Q1["Debris is metallic flake with transfer film?"]:::node
    Q2["Surface has parallel grooves?"]:::node
    Q3["Surface has crater-shaped pits?"]:::node
    Q4["Red-brown oxide powder at contact?"]:::node
    Q5["Honeycomb / rough pitted surface in liquid?"]:::node
    Q6["Directional pitting from particle impact?"]:::node

    Q0 --> Q1
    Q0 --> Q2
    Q0 --> Q3
    Q0 --> Q4
    Q0 --> Q5
    Q0 --> Q6

    Q1 -- YES --> R1["ADHESIVE WEAR"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q0

    Q2 -- YES --> R2["ABRASIVE WEAR"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q0

    Q3 -- YES --> R3["FATIGUE PITTING"]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q0

    Q4 -- YES --> R4["FRETTING CORROSION"]
    Q4 -- NO --> Q0

    Q5 -- YES --> R5["CAVITATION WEAR"]
    Q5 -- NO --> Q0

    Q6 -- YES --> R6["EROSIVE WEAR"]
    Q6 -- NO --> R7["REEXAMINE / COMBINED WEAR"]
```
