# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate clearly between Hydrodynamic and Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hydrodynamic (HL) | Elasto-Hydrodynamic (EHL) |
|---------|------------------|---------------------------|
| Surface Deformation | Negligible (rigid) | Significant (elastic) |
| Film Thickness | 1–100 µm | 0.05–1 µm |
| Contact Pressure | < 0.1 GPa | 0.5–4 GPa |
| Viscosity | Approximately constant | Strongly pressure-dependent |
| Governing Theory | Reynolds eqn (rigid) | Reynolds eqn + Hertz + Barus |
| Typical Application | Journal bearings | Ball/roller bearings, gears |
| Film Generation | Geometry + motion | Geometry + motion + elasticity |

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Stribeck Curve? Identify the lubrication regimes marked on it.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Stribeck Curve** is a graph of the **coefficient of friction ($\mu$)** versus the **Hersey Number ($He = \eta N / P$)**, where $\eta$ is dynamic viscosity, $N$ is the relative speed, and $P$ is the load per unit projected area. As the Hersey number increases (higher speed, lower load, or higher viscosity), the curve transitions through three regimes:

1. **Boundary Lubrication** (low $He$): Surfaces in asperity contact; $\mu$ is high and roughly constant.
2. **Mixed Lubrication** (intermediate $He$): Partial asperity contact plus partial fluid film; $\mu$ decreases.
3. **Full Film Lubrication** (high $He$): Complete separation of surfaces — subdivided into **Hydrodynamic (HL)** for lightly loaded, compliant-fluid cases, and **Elasto-Hydrodynamic (EHL)** for heavily loaded, concentrated-contact cases.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice]`

**(a) Derive the Reynolds Equation for a 1-D, steady, isoviscous hydrodynamic flow between a moving and a stationary surface. State all assumptions clearly.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions** (must be listed for full marks):
1. The lubricant is a Newtonian fluid.
2. The flow is laminar ($Re \ll 1$).
3. Body forces (gravity) are negligible.
4. The film is thin ($h/L \ll 1$); pressure is constant across the film.
5. No-slip conditions at both surfaces.
6. Steady-state, incompressible flow.
7. Inertial terms in Navier-Stokes are neglected.

**Derivation** (the full steps from Section 3.1 must be reproduced, leading to):

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{h^3}{12 \eta}\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = \frac{U}{2}\frac{dh}{dx}$$

or equivalently:

$$\frac{dp}{dx} = 6 \eta U \frac{h - h_m}{h^3}$$

**(b) Explain the Sommerfeld Number and its use in journal bearing design.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Sommerfeld Number** is a dimensionless bearing modulus defined as:

$$S_o = \frac{r}{c} \cdot \frac{\mu_s N}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{L}{D}\right)^2$$

where $r$ = journal radius, $c$ = radial clearance, $\mu_s$ = dynamic viscosity, $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s), $P$ = applied load per unit projected area $(F/(L \cdot D))$, $L$ = bearing length, $D$ = journal diameter.

**Uses in Journal Bearing Design:**
1. It correlates the **eccentricity ratio** ($\epsilon$) of the journal: $\epsilon$ decreases as $S_o$ increases.
2. It allows the calculation of **attitude angle** ($\phi$).
3. The **coefficient of friction** is a function of $S_o$.
4. It is used as the basis of the **Raimondi-Boyd charts**, the standard design tool for partial-arc journal bearings.
5. It enables the designer to select a lubricant viscosity and a clearance ratio to achieve the desired load capacity and friction.

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice]`

**(a) Discuss the Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL) regime, including its physical mechanism, governing equations, and the role of pressure-viscosity. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In EHL, two contacting bodies (e.g., ball on a race) under a high load deform elastically, and the lubricant trapped in the contact experiences pressures in the GPa range. The two coupled phenomena that dominate are:

1. **Elastic Deformation** of the surfaces, governed by the Hertzian contact equations:
$$a = \left(\frac{3 F R}{4 E^*}\right)^{1/3}, \quad \delta = \frac{a^2}{R}$$

2. **Piezoviscous Behaviour** of the lubricant, described by the Barus equation:
$$\eta(p) = \eta_0 \, e^{\alpha p}$$

These two effects are coupled through the Reynolds equation, in which $h$ is no longer the rigid-body geometry but includes the elastic deflection $\delta(x,y)$, and $\eta$ is no longer a constant but an exponential function of $p$.

**Physical Mechanism:** As the contact is loaded, the high pressure first increases the lubricant's viscosity dramatically, making the fluid behave almost like a solid. At the same time, the surfaces deform elastically, conforming to each other and creating a near-rectangular contact patch with a thin, nearly parallel-sided film. The pressure spike at the outlet of the contact (the famous **EHL pressure spike**) is a hallmark of the regime.

**(b) Derive the Hamrock-Dowson equation for central film thickness in an elliptical EHL contact. Compute the central film thickness for $R_x = 10$ mm, $U = 0.5$ m/s, $E' = 230$ GPa, $F = 50$ N, $\eta_0 = 0.1$ Pa·s, $\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹, $k = 1$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(Derivation steps as in Section 3.2, ending with the boxed formula.)

$$\boxed{h_c = 2.69 \, R_x \, (U^*)^{0.67} \, (G^*)^{0.53} \, (W^*)^{-0.067} \left(1 - 0.61 e^{-0.73 k}\right)}$$

**Numerical Computation** (reproducing Section 3.3):

- $U^* = \frac{0.1 \times 0.5}{230 \times 10^9 \times 0.01} = 2.174 \times 10^{-10}$
- $W^* = \frac{50}{230 \times 10^9 \times (0.01)^2} = 2.174 \times 10^{-6}$
- $G^* = 1.5 \times 10^{-8} \times 230 \times 10^9 = 3450$
- $k = 1 \Rightarrow (1 - 0.61 e^{-0.73}) = 0.7060$
- $h_c \approx 0.258 \, \mu m$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 1:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in the Reynolds equation derivation — costs a full 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing** the central ($h_c$) and minimum ($h_{min}$) Hamrock-Dowson formulae — the coefficients (2.69 vs 3.63) and exponents (0.67 vs 0.68) are **different**; using one for the other costs 7 marks instantly.
> 3. **Stating that the Hamrock-Dowson coefficients are analytical** — they are **empirical regression fits** to numerical solutions. Examiners specifically deduct a mark if you claim otherwise.
> 4. **Mixing up units** of the pressure-viscosity coefficient: $\alpha$ is in **Pa⁻¹** (or m²/N), not dimensionless.
> 5. **In Stribeck curve questions**, failing to label both axes (X: Hersey Number, Y: $\mu$) loses 1 mark each.
> 6. **In journal-bearing numericals**, forgetting to convert rotational speed from **RPM to rev/s** ($N$ in Sommerfeld Number is in rev/s, not RPM).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Hydrodynamic Lubrication** = full film + no deformation + viscous pressure build-up.
- [x] **Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication** = full film + elastic deformation + piezoviscous lubricant.
- [x] The **Reynolds Equation** is the governing PDE for both regimes; in EHL, $h$ and $\eta$ are pressure-dependent.
- [x] **Reynolds Equation (1D, steady, rigid, isoviscous)**: $\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{12 \eta}\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = \frac{U}{2}\frac{dh}{dx}$.
- [x] **Barus Law**: $\eta(p) = \eta_0 e^{\alpha p}$ — the simplest pressure-viscosity model.
- [x] **Hamrock-Dowson Central Film Thickness**: $h_c = 2.69 R_x (U^*)^{0.67} (G^*)^{0.53} (W^*)^{-0.067} (1 - 0.61 e^{-0.73 k})$.
- [x] **Hamrock-Dowson Minimum Film Thickness**: $h_{min} = 3.63 R_x (U^*)^{0.68} (G^*)^{0.49} (W^*)^{-0.073} (1 - e^{-0.68 k})$.
- [x] **Dimensionless EHL Parameters**: $U^* = \eta_0 U / (E' R_x)$, $W^* = F / (E' R_x^2)$, $G^* = \alpha E'$.
- [x] **Sommerfeld Number**: $S_o = (r/c)(\mu N/P)(L/D)^2$ — primary bearing design index.
- [x] **Hersey Number**: $He = \eta N / P$ — Stribeck curve abscissa.
- [x] **Stribeck Curve** has three regimes: Boundary → Mixed → Full Film (HL or EHL).
- [x] **Hertzian Contact Radius**: $a = (3 F R / 4 E^*)^{1/3}$ — used to set up EHL boundary conditions.
- [x] Typical EHL film thickness: **0.1 to 1 µm**; typical HL film thickness: **1 to 100 µm**.
- [x] Automotive applications: HL → journal bearings; EHL → ball/roller bearings, gear teeth, cam-follower.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalized Reynolds Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT831 - ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Generalized Reynolds Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Generalized Reynolds Equation** is the governing partial differential equation that describes the pressure distribution within a thin fluid film separating two surfaces in relative motion. It is the *master equation of hydrodynamic and elastohydrodynamic lubrication* and forms the mathematical backbone of all bearing, cam-follower, and gear-tooth lubrication analyses.

Unlike the classical (standard) Reynolds equation — derived under the restrictive assumption of laminar, isothermal, Newtonian, incompressible flow between smooth rigid surfaces — the **generalized form** systematically relaxes each of these assumptions by introducing correction factors, additional source terms, and modified transport coefficients. The general form may be expressed symbolically as:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left( \frac{\rho\, h^{3}}{\eta}\, G_{x}\,\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left( \frac{\rho\, h^{3}}{\eta}\, G_{y}\,\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) \;=\; 6\,U_{s}\,\frac{\partial (\rho h)}{\partial x} + 6\,V_{s}\,\frac{\partial (\rho h)}{\partial y} + 12\,\frac{\partial (\rho h)}{\partial t}
$$

where $G_x$ and $G_y$ are **turbulent flow correction factors** (also called generalized Reynolds or Couette-Poiseuille shear coefficients), $\rho$ is fluid density, $\eta$ is the local viscosity, $h$ is the local film thickness, and $U_s$, $V_s$ are the surface entrainment velocities in the $x$ and $y$ directions respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):**
> The generalized Reynolds equation is *the* central analytical tool for predicting load-carrying capacity, friction torque, oil flow rate, and temperature rise in journal bearings, thrust bearings, and piston-ring liner systems. Mastery of its derivation and its correction terms is essential for KTU university examination questions worth 7–14 marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are sliding two sheets of glass apart with a thin film of honey sandwiched between them. The **classical Reynolds equation** says: *the pressure that builds up in the honey depends only on the geometry and the slow, sticky, layer-by-layer sliding of the honey*. That is enough if the honey is cold, smooth on the glass, and not squished too hard.

But what if the film is moving so fast that the flow becomes **churning and turbulent**? What if the bearing surfaces are **rough** at a microscopic level? What if the load is so high that the fluid **heats up** and thins out, or the surfaces **flex elastically**? In each of these cases, the simple "sticky layers" picture is no longer accurate.

The **Generalized Reynolds Equation** is the upgraded model that *patches* the classical equation with extra mathematical terms — called **correction factors** ($G_x$, $G_y$, etc.) — that account for all these real-world complications simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Conventions Used:**
> - **Dynamic viscosity** $\eta$ (Pa·s) — fluid's resistance to shear
> - **Density** $\rho$ (kg/m³) — fluid's mass per unit volume
> - **Film thickness** $h$ (m) — perpendicular gap between surfaces
> - **Pressure** $p$ (Pa) — generated hydrodynamic load-supporting stress
> - **Entrainment velocity** $U_s$ (m/s) — effective surface speed dragging fluid into the convergent zone
> - **Standard gravity** $g = 9.81$ m/s²

## 1.3 Why "Generalized"? — The Concept of Relaxation of Assumptions

The word *generalized* explicitly signals that the equation has been derived **without imposing one or more of the classical Reynolds assumptions**. Each relaxation introduces an additional mathematical or physical term. The following table lists the principal relaxations:

| S.No. | Classical Assumption (Restricted) | Generalized Relaxation (Extended) | Mathematical Consequence |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Laminar flow | Turbulent flow permitted | $G_x, G_y$ correction factors |
| 2 | Newtonian fluid | Non-Newtonian (shear-thinning) | Apparent viscosity $\eta_a$ replaces $\eta$ |
| 3 | Constant viscosity | Pressure- and temperature-dependent | $\eta(p,T)$ via Barus / Roelands / Vogel |
| 4 | Incompressible | Compressible | $\rho$ inside the differential |
| 5 | Smooth surfaces | Surface roughness | Averaged flow factors $F_1, F_2, F_3$ |
| 6 | Rigid surfaces | Elastic deformation | Coupled with elasticity equation |
| 7 | Isothermal | Thermal effects | Energy equation coupled |
| 8 | No fluid inertia | Inertia retained | Additional convective terms |
| 9 | No-slip boundaries | Slip at wall | Navier-slip BC introduced |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Logic of Derivation

The Generalized Reynolds Equation is built in **three logical tiers**, each adding one layer of physical realism.

### Tier 1 — The Standard Reynolds Equation (Laminar, Newtonian, Smooth)
1. **Start** with the three-dimensional, incompressible Navier–Stokes equations.
2. **Apply thin-film (lubrication) approximations**:
   - $h \ll L$ and $h \ll B$, where $L$ and $B$ are the bearing length and width.
   - $\partial p / \partial z \approx 0$ (pressure is constant across the film).
   - Inertial terms $\rho(\mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{u}$ are *negligible* compared to viscous terms $\mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{u}$.
   - Body force acts only in the $z$-direction (gravity neglected in the load-bearing plane).
3. **Reduce** the $x$- and $y$-momentum equations to:
   $\partial p / \partial x = \eta \partial^2 u / \partial z^2$ and $\partial p / \partial y = \eta \partial^2 v / \partial z^2$.
4. **Integrate** twice with respect to $z$ and apply the **no-slip boundary conditions** at $z = 0$ and $z = h$ to obtain the velocity profile (Couette + Poiseuille).
5. **Integrate** the continuity equation across the film thickness from $z = 0$ to $z = h(x, y, t)$ to obtain the standard Reynolds equation.

### Tier 2 — Introduction of Compressibility and Time-Dependence
- Replace the constant $\eta$ with a local $\eta(x, y, z, p, T)$ and bring $\rho$ inside the differential operators.
- This allows the equation to model **squeeze-film action** (parking a car — the film is squeezed, not sheared) and **gas lubrication** (air bearings in HDD spindles, aerospace).

### Tier 3 — Introduction of Turbulent / Non-Newtonian / Roughness Corrections
- The local shear stress is no longer $\tau = \eta \, \partial u / \partial z$. It becomes a non-linear function $\tau = f(\partial u / \partial z)$.
- Integrate this constitutive law across $z$ to obtain modified volumetric flow rates:
  $q_x = -\frac{\rho h^{3}}{12 \eta_{eq}} G_x \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\rho h}{2} U_s$ (and similarly for $q_y$).
- Substitution into the integrated continuity equation yields the **Generalized Reynolds Equation**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why neglect inertia?** Because the film is so thin that viscous shear overwhelms inertial convection — characterized by a very small Reynolds number of the film $Re_{film} = \rho U h / \eta \ll 1$. However, in high-speed turbocharger bearings and large marine propeller shaft bearings, this assumption breaks down — hence the need for the *generalized* form with inertia.
- **Why is $\partial p / \partial z \approx 0$?** Because the film is thin in $z$; the dominant pressure gradient is in the $x$ and $y$ (planar) directions.
- **Why integrate across the film?** Because the engineer does not need the velocity field at every $z$ — only the *volumetric flow rate* per unit width, which is what feeds the bearing's oil supply.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all high-yield formulas for the Generalized Reynolds Equation. It is laid out for rapid last-minute revision. **Note:** All absolute value expressions use $\lvert$ $\rvert$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\dfrac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\dfrac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 6\, U_s \dfrac{\partial(\rho h)}{\partial x} + 12\,\dfrac{\partial(\rho h)}{\partial t}$ | **Standard (laminar) Reynolds equation** — incompressible, smooth, Newtonian | Pa/s² |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\dfrac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\,G_x \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\dfrac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\,G_y \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 6\, U_s \dfrac{\partial(\rho h)}{\partial x} + 6\, V_s \dfrac{\partial(\rho h)}{\partial y} + 12\,\dfrac{\partial(\rho h)}{\partial t}$ | **Generalized Reynolds equation (turbulent)** | Pa/s² |
| 3 | $G_x = \dfrac{q_x}{q_{x,laminar}}$ , $\quad G_y = \dfrac{q_y}{q_{y,laminar}}$ | Definition of turbulent correction factors (Constantinescu / Ng-Pan) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G_x = \dfrac{1}{12}\!\left(\dfrac{\tau_{bx}}{h \, \partial p/\partial x}\right)^{2}$ (Constantinescu, fully turbulent) | Fully-turbulent shear coefficient (rough approx.) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta(p) = \eta_0 \exp(\alpha p)$ — Barus equation | Piezoviscous pressure–viscosity law | Pa·s |
| 6 | $\ln \eta + 9.67 = \left(\ln \eta_0 + 9.67\right)\!\left(1 + \dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)^{z}$ — Roelands | High-pressure viscosity model (EHL) | Pa·s |
| 7 | $\rho = \rho_0 \left[1 + \dfrac{C_p \, p}{1 + C_p \, p}\right]$ | Dowson–Higginson density model | kg/m³ |
| 8 | $q_x = -\dfrac{\rho h^{3}}{12 \eta}\, G_x\, \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\rho h}{2} U_s$ | Volumetric flow rate per unit width in x-direction | m²/s |
| 9 | $h = h_0 + h(x,y,t)$ | Film thickness (concavity function) | m |
| 10 | $Re_{film} = \dfrac{\rho\, U_s\, h}{\eta}$ | Local film Reynolds number (laminar threshold $Re_{cr} \approx 1900$) | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Generalized Reynolds Equation is *the* design equation for:

- **Journal bearings** in crankshafts, turbochargers, and electric motors.
- **Thrust bearings** in hydroelectric generators and marine propeller shafts.
- **Rolling-element bearings** — coupled with Hertzian contact theory (EHL).
- **Cam-follower systems** in IC engine valve trains.
- **Piston-ring/liner conjunction** — the largest source of friction loss in a passenger car.
- **Computer hard-disk spindles** — gas-lubricated, operating in the molecular/transition regime.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked to "derive" or "state" the Generalized Reynolds Equation, always include the correction factors $G_x$ and $G_y$ explicitly, and state *at least one* physical relaxation (e.g., turbulence, roughness, or thermal). Simply writing the classical form will cost you up to **3–4 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Navier–Stokes to Standard Reynolds Equation

### Step 1: Governing Equations for an Incompressible Newtonian Fluid

The continuity equation (mass conservation):
$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

The Navier–Stokes momentum equations (vector form) in the absence of body forces other than gravity along $z$:
$$
\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \eta \frac{\partial^{2} u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} + \rho g_i
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Lubrication (Thin-Film) Approximations

For a thin film where $z$ is perpendicular to the surfaces, the dimensionless thin-film parameter $\epsilon = h/L \ll 1$. The standard lubrication approximations are:

1. Inertia terms are negligible: $\rho(u \partial u / \partial x) \ll \eta \partial^2 u / \partial z^2$.
2. Pressure is hydrostatic across the film: $\partial p / \partial z = 0$, so $p = p(x, y, t)$.
3. The velocities scale such that $u, v \sim U_s$ and $w \sim \epsilon U_s$.
4. The body force is purely in $z$ (gravity drains oil but does not generate load).

Applying these, the $x$- and $y$-momentum equations collapse to:
$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \eta\, \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial z^{2}}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} = \eta\, \frac{\partial^{2} v}{\partial z^{2}}
$$

### Step 3: Integrate the Momentum Equation Twice in $z$

Integrating $\partial^2 u / \partial z^2 = (1/\eta)(\partial p / \partial x)$ once:
$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = \frac{1}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\, z + C_1
$$

Integrating a second time:
$$
u = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\, z^{2} + C_1 z + C_2
$$

### Step 4: Apply the No-Slip Boundary Conditions

Let the lower surface ($z = 0$) move with velocity $U_1$ in the $x$-direction, and the upper surface ($z = h$) move with velocity $U_2$. Similarly for $v$.

**Boundary Condition 1** (at $z = 0$): $u = U_1$, $v = V_1$.
**Boundary Condition 2** (at $z = h$): $u = U_2$, $v = V_2$.

Substituting:
- $u(0) = U_1 \Rightarrow C_2 = U_1$
- $u(h) = U_2 \Rightarrow U_2 = \dfrac{1}{2\eta}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} h^{2} + C_1 h + U_1$

Solving for $C_1$:
$$
C_1 = \frac{U_2 - U_1}{h} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\, h
$$

### Step 5: Write the Final Velocity Profile

Substituting $C_1$ and $C_2$ back:
$$
u = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,(z^{2} - z h) + (U_2 - U_1)\frac{z}{h} + U_1
$$

Similarly:
$$
v = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\,(z^{2} - z h) + (V_2 - V_1)\frac{z}{h} + V_1
$$

This is the **Couette + Poiseuille superposition**. The first term is the Poiseuille (pressure-driven) component; the second is the Couette (drag-driven) component.

### Step 6: Define the Entrainment Velocity

The entrainment (or surface) velocity is defined as the average of the two surface velocities:
$$
U_s = \frac{U_1 + U_2}{2}
$$

Define the **sliding velocity** $U_d = U_2 - U_1$ for the difference.

### Step 7: Integrate the Continuity Equation Across the Film

Start with continuity:
$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

Integrate from $z = 0$ to $z = h$:
$$
\int_{0}^{h}\!\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\,dz + \int_{0}^{h}\!\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\,dz + \int_{0}^{h}\!\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\,dz = 0
$$

The third integral gives $[w]_{0}^{h} = w(h) - w(0) = \partial h / \partial t$ (kinematic condition at the moving surface).

Rearranging:
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\int_{0}^{h}\! u\,dz + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\int_{0}^{h}\! v\,dz + \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0
$$

### Step 8: Compute the Volumetric Flow Rates per Unit Width

Compute $q_x = \int_0^h u\,dz$:
$$
q_x = \int_{0}^{h}\!\left[\frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,(z^{2} - z h) + U_s + \frac{U_d}{h}\!\left(z - \frac{h}{2}\right)\right]dz
$$

Carrying out the integration term-by-term:

**Poiseuille term**:
$$
\int_{0}^{h}\frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,(z^{2} - z h)\,dz = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\left[\frac{z^{3}}{3} - \frac{z^{2} h}{2}\right]_{0}^{h} = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\left(\frac{h^{3}}{3} - \frac{h^{3}}{2}\right) = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}
$$

**Couette / drag term** (mean-flow contribution $U_s$):
$$
\int_{0}^{h} U_s\,dz = U_s h
$$

**Sliding term** integrates to zero by symmetry: $\int_0^h (z - h/2) dz = 0$.

Therefore:
$$
q_x = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + U_s h
$$

Similarly:
$$
q_y = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} + V_s h
$$

### Step 9: Substitute into Integrated Continuity

$$
\frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial q_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(-\frac{h^{3}}{12\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + U_s h\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(-\frac{h^{3}}{12\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} + V_s h\right) + \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0
$$

### Step 10: Rearrange to Obtain the Standard Reynolds Equation

Assuming constant $\eta$ and grouping:
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 6\, U_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} + 6\, V_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial y} + 12\,\frac{\partial h}{\partial t}
$$

For an **incompressible** fluid with time-independent geometry ($\partial h / \partial t = 0$):
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 6\, U_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}
$$

✅ This is the **classical Reynolds equation** for steady, incompressible, laminar flow.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Generalized Form — Turbulent Correction

### Step 11: Introduce Non-Linear (Turbulent) Constitutive Law

In turbulent flow, the viscous constitutive law $\tau = \eta (\partial u / \partial z)$ is no longer valid. Instead, the **mixing-length** or **Constantinescu** model gives:
$$
\tau = \frac{\eta}{h} \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) \left( 1 + \frac{\rho l^{2}}{\eta} \left\lvert \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right\rvert \right)
$$

where $l$ is the Prandtl mixing length. For a thin film, $l \approx \kappa z$ with $\kappa \approx 0.4$ being the von Kármán constant.

### Step 12: Integrate Across the Film

The volumetric flow rate per unit width is now:
$$
q_x = \int_{0}^{h}\! u\,dz = -\frac{\rho h^{3}}{12 \eta}\, G_x\, \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\rho h}{2} U_s
$$

The dimensionless correction factor $G_x$ is defined as the **ratio of the actual flow rate to the laminar (Poiseuille) flow rate**:
$$
G_x = \frac{q_{x,\, turbulent}}{q_{x,\, laminar}}
$$

Similarly for $G_y$.

### Step 13: Substitution into Continuity Yields the Generalized Reynolds Equation

Repeating Steps 7–10 with the modified $q_x$ and $q_y$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left( \frac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\, G_x\, \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left( \frac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\, G_y\, \frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) \;=\; 6\, U_s\, \frac{\partial (\rho h)}{\partial x} + 6\, V_s\, \frac{\partial (\rho h)}{\partial y} + 12\, \frac{\partial (\rho h)}{\partial t}
$$

This is the **Generalized Reynolds Equation** in its most general (turbulent, compressible, time-dependent) form.

### Step 14: Empirical Closed-Form Expressions for $G_x$ and $G_y$

**Constantinescu (1962) model**:
$$
G_x = \frac{1}{12} \left[ 1 + \left(\frac{Re_{h}^{*}}{1600}\right)^{2} \right], \quad \text{where } Re_{h}^{*} = \frac{\rho h^{2}}{\eta}\sqrt{\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right)^{2}}
$$

**Ng-Pan (1965) model** (more accurate, two coefficients):
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left( \frac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\, \frac{G_{xx}}{G_{x}}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left( \frac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\, \frac{G_{yy}}{G_{y}}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = \ldots
$$

with separate Couette and Poiseuille correction coefficients $G_{xx}, G_{x}$ etc.

## 3.3 Analytical Boundary-Condition Matrix

| Region / Surface | Boundary Condition Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Bearing edges ($x = 0, L$) | Reynolds (cavitation) | $p = 0$, $\partial p / \partial x = 0$ | Pressure cannot be negative (sub-ambient → cavitation) |
| Film rupture (Reynolds) | $p = \partial p / \partial x = 0$ | Pressure and gradient vanish at rupture boundary |  |
| Supply groove | Dirichlet | $p = p_{supply}$ | External oil pump pressure imposed |
| Squeeze film | $\partial h / \partial t \neq 0$ | Source term $12\,\partial(\rho h)/\partial t$ | Film being squeezed normal to surfaces |
| Slip boundary (nanolubrication) | Navier-slip | $u = U_s + L_s (\partial u / \partial z)$ at wall | Slip length $L_s$ for rarefied / nano-film flow |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generalization Path

The diagram below maps the **logical evolution** from the Navier–Stokes equations to the Generalized Reynolds Equation, and the branching of correction factors that produce specialized forms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Navier-Stokes Equations<br/>Full 3D Incompressible Form"] --> B["Apply Thin-Film Approximations<br/>Inertia Neglected, dp/dz=0"]
    B --> C["Reduced Momentum Equations<br/>dp/dx = eta d2u/dz2"]
    C --> D["Integrate Twice in z<br/>Couette + Poiseuille"]
    D --> E["Apply No-Slip BC<br/>u=U1 at z=0, u=U2 at z=h"]
    E --> F["Velocity Profile Obtained<br/>u = f of z, p, eta"]
    F --> G["Integrate Continuity eqn<br/>across film 0 to h"]
    G --> H["STANDARD Reynolds Equation<br/>Laminar, Smooth, Isothermal"]
    
    H --> I{"Relax Assumption"}
    
    I --> I1["Allow Turbulent Flow<br/>tau non-linear in du/dz"]
    I --> I2["Allow Compressibility<br/>rho = rho of p, T"]
    I --> I3["Allow Surface Roughness<br/>h = h of x, y, t with stochastic"]
    I --> I4["Allow Thermal Effects<br/>eta = eta of T, coupled energy"]
    I --> I5["Allow Non-Newtonian<br/>tau = eta_a gamma_dot"]
    I --> I6["Allow Fluid Inertia<br/>retain rho u du/dx"]
    
    I1 --> J["Introduce Gx, Gy Correction<br/>Constantinescu or Ng-Pan"]
    I2 --> K["Insert rho inside derivatives<br/>Dowson-Higginson density"]
    I3 --> L["Patir-Cheng Flow Factors<br/>phi_x, phi_y, phi_s"]
    I4 --> M["Couple with Energy Eqn<br/>T = T of x, y, z, t"]
    I5 --> N["Apparent Viscosity eta_a<br/>from constitutive law"]
    I6 --> O["Inertial Reynolds Equation<br/>with convective terms"]
    
    J --> P["GENERALIZED REYNOLDS EQUATION<br/>Master Equation of Tribology"]
    K --> P
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Bearing Coordinate System & Variable Definitions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph B["Journal Bearing Geometry"]
        J1["Journal<br/>Center O1<br/>Radius R"]
        B1["Bearing<br/>Center O<br/>Radius R plus c"]
        F1["Film Thickness h<br/>h = c one plus eps cos theta"]
        V1["Entrainment Velocity Us<br/>Us = omega R"]
    end
    J1 -->|rotates| F1
    B1 -->|stationary| F1
    J1 -->|surface speed| V1
```

## 4.3 Film Geometry — Convergent–Divergent Wedge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Lower Surface<br/>U1, V1 velocity"] --> S2["Film of thickness h of x, t"]
    S2 --> S3["Upper Surface<br/>U2, V2 velocity"]
    S3 --> S4["Pressure Distribution p of x, y<br/>generated by wedge action"]
    S4 --> S5["Peak Pressure p_max<br/>in convergent region"]
    S4 --> S6["Cavitation Zone<br/>negative gauge pressure physically set to zero"]
```

## 4.4 Correction-Factor Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CF["Generalized Reynolds Equation"] --> CFX["Gx in x-direction"]
    CF --> CFY["Gy in y-direction"]
    
    CFX --> Mod1["Turbulent<br/>Constantinescu, Ng-Pan"]
    CFX --> Mod2["Roughness<br/>Patir-Cheng phi_x"]
    CFX --> Mod3["Inertia<br/>Tichy-Winer modification"]
    CFX --> Mod4["Non-Newtonian<br/>Reiner-Philippoff, Eyring"]
    
    CFY --> Mod1
    CFY --> Mod2
    CFY --> Mod3
    CFY --> Mod4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> Each branch in the taxonomy above represents a *modification* of the basic Couette-Poiseuille flow field. The Generalized Reynolds Equation is the **union** of all these modifications, evaluated simultaneously for a given operating condition.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*State the standard Reynolds equation for hydrodynamic lubrication and explain the physical meaning of each term in the equation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The standard Reynolds equation for steady, incompressible, laminar flow in a 2D thin film is:
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 6\, U_s\, \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}
$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- **LHS, term 1** — $\partial/\partial x(h^{3}/\eta \cdot \partial p/\partial x)$: net pressure-driven (Poiseuille) flow in the $x$-direction integrated across the film.
- **LHS, term 2** — analogous flow in the $y$-direction.
- **RHS, term 1** — $6 U_s \partial h / \partial x$: the rate at which the moving surfaces drag fluid into the **convergent wedge**, generating pressure.
- **RHS, term 2 (if present)** — $12 \partial h / \partial t$: rate of **squeeze-film** action (pressure generation by normal approach of surfaces).
- **Missing RHS term** — $6 V_s \partial h / \partial y$: analogous entrainment in the $y$-direction.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is meant by the "generalized" Reynolds equation? List any four assumptions of the standard Reynolds equation that are relaxed.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Generalized Reynolds Equation** is the form of the Reynolds equation obtained by relaxing one or more of the restrictive assumptions made in the classical derivation, so as to extend its validity to more realistic (high-speed, high-pressure, rough, non-Newtonian, thermal, etc.) operating conditions.

**Four classical assumptions relaxed:**

1. **Laminar flow assumption** → relaxed to allow **turbulent flow** via correction factors $G_x$, $G_y$.
2. **Constant viscosity assumption** → relaxed to allow **piezoviscous and thermoviscous** dependence: $\eta = \eta(p, T)$.
3. **Smooth, rigid surface assumption** → relaxed to allow **surface roughness** (Patir–Cheng flow factors) and **elastic deformation** (EHL coupling).
4. **Isothermal / no-inertia assumption** → relaxed to allow **thermal effects** (energy equation coupled) and **fluid inertia** (convective terms retained).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ BEFORE ATTEMPTING:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $G_x$ with $G_{xx}$**: $G_x$ in the Generalized Reynolds equation is the *combined* flow factor. The Ng-Pan model uses *four* separate coefficients ($G_{xx}, G_x, G_{yy}, G_y$) — writing only one is a 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Do not omit the assumption list** in a "derive" question. The thin-film approximations are worth **at least 2 marks** on their own.
> 3. **Always state units** in a "state" question. Writing the equation without units is treated as incomplete at KTU board valuation.
> 4. **Avoid mixing up Couette and Poiseuille terms** — the examiner specifically looks for the integration steps separately.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Derivation + Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** Starting from the Navier–Stokes equations for a thin Newtonian fluid film, **derive the standard Reynolds equation** for steady, incompressible, two-dimensional flow. State clearly all the lubrication approximations used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Extend this derivation to obtain the **Generalized Reynolds Equation** for turbulent flow. Define the turbulent correction factors $G_x$ and $G_y$ and explain the Constantinescu closed-form expression for them. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Step 1 — Write the Navier–Stokes equations** (1 mark):
$$
\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \eta\!\left(\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial z^{2}}\right) + \rho g_x
$$

And the analogous equation for the $y$-direction. Continuity: $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y + \partial w/\partial z = 0$.

**Step 2 — State the thin-film (lubrication) approximations** (1 mark):
1. The film thickness $h$ is very small compared to the bearing length $L$ ($h/L \ll 1$).
2. Inertial terms are negligible compared to viscous terms.
3. $\partial p / \partial z = 0$ (pressure is uniform across the film).
4. Body force in $z$ direction only (gravity neglected in the load-bearing plane).
5. No-slip at the surfaces.

**Step 3 — Reduce the momentum equations** (1 mark):
$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \eta\, \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial z^{2}}, \qquad \frac{\partial p}{\partial y} = \eta\, \frac{\partial^{2} v}{\partial z^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate twice and apply boundary conditions** (2 marks):
After applying $u(0) = U_1$, $u(h) = U_2$ and analogous for $v$, the velocity profile is:
$$
u = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,(z^{2} - z h) + U_1 + (U_2 - U_1)\frac{z}{h}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate continuity across the film** (1 mark):
$$
\frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial q_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Obtain the Reynolds equation** (1 mark):
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 6\, U_s\, \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} + 12\,\frac{\partial h}{\partial t}
$$

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the failure of the linear constitutive law under turbulence** (1 mark):
The classical form assumes $\tau = \eta(\partial u / \partial z)$. Under turbulent flow, the shear stress becomes a non-linear function involving the mixing length $l$:
$$
\tau = \eta \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \rho l^{2}\left\lvert \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right\rvert \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}
$$

**Step 2 — Define turbulent correction factors** (1 mark):
$$
G_x = \frac{\int_0^h u_{turb}\, dz}{\int_0^h u_{laminar}\, dz}, \qquad G_y \text{ analogous}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the modified flow rate per unit width** (1 mark):
$$
q_x = -\frac{\rho h^{3}}{12 \eta}\, G_x\, \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\rho h}{2}\, U_s
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into continuity** (1 mark):
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\, G_x\, \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\, G_y\, \frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 6\, U_s\, \frac{\partial(\rho h)}{\partial x} + 12\,\frac{\partial(\rho h)}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 5 — State the Constantinescu closed-form expression** (2 marks):
$$
G_x = \frac{1}{12}\!\left[1 + \left(\frac{Re_{h}^{*}}{1600}\right)^{2}\right]
$$
where $Re_{h}^{*} = (\rho h^{2}/\eta)\sqrt{(\partial p/\partial x)^{2} + (\partial p/\partial y)^{2}}$ is the local modified Reynolds number of the film. **Physical meaning**: $G_x > 1$ for $Re_h^{*} > 1600$ (turbulent regime), which *reduces* the effective flow generated by a given pressure gradient — i.e., turbulent mixing increases apparent resistance.

**Step 6 — Conclude with engineering significance** (1 mark):
For high-speed bearings (gas turbine main shaft bearings, large marine propeller-shaft bearings), the turbulent form is essential; the laminar form would over-predict the load capacity by **20–40%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** What are the **lubrication approximations** used in the derivation of the standard Reynolds equation? Discuss their validity and the conditions under which they break down. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **various correction factors and additional terms** that appear in the Generalized Reynolds Equation. Specifically explain how the equation is modified to account for:
 (i) **Turbulent flow**,
 (ii) **Compressibility and pressure-dependent viscosity**,
 (iii) **Surface roughness**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

The thin-film (lubrication) approximations are (each worth ~1 mark):

1. **Neglect of fluid inertia** — valid when $Re_{film} = \rho U_s h / \eta \ll 1900$. **Breaks down** in high-speed gas bearings, large marine bearings, and turbocharger bearings where $Re_{film}$ can exceed 10,000.

2. **No pressure variation across the film thickness** ($\partial p / \partial z = 0$) — valid when the film aspect ratio $h/L$ is very small (typically $< 10^{-3}$). **Breaks down** in stepped bearings and at sharp edges of supply grooves.

3. **Neglect of body forces in the $x$ and $y$ directions** — valid when the bearing is horizontal or when the load is much larger than the fluid weight. **Breaks down** in very large slow-speed bearings (e.g., hydroelectric thrust bearings) where gravitational head is comparable to hydrodynamic pressure.

4. **No-slip boundary conditions at both walls** — valid for continuum flow with $Knudsen\ number < 0.001$. **Breaks down** in nano-scale lubrication (MEMS, hard-disk heads, nanotribology) and for gas-lubricated bearings in the rarefied regime.

5. **Constant viscosity** — valid only at low pressure and low temperature. **Breaks down** in EHL contacts where peak pressures reach 1–3 GPa (gear, rolling-element bearings).

6. **Smooth, rigid surfaces** — valid for non-compliant, machined surfaces with low $R_a$. **Breaks down** for highly loaded contacts where elastic deflection exceeds film thickness.

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**(i) Turbulent flow correction (≈ 2.5 marks):**
The generalized form introduces $G_x$ and $G_y$ (Constantinescu, 1962) into the flow terms:
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\, G_x\, \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{\rho h^{3}}{\eta}\, G_y\, \frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 6\, U_s\, \frac{\partial(\rho h)}{\partial x} + \ldots
$$
$G_x, G_y > 1$ in turbulent flow, reducing the effective load-carrying capacity compared to the laminar prediction. The Ng-Pan (1965) refined model uses four separate factors $G_{xx}, G_x, G_{yy}, G_y$ for greater accuracy.

**(ii) Compressibility and pressure-dependent viscosity (≈ 2.5 marks):**
The density $\rho$ is moved *inside* the differential operators on both sides of the equation. The Dowson–Higginson equation:
$$
\rho = \rho_0\!\left[1 + \frac{0.6 \times 10^{-9}\, p}{1 + 1.7 \times 10^{-9}\, p}\right]
$$
relates density to pressure. Viscosity may use the Roelands equation (for EHL) or the Barus equation (for moderate pressures):
$$
\eta = \eta_0 \exp(\alpha p) \quad \text{(Barus, with } \alpha \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}\text{)}
$$

**(iii) Surface roughness correction (≈ 2 marks):**
Patir and Cheng (1978) introduced **averaged flow factors** $\phi_x$, $\phi_y$, and a shear flow factor $\phi_s$ for combined longitudinal and transverse roughness. The generalized Reynolds equation becomes:
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\phi_x\, \frac{\rho h^{3}}{12 \eta}\, \frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\phi_y\, \frac{\rho h^{3}}{12 \eta}\, \frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial y}\right) = \frac{U_1 + U_2}{2}\, \frac{\partial \bar{h}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \bar{h}}{\partial t}
$$
where $\bar{p}$ and $\bar{h}$ are averaged quantities and the $\phi$-factors are functions of the surface texture parameters (Peklenik number $\gamma$, $\sigma$, $\sigma^{*}$). For $\gamma = 1$ (isotropic roughness) and roughness parameter $\Lambda = h/\sigma < 3$, **significant flow reduction** (up to 50%) is observed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **Generalized Reynolds Equation** is the master PDE of hydrodynamic, mixed, and EHL lubrication; all bearing analyses reduce to solving it numerically.
- ✅ It is **derived from Navier–Stokes** under thin-film approximations, integrated across $z$ from $0$ to $h$, and re-expressed in terms of volumetric flow rate.
- ✅ The classical form assumes **laminar, Newtonian, isothermal, smooth, rigid, incompressible, no-inertia, no-slip** flow.
- ✅ Each relaxation introduces a **correction factor**:
  - Turbulent → $G_x$, $G_y$ (Constantinescu / Ng-Pan)
  - Roughness → $\phi_x$, $\phi_y$, $\phi_s$ (Patir–Cheng)
  - Non-Newtonian → apparent viscosity $\eta_a$ (Reiner–Philippoff, Eyring)
  - Compressibility → $\rho$ inside derivatives + Dowson–Higginson
  - Piezoviscous → Barus / Roelands
  - Thermal → coupled energy equation
  - Inertia → retained convective terms (Tichy–Winer)
- ✅ The **three physical mechanisms** generating pressure are:
  1. **Wedge action** — $6 U_s \partial h / \partial x$ (Couette + Poiseuille, steady-state load support)
  2. **Stretch action** — $6 V_s \partial h / \partial y$
  3. **Squeeze action** — $12 \partial h / \partial t$ (transient, e.g., starting/stopping a journal)
- ✅ The **LHS of the equation** represents the *net lateral flow divergence*; the **RHS** represents the *rate of film volume change*. Their balance guarantees mass conservation.
- ✅ The cubic dependence on $h$ in $h^3/\eta$ is the **source of non-linearity** and the reason why even a tiny change in oil viscosity produces a large change in load capacity.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark derivations, always write: (1) **Navier–Stokes form**, (2) **assumptions list**, (3) **two integrations in $z$**, (4) **boundary conditions**, (5) **continuity integration**, (6) **final rearrangement**. Missing any one of these is a 1–2 mark deduction.
- ✅ Always state **units** (Pa, m, m/s, Pa·s) in derivation steps to score the unit-and-dimension marks allocated by KTU board examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanism of Hydrodynamic Instability and Dynamic Characteristics of Hydrodynamic Journal Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT831 - ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanism of Hydrodynamic Instability and Dynamic Characteristics of Hydrodynamic Journal Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **hydrodynamic journal bearing** is a sliding-contact bearing in which a pressurized fluid film — generated entirely by the relative motion of the journal inside the bearing — separates the rotating shaft from the stationary housing. When the bearing operates far from its design point, the lubricant film can no longer suppress rotor vibrations, and the bearing exhibits **hydrodynamic instability** — a self-excited vibration phenomenon in which the journal center performs a circulatory (whirling) motion inside the clearance space at frequencies typically close to **one-half** of the shaft rotational speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrodynamic Instability (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A self-excited lateral vibration of the rotor-bearing system that occurs when the energy supplied by the pressurized oil film in a journal bearing exceeds the energy dissipated by viscous damping, causing the journal center to whirl inside the bearing clearance.

Two principal instability modes are recognized:

| Mode | Frequency | Cause |
|------|-----------|-------|
| **Oil Whirl** | ≈ 0.42 – 0.48 × N (shaft speed) | Inherent instability of the fluid film at any speed |
| **Oil Whip** | ≈ N/2 (locked to natural frequency) | Resonance with the rotor's first lateral critical speed |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Spinning Coin on a Table**
> Imagine a coin spinning rapidly on a smooth table. While it spins fast, the contact point remains nearly fixed and the coin appears stable. As it slows, the contact point begins to drift in a circular path underneath the coin — this drifting, self-driven motion is **whirl**. When the drift speed synchronizes with a natural rocking frequency of the coin (the rigid-body wobble), the wobble grows uncontrollably — this is **whip**. The journal inside its bearing behaves in exactly the same way: the oil film is the "contact patch," and any imbalance in film pressure feeds energy into a circling motion of the journal center.

A second analogy useful for automotive students: think of a car's steering wheel released on a highway at low speed. The wheel oscillates gently (whirl); at a particular speed (the resonant speed), the oscillations amplify dramatically (whip), potentially causing loss of control — exactly analogous to bearing failure.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **system-level constants** govern the dynamics:

- **Universal Stiffness Rule:** Direct stiffness coefficients $K_{xx}, K_{yy} > 0$
- **Cross-Coupling Rule:** Cross-coupled coefficients $K_{xy} = -K_{yx} \neq 0$ are the *root cause* of hydrodynamic instability
- **Critical Mass Parameter:** $m_c$ defines the maximum rotor mass for stable operation at a given Sommerfeld number
- **Whirl Frequency Ratio:** $f_w / f_s \approx 0.42 - 0.48$ for oil whirl
- **Lock-In Ratio:** $f_w / f_s = 0.50$ exactly for oil whip
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Hydrodynamic Instability — Step-by-Step Logic

The instability phenomenon is best understood as a four-stage energy cycle:

1. **Initial Perturbation:** The journal is displaced slightly from its equilibrium position $(x_0, y_0)$ inside the bearing clearance.
2. **Asymmetric Pressure Field:** Because the film thickness $h(\theta)$ is no longer symmetric, the Reynolds equation produces a pressure distribution that is no longer centered on the journal — it is offset.
3. **Tangential Force Generation:** The offset pressure field produces a resultant force that has components both opposing the displacement (stiffness) and tangent to it (cross-coupling). The tangential component *pushes* the journal in the direction of rotation — this is the energy input.
4. **Self-Excitation Threshold:** When this tangential force input exceeds the viscous damping force provided by the squeeze term of the film, the journal center begins to execute an exponentially growing circular motion — instability occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Instability is *not* caused by external forcing; the energy comes from the rotational kinetic energy of the shaft being continuously tapped by the cross-coupled stiffness. This is why instability persists even on a perfectly balanced rotor.

### 2.2 Oil Whirl vs. Oil Whip — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | Oil Whirl | Oil Whip |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| **Whirl Speed** | Approximately **half** of shaft speed, varying with load | Locked at **exactly half** of first lateral natural frequency |
| **Speed Range** | Occurs at all speeds above threshold | Occurs only near rotor's first critical speed |
| **Frequency Behaviour** | Proportional to shaft speed | Constant, independent of shaft speed |
| **Disappearance** | Disappears when shaft is decelerated | Persists until shaft falls below critical speed |
| **Severity** | Lower amplitude, often tolerable | Severe, causes bearing failure |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation a student must master for this topic. All symbols use SI units.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Magnitude |
|---|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\dfrac{h^3}{\mu}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\dfrac{h^3}{\mu}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6U\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ | Reynolds equation in 2-D (governs film pressure) | Non-dimensional form used in design |
| 2 | $h = c\left(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta\right)$ | Film thickness around journal circumference | $c = 10 - 100~\mu m$ |
| 3 | $S_o = \dfrac{r\,\omega}{\psi^{2}}\dfrac{\mu}{P} = \dfrac{\mu\,N}{P}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{\!2}$ | **Sommerfeld Number** (dimensionless) | $S_o = 0.001 - 1.0$ |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{e}{c}$ | Eccentricity ratio (0 = concentric, 1 = metal contact) | $\varepsilon = 0.4 - 0.8$ typical |
| 5 | $\begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \end{bmatrix} = -\!\begin{bmatrix} K_{xx} & K_{xy} \\ K_{yx} & K_{yy} \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} C_{xx} & C_{xy} \\ C_{yx} & C_{yy} \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{bmatrix} \dot x \\ \dot y \end{bmatrix}$ | Linearized dynamic force model | 8 stiffness + 8 damping coefficients |
| 6 | $K_{xy} = -K_{yx}$ | Cross-coupled symmetry (from antisymmetric pressure) | Always true |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\omega_{whirl}}{\omega_{shaft}} \approx \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{m}{m_{c}}\right)^{\!2}}$ | Whirl frequency ratio | Approaches 0.5 at threshold |
| 8 | $m_c = \dfrac{C_{xx}\,K_{yy} - C_{yx}\,K_{xy}}{K_{xx}\,K_{yy} - K_{xy}\,K_{yx}}$ | **Critical Mass** for stability | $m < m_c \Rightarrow$ unstable |
| 9 | $K_{xx} = \dfrac{\partial W}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{eq}$, etc. | Definition of dynamic coefficients | Linearization of static load curve |
| 10 | $f_{whip} = f_{c,1} \approx 0.5\,N$ | Oil whip frequency = first critical speed | Synchronous with rotor mode |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the cornerstone of every commercial rotor-dynamics software package (e.g., ANSYS Rotordynamics, DyRoBeS, XLRotor). Automotive turbocharger rotors, electric vehicle traction motor shafts, and crankshaft-bearing systems are all designed against these stability limits.

### 2.4 The Eight Dynamic Coefficients — Physical Interpretation

The linearized force-displacement-velocity relationship of the fluid film is fully characterized by **eight coefficients** organized into two $2\times 2$ matrices:

- **Direct Stiffness** $K_{xx}, K_{yy}$: Resistance to displacement along each axis. *Always positive.*
- **Cross-Coupled Stiffness** $K_{xy}, K_{yx}$: Force generated in a direction perpendicular to displacement. *These are the instability drivers.*
- **Direct Damping** $C_{xx}, C_{yy}$: Viscous dissipation when journal moves along an axis. *Always positive.*
- **Cross-Coupled Damping** $C_{xy}, C_{yx}$: Generally small; arises from squeeze-film asymmetry.

The matrix formulation is the standard language of KTU board examination answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reynolds Equation for a Finite Journal Bearing

Starting from Navier–Stokes equations for an incompressible Newtonian fluid with the classical hydrodynamic assumptions (no-slip, thin film, laminar, isoviscous, negligible inertia), the pressure distribution in the oil film is governed by the **generalized Reynolds equation**.

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6\,U\,\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

**Step 1 — Coordinate System.**
Fix the bearing (stator) and place the journal center at an eccentricity $e$ from the bearing center. Use cylindrical coordinates $(z, \theta)$ where $\theta = 0$ is aligned with the line of centers.

**Step 2 — Film Thickness Profile.**
By geometry, with clearance $c = R - r$ and eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon = e/c$:

$$h(\theta) = c\,\bigl(1 + \varepsilon\cos\theta\bigr)$$

**Step 3 — Velocity Components.**
With journal surface velocity $U = r\omega$, the Couette and Poiseuille velocity profiles integrate to give the flow rate per unit width:

$$q_{x} = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{U\,h}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Continuity.**
Applying conservation of mass across a control volume of width $\mathrm{d}x$ and depth $\mathrm{d}z$:

$$\frac{\partial q_{x}}{\partial x}\,\mathrm{d}x\,\mathrm{d}z + \frac{\partial q_{z}}{\partial z}\,\mathrm{d}z\,\mathrm{d}x = 0$$

**Step 5 — Substitution.**
Substituting the flow rates from Step 3 and simplifying by the thin-film assumption ($\partial p/\partial z$ retained but $\partial^{2}p/\partial z^{2}$ neglected vs. $\partial^{2}p/\partial x^{2}$) yields the **Reynolds equation** as given at the top.

### 3.2 Derivation: Sommerfeld Number

**Step 1 — Integrate Pressures Around Journal.**

The load capacity of a finite bearing is obtained by integrating the pressure around the bearing circumference:

$$W = \int_{0}^{2\pi} p(\theta)\,r\cos(\theta - \phi)\,\mathrm{d}\theta$$

where $\phi$ is the attitude angle.

**Step 2 — Non-Dimensionalize.**

Define dimensionless variables $\bar{x} = x/c$, $\bar{p} = p\,\psi^{2}/(\mu\omega)$, where $\psi = c/r$ is the clearance ratio. Substituting into the Reynolds equation and integrating twice yields:

$$\bar{p}(\theta) = \frac{6\,\varepsilon\sin\theta\,(2 + \varepsilon\cos\theta)}{(2 + \varepsilon^{2})(1 + \varepsilon\cos\theta)^{2}} \cdot \frac{L}{D}$$

**Step 3 — Load Expression.**

After integration, the vertical load capacity is:

$$W = \frac{\mu\,\omega\,r\,L}{\psi^{2}}\cdot\frac{6\,\pi\,\varepsilon\,(1 - \tfrac{1}{4}\varepsilon^{2})^{1/2}}{(2 + \varepsilon^{2})(1 - \varepsilon^{2})^{1/2}}$$

**Step 4 — Sommerfeld Number.**

The dimensionless group governing load capacity is:

$$\boxed{S_{o} = \frac{r\,\omega}{\psi^{2}}\cdot\frac{\mu}{P}}$$

where $P = W/(2rL)$ is the projected load. This is the master design parameter for all hydrodynamic bearings.

### 3.3 Derivation: Linearized Dynamic Coefficients

The dynamic force on the journal is non-linear in $(x, y)$. We linearize around the equilibrium position $(x_{0}, y_{0})$.

**Step 1 — Static Equilibrium.**
The static load $W$ is balanced by the integral of steady-state pressure:

$$W = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} p_{0}(\theta, z)\,r\cos\theta\,\mathrm{d}\theta\,\mathrm{d}z$$

**Step 2 — Perturbation.**
Let $x = x_{0} + \Delta x$, $y = y_{0} + \Delta y$. Perturb the pressure:

$$p(\theta, z, t) = p_{0}(\theta, z) + \Delta p(\theta, z, t)$$

**Step 3 — Linearized Reynolds Equation.**
Substituting into Reynolds and retaining only first-order terms:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h_{0}^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial \Delta p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\frac{h_{0}^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial \Delta p}{\partial z}\right) = 6\,U\,\frac{\partial \Delta h}{\partial x} + 12\,\frac{\partial h_{0}}{\partial x}\frac{\Delta h}{\mu\,...}$$

After substantial algebra (omitted here but required at KTU 14-mark level), the perturbation pressure has the form:

$$\Delta p(\theta, t) = \Delta p_{c}(\theta)\cos(\omega t) + \Delta p_{s}(\theta)\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 — Force Coefficients.**

Integrating $\Delta p$ over the bearing surface yields the eight coefficients. For brevity (the KTU 14-mark derivation is shown step-by-step in board solutions), the result is:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_{xx} &= \frac{\mu\,\omega\,L^{3}}{c^{3}}\,F_{Kxx}(\varepsilon, L/D) \\
K_{xy} &= -K_{yx} = \frac{\mu\,\omega\,L^{3}}{c^{3}}\,F_{Kxy}(\varepsilon, L/D) \\
C_{xx} &= \frac{\mu\,L^{3}}{c^{3}}\,F_{Cxx}(\varepsilon, L/D) \\
C_{xy} &= \frac{\mu\,L^{3}}{c^{3}}\,F_{Cxy}(\varepsilon, L/D)
\end{aligned}$$

where $F$ are dimensionless functions tabulated in standard tribology textbooks (Hamrock, Cameron, Pinkus & Sternlicht).

### 3.4 Derivation: Critical Mass & Routh Stability Criterion

The equations of motion for the rotor-bearing system, neglecting gyroscopic effects, are:

$$m\ddot{x} + C_{xx}\dot{x} + C_{xy}\dot{y} + K_{xx}x + K_{xy}y = 0$$
$$m\ddot{y} + C_{yx}\dot{x} + C_{yy}\dot{y} + K_{yx}x + K_{yy}y = 0$$

**Step 1 — Matrix Form.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & m \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{bmatrix} \ddot x \\ \ddot y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} C_{xx} & C_{xy} \\ C_{yx} & C_{yy} \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{bmatrix} \dot x \\ \dot y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} K_{xx} & K_{xy} \\ K_{yx} & K_{yy} \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Assume Solution.**
Let $x = X\,e^{st}$, $y = Y\,e^{st}$. Substituting yields:

$$\left[s^{2}\mathbf{M} + s\mathbf{C} + \mathbf{K}\right]\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic Equation.**
For non-trivial solution, $\det[\cdot] = 0$:

$$s^{4} + a_{3}s^{3} + a_{2}s^{2} + a_{1}s + a_{0} = 0$$

where the coefficients are:

$$\begin{aligned}
a_{3} &= \frac{C_{xx} + C_{yy}}{m} \\
a_{2} &= \frac{K_{xx} + K_{yy}}{m} + \frac{C_{xx}C_{yy} - C_{xy}C_{yx}}{m^{2}} \\
a_{1} &= \frac{K_{xx}C_{yy} + K_{yy}C_{xx} - K_{xy}C_{yx} - K_{yx}C_{xy}}{m^{2}} \\
a_{0} &= \frac{K_{xx}K_{yy} - K_{xy}K_{yx}}{m^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Routh-Hurwitz Criterion.**
For all roots of the characteristic polynomial to have **negative real parts** (stability), the following must hold:

$$\boxed{a_{i} > 0 \;\;\text{and}\;\; a_{3}a_{2}a_{1} > a_{3}^{2}a_{0} + a_{1}^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Critical Mass.**
At the threshold of instability, the condition simplifies to:

$$m_{c} = \frac{C_{xx}K_{yy} - C_{yx}K_{xy}}{K_{xx}K_{yy} - K_{xy}K_{yx}}$$

- If $m < m_c$: **unstable** (light rotor whirls).
- If $m > m_c$: **stable** (heavy rotor is damped by inertia).

This is why **heavier rotors are more stable** — a counterintuitive but central result of journal-bearing dynamics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Hydrodynamic Instability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotor Rotation at omega] --> B[Journal Whirls Inside Clearance c]
    B --> C{Equilibrium Position x0 y0}
    C -->|Perturbation dx dy| D[Asymmetric Film Thickness h theta]
    D --> E[Offset Pressure Field p theta]
    E --> F[Force Decomposition]
    F --> G[Direct Stiffness Kxx Kyy]
    F --> H[Cross Stiffness Kxy -Kyx]
    F --> I[Direct Damping Cxx Cyy]
    F --> J[Cross Damping Cxy Cyx]
    H --> K{Energy Balance Check}
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K -->|Excess Energy| L[Oil Whirl - Half Shaft Speed]
    K -->|Locked to Natural Freq| M[Oil Whip - Critical Speed]
    L --> N[Severe Bearing Failure]
    M --> N
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style N fill:#ffe1e1
    style K fill:#fff4cc
    style L fill:#ffe4b3
    style M fill:#ffcccc
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of the Dynamic Coefficient Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Journal Displacement x y] --> B[Linearized Film Force Fx Fy]
    C[Journal Velocity dxdt dydt] --> D[Squeeze Film Damping]
    B --> E[Stiffness Matrix K]
    D --> F[Damping Matrix C]
    E --> G[Rotor Equation of Motion]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Eigenvalue Analysis}
    H -->|Negative Real Part| I[Stable]
    H -->|Positive Real Part| J[Unstable - Whirl]
    J --> K[Stabilizer Design]
    K --> K1[Increase Mass]
    K --> K2[Reduce Clearance]
    K --> K3[Groove Bearings]
    K --> K4[Whip Restrictors]
    style I fill:#d4f4dd
    style J fill:#ffcccb
    style K fill:#cce5ff
```

### 4.3 Whirl vs. Whip — Campbell Diagram (Textual Representation)

```
Frequency (Hz)
     |
 f_s |--*----*----*----*----* (Shaft speed line, slope = N/60)
     |   \   |   /
 0.5f_s|---\--|--/----*------- (Half-shaft speed line)
     |    \  | /    /
     |     \ |/    /          * = Oil Whip Lock-In
     |      \X    /           / = Oil Whirl Path
     |       \  /
 f_c1|--------*-------------- (1st Critical speed = constant)
     |_________________________ Shaft Speed (rpm)
          N_thr       N_critical
```

> **Reading the diagram:** As shaft speed $N$ increases, oil whirl frequency tracks $0.5\,N$ (rising line). When the rising whirl frequency intersects the constant first critical frequency $f_{c1}$, **lock-in** occurs and the whirl frequency detaches from the half-shaft line and rides the constant critical line. This is **oil whip**.

### 4.4 8-Coefficient Force Representation Block

```mermaid

        K12[Kxy]
        K21[Kyx]

        C11[Cxx]
        C12[Cxy]
        C21[Cyx]
        C22[Cyy]
    end
    X[Displacement Vector x y] --> K11
    X --> K12
    X --> K21
    X --> K22
    V[Velocity Vector dxdt dydt] --> C11
    V --> C12
    V --> C21
    V --> C22

    K12 --> SUM
    K21 --> SUM
    K22 --> SUM
    C11 --> SUM
```
