<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define hydrodynamic instability. Differentiate between oil whirl and oil whip.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Hydrodynamic Instability:** It is a self-excited lateral vibration phenomenon in journal bearings that arises when the pressurized oil film supplies more energy to the rotor through cross-coupled stiffness forces than is dissipated by viscous damping.

| Parameter | Oil Whirl | Oil Whip |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Frequency | ≈ 0.42 – 0.48 × shaft speed | Constant at rotor's first critical speed |
| Synchronism | Asynchronous (varies with N) | Locked to natural frequency |
| Speed Range | All speeds above threshold | Only near critical speed |
| Cause | Fluid film instability | Resonance amplification of whirl |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for comparison)*

---

**Q2. What is the significance of the cross-coupled stiffness coefficient $K_{xy}$ in journal bearing dynamics?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The cross-coupled stiffness coefficient $K_{xy}$ represents the force generated in the **y-direction** per unit displacement in the **x-direction** due to the pressurized oil film. Its significance is:

1. **Instability Driver:** $K_{xy}$ is the principal cause of hydrodynamic instability; it feeds rotational energy into a tangential motion of the journal.
2. **Direction of Action:** $K_{xy} = -K_{yx} \neq 0$ indicates that displacement in one axis produces a force orthogonal to it — a gyroscopic-like behaviour in the fluid.
3. **Stability Control:** Reducing $K_{xy}$ (via elliptical bearings, offset halves, or multi-lobe geometry) directly raises the stability threshold speed.
4. **Sign Convention:** In KTU notation, positive $K_{xy}$ represents a force that *assists* the rotational direction, which is destabilizing.

*(1 mark each for 3 points + 1 for engineering implication)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
>
> **(a)** Starting from the Navier–Stokes equations, derive the Reynolds equation for a hydrodynamic journal bearing and state clearly all assumptions used. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the mechanisms of **oil whirl** and **oil whip**. State two practical methods to suppress each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Assumptions:**
1. Newtonian lubricant with constant viscosity $\mu$
2. Laminar flow (low Reynolds number in film)
3. Thin film: $h \ll r$
4. No-slip at both surfaces (journal and bearing)
5. Steady-state, incompressible flow
6. Body forces neglected (gravity negligible compared with film pressure)
7. $\partial p/\partial y = 0$ (pressure constant across film thickness)
8. Inertia terms neglected (low Reynolds number)

**Step 2 — Navier-Stokes Simplification.**
For the x-direction, the momentum equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \mu\,\frac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial y^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity Profile.**
Integrating twice and applying $u = U$ at $y = h$ and $u = 0$ at $y = 0$:

$$u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,y(y - h) + \frac{U\,y}{h}$$

**Step 4 — Flow Rate Integration.**
Volume flow rate per unit width:

$$q_{x} = \int_{0}^{h} u\,\mathrm{d}y = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{U\,h}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Mass Conservation.**
$\partial q_{x}/\partial x + \partial q_{z}/\partial z = 0$ leads directly to the 2-D Reynolds equation as stated in the formula sheet.

*(2 marks assumptions + 2 marks velocity profile + 3 marks derivation)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Sketch Description (in words for viva):**
Draw a journal inside a bearing, with the journal center offset by eccentricity $e$. The minimum film thickness $h_{min}$ is on one side, maximum $h_{max}$ on the other. Show the pressure distribution (positive on the converging side, slightly negative near the diverging side, returning to zero at $\theta = \pi$ cavitation boundary).

**Mechanism of Oil Whirl:**
When the journal is perturbed from its equilibrium, the asymmetric pressure field generates a tangential force component in the direction of shaft rotation. If this tangential force exceeds the damping, the journal center precesses (whirls) at approximately half the shaft speed in the same direction as rotation.

**Mechanism of Oil Whip:**
At the rotor's first critical speed, the whirl frequency synchronizes with the rotor's lateral natural frequency. The whirl frequency then locks to this natural frequency and remains constant even as shaft speed continues to increase — this is oil whip.

**Suppression Methods:**
- **Oil Whirl:** (i) Multi-lobe bearings (elliptical, lemon-bore); (ii) Reduce cross-coupled stiffness via bearing profile; (iii) Use tilting-pad bearings.
- **Oil Whip:** (i) Increase rotor mass to exceed $m_c$; (ii) Add squeeze-film dampers; (iii) Use active magnetic bearings as backup.

*(2 marks sketch + 2 marks whirl + 2 marks whip + 1 mark suppression)*

---

> **Question B**
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the dynamic characteristics of a hydrodynamic journal bearing. Derive expressions for the **stiffness and damping coefficients** using the linearized perturbation method. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** State the **Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion** for the journal bearing system and derive an expression for the **critical mass** $m_c$. Discuss why heavier rotors are more stable against hydrodynamic instability. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Dynamic Force Representation.**
The total hydrodynamic force on the journal is split into static and dynamic parts:

$$\begin{bmatrix} F_{x} \\ F_{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} W_{x} \\ W_{y} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \Delta F_{x} \\ \Delta F_{y} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Linearization.**
The dynamic force depends on instantaneous journal position $(x, y)$ and velocity $(\dot x, \dot y)$. Linearizing around equilibrium by Taylor expansion:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta F_{x} &\approx \frac{\partial F_{x}}{\partial x}\Delta x + \frac{\partial F_{x}}{\partial y}\Delta y + \frac{\partial F_{x}}{\partial \dot x}\Delta\dot x + \frac{\partial F_{x}}{\partial \dot y}\Delta\dot y \\
\Delta F_{y} &\approx \frac{\partial F_{y}}{\partial x}\Delta x + \frac{\partial F_{y}}{\partial y}\Delta y + \frac{\partial F_{y}}{\partial \dot x}\Delta\dot x + \frac{\partial F_{y}}{\partial \dot y}\Delta\dot y
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient Definitions.**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_{xx} &= -\frac{\partial F_{x}}{\partial x}, \quad K_{xy} = -\frac{\partial F_{x}}{\partial y} \\
K_{yx} &= -\frac{\partial F_{y}}{\partial x}, \quad K_{yy} = -\frac{\partial F_{y}}{\partial y} \\
C_{xx} &= -\frac{\partial F_{x}}{\partial \dot x}, \quad C_{xy} = -\frac{\partial F_{x}}{\partial \dot y} \\
C_{yx} &= -\frac{\partial F_{y}}{\partial \dot x}, \quad C_{yy} = -\frac{\partial F_{y}}{\partial \dot y}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Matrix Form.** The compact 8-coefficient force law is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta F_{x} \\ \Delta F_{y} \end{bmatrix} = -\!\begin{bmatrix} K_{xx} & K_{xy} \\ K_{yx} & K_{yy} \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} C_{xx} & C_{xy} \\ C_{yx} & C_{yy} \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{bmatrix} \Delta\dot x \\ \Delta\dot y \end{bmatrix}$$

*(2 marks for explanation + 3 marks for derivation + 2 marks for matrix form)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Equation of Motion.**
For an unlumped rotor of mass $m$ supported by a single hydrodynamic bearing:

$$\mathbf{M}\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{K}\,\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 2 — Characteristic Polynomial.**
Substituting $\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{Q}e^{st}$ and requiring $\det[s^{2}\mathbf{M} + s\mathbf{C} + \mathbf{K}] = 0$ gives a 4th-order polynomial in $s$ as derived in Section 3.4.

**Step 3 — Routh-Hurwitz Conditions.**
For asymptotic stability, all four conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:

$$\boxed{(i)\; a_{0}>0,\quad (ii)\; a_{1}>0,\quad (iii)\; a_{2}>0,\quad (iv)\; a_{3}>0,\quad (v)\; a_{3}a_{2}a_{1} > a_{3}^{2}a_{0} + a_{1}^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Critical Mass.**
Setting condition (v) to equality at the threshold of instability and solving for $m$:

$$m_{c} = \frac{C_{xx}K_{yy} - C_{yx}K_{xy}}{K_{xx}K_{yy} - K_{xy}K_{yx}}$$

**Step 5 — Why Heavier Rotors Are Stable.**
The rotor appears in the inertia term. When $m$ is large, the inertia forces dominate over the destabilizing cross-coupled stiffness force, and the journal cannot follow the rapidly circulating whirl orbit. Beyond $m_c$, the system gains enough inertia to "ride through" the instability without significant lateral motion. Light rotors, in contrast, are easily pushed into the whirl orbit by small cross-coupled forces.

*(2 marks state criterion + 2 marks derive $m_c$ + 3 marks physical interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** $K_{xy}$ and $K_{yx}$ have opposite signs; examiners deduct 1 mark if this symmetry is not stated.
> 2. **Frequency Confusion:** Oil whirl frequency is **NOT** equal to shaft speed — it is **half**. This is the most common 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Missing Negative Sign in Reynolds Equation:** The right-hand side must read $6U\,\partial h/\partial x$, not $6U\,\partial h/\partial t$ — partial with respect to $x$.
> 4. **Units:** Dynamic coefficients $K$ are in N/m, $C$ in N·s/m. Mixing units costs marks.
> 5. **Stability Criterion:** Many students write *only* $m < m_c$ as unstable; the correct statement is that **all four Routh-Hurwitz conditions must be simultaneously satisfied** for stability.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mechanism of Hydrodynamic Instability & Dynamic Characteristics of Journal Bearings**

- [ ] **Reynolds Equation** is the master governing PDE of the oil film; integrates to give Sommerfeld number.
- [ ] **Sommerfeld Number** $S_o = (\mu N / P)(r/c)^2$ — the master design parameter.
- [ ] **Eccentricity Ratio** $\varepsilon = e/c$; ranges $0.4 - 0.8$ in practice.
- [ ] **Hydrodynamic Instability** = self-excited whirl driven by cross-coupled stiffness, *not* external forcing.
- [ ] **Oil Whirl** = asynchronous, ≈ 0.5 × shaft speed; **Oil Whip** = synchronous, locked at first critical speed.
- [ ] **Eight Dynamic Coefficients** — four $K_{ij}$ (stiffness) + four $C_{ij}$ (damping) — fully characterize the linearized fluid film.
- [ ] **Cross-coupled stiffness** $K_{xy} = -K_{yx} \neq 0$ is the **root cause** of instability.
- [ ] **Direct stiffness** $K_{xx}, K_{yy} > 0$ — restores journal to equilibrium.
- [ ] **Direct damping** $C_{xx}, C_{yy} > 0$ — dissipates vibrational energy.
- [ ] **Routh-Hurwitz Criterion** gives the stability threshold from the 4th-order characteristic polynomial.
- [ ] **Critical Mass** $m_c = (C_{xx}K_{yy} - C_{yx}K_{xy})/(K_{xx}K_{yy} - K_{xy}K_{yx})$ — heavy rotors are stable.
- [ ] **Whirl Frequency Ratio** approaches 0.5 as $m \to m_c$ from above.
- [ ] **Suppression Strategies:** multi-lobe bearings, squeeze-film dampers, tilting-pad bearings, magnetic bearings.
- [ ] **Automotive Relevance:** crankshaft bearings, turbocharger rotors, EV motor shafts — all governed by these equations.
- [ ] **Campbell Diagram** is the standard tool to visualize lock-in from oil whirl to oil whip.
- [ ] **Attitude Angle** $\phi$ is the angle between load line and line of centers — frequently asked as a 3-mark question.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Hydrodynamic Instability & Dynamic Characteristics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plain Bearing Lubrication and Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT831 - ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plain Bearing Lubrication and Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **plain bearing** (also called a **sleeve bearing** or **journal bearing**) is a tribological component that supports a rotating shaft through a continuous lubricating film, without the use of rolling elements. It relies on **hydrodynamic** or **mixed/elastohydrodynamic lubrication** to separate the two contacting surfaces, transferring radial or axial loads while minimizing friction and wear.

In automotive applications, plain bearings are critical in:
- Engine crankshafts (main and connecting rod bearings)
- Camshaft supports
- Transmission gear shafts
- Turbocharger rotor journals
- Connecting rod big-end and small-end bearings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, plain bearings are studied as a closed-coupled system where *load capacity, friction, flow rate, and temperature rise* are interdependent — solving for one invariably requires solving for all (typically using iterative Raimondi-Boyd design charts or modern numerical solvers).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for a KTU Student

Imagine pushing a **flat-bottomed boat** across a still lake by tossing **wet, slippery leaves** underneath the hull as you move.

- As the boat (the **journal/shaft**) slides forward, the leaves (the **lubricant**) get dragged into the narrowing gap, building up a wedge of water.
- The pressure created by this wedge of leaves lifts the boat just enough so it no longer scrapes the lakebed.
- The boat "floats" on a self-generated film — no external pump is needed; the motion itself creates the supporting pressure.

That is **hydrodynamic lubrication** in plain bearings. The shaft, when rotating, drags oil into the convergent wedge between the shaft and bearing, generating pressure that supports the applied load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realisation:** The bearing is not "active" — it has no pump or external pressure source (unlike hydrostatic bearings). The load-carrying pressure is **self-generated** purely by the viscous shearing of the oil in a converging film geometry. This is the heart of Reynolds' equation.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value/Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Density of mineral oil | $\rho$ | **860 – 920** | kg/m³ |
| Specific heat of oil | $C_p$ | **~1800 – 2000** | J/(kg·K) |
| Typical L/D ratio (automotive crankshaft) | $L/D$ | **0.25 – 1.0** | – |
| Typical clearance ratio | $C/D$ or $c/r$ | **0.001 – 0.002** | – |
| Viscosity classification (SAE 20W-50) | $\mu$ | **0.01 – 0.1** | Pa·s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why viscosity matters:** In a plain bearing, viscosity ($\mu$) is the *only* parameter that simultaneously affects pressure generation, friction torque, and heat generation. Choosing the wrong oil grade is the #1 cause of bearing failure in practice.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometry of a Journal Bearing — The Building Blocks

Consider a circular journal of diameter $D$ (radius $r$) rotating inside a circular bearing of diameter $D + 2c$, where $c$ is the **radial clearance**.

- **Diametral clearance:** $C_d = 2c$
- **Clearance ratio:** $C_d / D = c/r$ (typically 0.001 – 0.002 in engine bearings)

When a load $W$ is applied, the journal shifts from the bearing center by an amount $e$ (the **eccentricity**). The closest approach between the two surfaces is the **minimum film thickness**:

$$h_{min} = c - e = c(1 - \varepsilon)$$

where $\varepsilon = e/c$ is the **attitude ratio** or **eccentricity ratio** (a dimensionless number, $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$).

The maximum film thickness (at the opposite side):

$$h_{max} = c + e = c(1 + \varepsilon)$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Lubrication

1. **The journal rotates** at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s.
2. **Viscous oil is dragged** by the rotating surface into the converging wedge of the clearance space.
3. **Pressure builds up** in the wedge due to the resistance to outflow (Poiseuille + Couette flow combination).
4. **The pressure field** integrates to produce a net hydrodynamic force that exactly balances the externally applied load $W$ at steady state.
5. **Simultaneously**, the shearing action dissipates energy as heat, raising the oil's temperature.
6. **Equilibrium** is reached when the heat generated equals the heat dissipated through the bearing housing.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind Step 3:** If the gap were *parallel* (no convergence), no net pressure would develop. The converging wedge is the geometric "engine" of hydrodynamic lubrication. This is why even a tiny taper or misalignment is enough to generate substantial load support.

## 2.3 The Petroff Equation — Friction of a Concentric (Unloaded) Bearing

Petroff (1883) solved the trivial case of a *concentric* journal (no load, $\varepsilon = 0$, full Sommerfeld). The friction torque is purely viscous:

$$T_f = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c}$$

Equivalent coefficient of friction:

$$f = \frac{T_f}{W r} \to \text{(Petroff assumes } W = 0, \text{ so we use } f_{Petroff} = \frac{2 \pi^2 \mu N r}{P c}\text{)}$$

$$\boxed{f = 2\pi^2 \left(\frac{\mu N}{P}\right) \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)}$$

where $N$ is rotational speed (rev/s) and $P = W/(2rL)$ is the bearing pressure (projected area basis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Petroff's equation is the **friction baseline**. Real bearings always have *less* friction than Petroff predicts at the same Sommerfeld number, because the loaded bearing is eccentric and parts of the film are thick. Petroff is used as a quick upper-bound check.

## 2.4 Reynolds Equation — The Governing PDE

For an incompressible, isoviscous fluid in a thin journal-bearing film, Reynolds' equation reduces to (neglecting side leakage first):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 \mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

For a circular bearing, using angular coordinate $\theta$ around the circumference:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 \mu U r \frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta}$$

The film thickness variation around the bearing (assuming the load is applied at $\theta = 0$):

$$h(\theta) = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$$

## 2.5 The Sommerfeld Number (So) — The Master Design Parameter

The Sommerfeld number is the *single most important dimensionless group* in journal-bearing design. It represents the ratio of viscous forces to applied load:

$$\boxed{S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{\mu N \, D\, L}{W} \left(\frac{D}{2c}\right)^2}$$

Equivalently, using projected load $P = W/(LD)$:

$$S_o = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu N}{P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** A *low* $S_o$ means the bearing is heavily loaded (or low speed, or thin oil) → eccentric operation → thick pressure peak, but high friction. A *high* $S_o$ means lightly loaded → nearly concentric → low friction but small minimum film thickness.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your exam-day cheat sheet. Memorize the symbols, units, and inter-relationships.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum film thickness | $h_{min} = c(1-\varepsilon)$ | m | Critical for wear prediction |
| 2 | Maximum film thickness | $h_{max} = c(1+\varepsilon)$ | m | Opposite side of load line |
| 3 | Petroff's friction coefficient | $f = 2\pi^2 \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \dfrac{r}{c}$ | – | Concentric, isoviscous |
| 4 | Petroff's friction torque | $T_f = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c}$ | N·m | Baseline torque |
| 5 | Sommerfeld number | $S_o = \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2 \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | – | **Master parameter** |
| 6 | Specific load | $P = \dfrac{W}{L D}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Projected area basis |
| 7 | Coefficient of friction (loaded) | $\dfrac{r}{c} f$ vs $S_o$ (chart) | – | Read from Raimondi-Boyd |
| 8 | Flow rate (side flow) | $Q$ vs $S_o$ (chart) | m³/s | $Q/(rc\omega L)$ form |
| 9 | Temperature rise | $\Delta T = \dfrac{f W V}{Q \rho C_p}$ | K or °C | Energy balance |
| 10 | Heat dissipated | $H_d = h_{tc} A_s (T_b - T_\infty)$ | W | $h_{tc} \approx 100\text{–}250$ W/(m²·K) |
| 11 | Friction variable (Sommerfeld alt.) | $S_o = \dfrac{r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\mu N}{P/(r/c)}$ | – | Useful for charts |

> **Critical Pitfall:** In exam answers, **never** confuse the **diametral clearance** $C_d = 2c$ with the **radial clearance** $c$. Most KTU questions use $c$ as radial clearance. Always state your assumption.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Automotive Engineering

- **Engine Bearings:** Main bearings operate at $S_o \approx 0.5$ to $5$, with $\varepsilon$ between 0.6 and 0.9.
- **Connecting Rod (Big-End):** Operates at higher $S_o$ due to oscillating motion — mixed lubrication regime is common.
- **Turbocharger Journal Bearings:** Run at extremely high speeds ($N >$ 100,000 rpm) — require very low-viscosity oils and small clearances to maintain hydrodynamic film.
- **Modern Trend:** Plain bearings are still preferred over rolling-element bearings in high-speed, low-friction applications because they are quieter, cheaper, and tolerate misalignment better.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Friction Equation (Exhaustive)

### Step 1: Geometry Setup
Consider a journal of radius $r$ running *concentrically* (no load) inside a bearing of radius $r + c$. The clearance $c \ll r$.

The linear velocity at the journal surface is:

$$U = \omega r = 2\pi N r$$

where $N$ is the rotational speed in revolutions per second.

### Step 2: Shear Stress in the Couette Flow
The fluid film is so thin that the velocity profile is linear (Couette flow approximation). The shear stress is:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \frac{U}{c} = \frac{2\pi \mu N r}{c}$$

### Step 3: Friction Force on the Journal
Integrating the shear stress over the journal's projected area $A = 2\pi r L$:

$$F_f = \tau \cdot A = \frac{2\pi \mu N r}{c} \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^2}{c}$$

### Step 4: Friction Torque
The friction force acts at radius $r$, so the torque is:

$$\boxed{T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c}}$$

### Step 5: Coefficient of Friction
Using the bearing pressure $P = W/(2rL)$ (Petroff's convention uses projected area $2rL$):

$$f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^2 / c}{2rLP} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P c}$$

$$\boxed{f = 2\pi^2 \left(\frac{\mu N}{P}\right) \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)}$$

**Conversion Check:** $\mu N/P$ has units of (Pa·s)(1/s) / Pa = 1 (dimensionless). Multiplied by $r/c$ (also dimensionless) → $f$ is dimensionless. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Sommerfeld Number from Reynolds' Equation (Short Form)

For a short bearing (i.e., $L \ll D$, side flow dominates), the Reynolds equation in $z$-direction dominates and reduces to:

$$h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = 6 \mu U r \frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta} \cdot (\theta - \theta_1)$$

Integrating twice across the bearing width ($-L/2$ to $+L/2$) with zero pressure at the ends gives a parabolic pressure profile. The load capacity becomes:

$$W = \frac{6 \mu U L^3 r \varepsilon \sin\theta_0}{c^2 (1 - \varepsilon^2)^2}$$

Rearranging for the load-to-viscosity ratio:

$$\frac{W}{\mu N L} \cdot \left(\frac{c}{r}\right)^2 = f(\varepsilon, \theta_0)$$

The left-hand side is the **inverse Sommerfeld number** $1/S_o$. This confirms that $\varepsilon$ and $\theta_0$ (the location of the pressure peak) are functions of $S_o$ alone — the foundation of the Raimondi-Boyd design charts.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A Complete Bearing Design Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a KTU-style problem.** Walk through every step. This kind of problem carries 7–14 marks in the End Semester Exam.

### Problem Statement
A 100 mm diameter crankshaft journal in an engine runs at 3000 rpm. It is supported by a bearing of length $L = 50$ mm and radial clearance $c = 0.05$ mm. The oil viscosity is $\mu = 0.02$ Pa·s and the radial load on the bearing is $W = 25$ kN. Calculate:
- (a) The Sommerfeld number
- (b) Petroff's coefficient of friction
- (c) Petroff's friction torque
- (d) The specific bearing pressure

### Given Data
- $D = 0.100$ m, $r = 0.050$ m
- $L = 0.050$ m
- $c = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $N = 3000/60 = 50$ rev/s
- $\mu = 0.02$ Pa·s
- $W = 25\,000$ N

### Part (a): Sommerfeld Number

The specific load (using projected area $L \times D$):

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{25\,000}{0.050 \times 0.100} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 5 \text{ MPa}$$

The clearance ratio:

$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.050}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1000$$

The Sommerfeld number:

$$S_o = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} = (1000)^2 \cdot \frac{0.02 \times 50}{5 \times 10^6}$$

$$S_o = 10^6 \cdot \frac{1.0}{5 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.0}{5} = 0.20$$

$$\boxed{S_o = 0.20}$$

### Part (b): Petroff's Coefficient of Friction

$$f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{0.02 \times 50}{5 \times 10^6} \cdot 1000$$

$$f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{1.0}{5 \times 10^6} \cdot 1000 = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{1.0}{5000} = \frac{2\pi^2}{5000}$$

$$f = \frac{2 \times 9.8696}{5000} = \frac{19.739}{5000} = 3.948 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.00395}$$

### Part (c): Petroff's Friction Torque

$$T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c} = \frac{4 \pi^2 \times 0.02 \times 50 \times 0.050 \times (0.050)^3}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$T_f = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.02 \times 50 \times 0.050 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.02 \times 50 \times 0.050 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$
$= 4 \times 9.8696 = 39.478$
$\times 0.02 = 0.7896$
$\times 50 = 39.478$
$\times 0.050 = 1.974$
$\times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 2.467 \times 10^{-4}$

Denominator: $5 \times 10^{-5}$

$$T_f = \frac{2.467 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.935 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_f \approx 4.94 \text{ N·m}}$$

### Part (d): Specific Bearing Pressure

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{25\,000}{0.0050} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{P = 5 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity check:** A bearing pressure of 5 MPa is **very low** for an automotive crankshaft (typical range 10–30 MPa). This suggests the problem represents a *lightly loaded* main bearing, possibly an idler gear. In a real engine, the load would be much higher, giving a smaller $S_o$ and a higher $f$ — illustrating the trade-off in design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Variables vs. Sommerfeld Number

This table is the **Raimondi-Boyd chart** in tabular form. KTU expects you to know the *qualitative* trends and be able to *read* chart values in exam questions where chart images are provided.

| $S_o$ | $\varepsilon$ | $h_{min}/c$ | $\theta_0$ (°) | $f$ (loaded) | $Q/(rc\omega L)$ | $Q_s/Q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.01 | 0.995 | 0.005 | 6.7° | 0.090 | 3.94 | 0.150 |
| 0.1 | 0.950 | 0.050 | 14.1° | 0.043 | 3.37 | 0.280 |
| 0.5 | 0.815 | 0.185 | 25.4° | 0.019 | 2.81 | 0.450 |
| 1.0 | 0.700 | 0.300 | 36.0° | 0.013 | 2.45 | 0.550 |
| 5.0 | 0.430 | 0.570 | 59.0° | 0.005 | 1.74 | 0.750 |
| 10.0 | 0.300 | 0.700 | 68.0° | 0.003 | 1.45 | 0.820 |
| ∞ | 0.000 | 1.000 | 90.0° | 0.000 | 0.000 | 1.000 |

> **Key Reading:** As $S_o$ **decreases** (heavier load, lower speed, lower viscosity):
> - $\varepsilon$ → 1 (nearly concentric — but this is misleading; it is the *other* side that becomes thin)
> - $h_{min}/c$ → 0 (risk of metal-metal contact)
> - Friction $f$ **increases**
> - Side flow $Q_s$ becomes a smaller fraction (oil is squeezed out axially less)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often assume low $S_o$ means *low* friction. **The opposite is true** — low $S_o$ (high load) drives the journal into the bearing wall, creating a steep pressure gradient and high viscous shear.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Plain Bearing Lubrication System

The following Mermaid block diagrams the *interactions* between the physical, fluid-mechanical, and thermal domains of a journal bearing — equivalent to a system-level schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        A1["Shaft Diameter D"]
        A2["Bearing Length L"]
        A3["Radial Clearance c"]
        A4["Oil Viscosity mu"]
        A5["Rotational Speed N"]
        A6["Applied Load W"]
    end

    subgraph GEOM["GEOMETRY & KINEMATICS"]
        B1["Specific Pressure P = W / (L x D)"]
        B2["Velocity U = 2 pi N r"]
        B3["Clearance Ratio r / c"]
    end

    subgraph DIMLESS["DIMENSIONLESS GROUPS"]
        C1["Sommerfeld Number So"]
        C2["Attitude Ratio epsilon"]
    end

    subgraph PERFORMANCE["PERFORMANCE OUTPUTS"]
        D1["Load Capacity W"]
        D2["Friction Coeff f"]
        D3["Friction Torque Tf"]
        D4["Film Thickness hmin"]
        D5["Flow Rate Q"]
        D6["Temperature Rise Delta T"]
    end

    subgraph THERMAL["THERMAL EQUILIBRIUM"]
        E1["Heat Generated Hg = f W V"]
        E2["Heat Dissipated Hd = htc As Tb Tinf"]
        E3["Equilibrium Oil Temperature Tb"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> C1
    A5 --> B2
    A5 --> C1
    A6 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> D2
    C1 --> D4
    C1 --> D5
    C2 --> D4
    D2 --> D3
    D2 --> D6
    D3 --> D6
    D5 --> D6
    D6 --> E1
    D6 --> E3
    E1 --> E3
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> A4
    A4 -.->|Viscosity-Temperature Coupling| C1
```

> **How to read this diagram:** The outer rectangle forms a *closed loop*. The thermal output (E3) feeds back into the oil viscosity (A4), which re-enters the Sommerfeld number. This is why **bearing design is iterative** — you guess $T_b$, read $\mu$ from a viscosity-temperature chart, compute $S_o$, then re-check $T_b$ until convergence.

## 4.2 Bearing Geometry Schematic — Eccentricity Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEARING["JOURNAL BEARING CROSS-SECTION"]
        O1["Bearing Center O"]
        O2["Journal Center O'"]
        W1["Applied Load W (downward)"]
    end

    O1 --- O2
    O2 -- "e (eccentricity)" --> O1
    W1 --> O2

    subgraph FILM["FILM THICKNESS AROUND CIRCUMFERENCE"]
        Hmin["hmin = c(1 - epsilon)"]
        Hmax["hmax = c(1 + epsilon)"]
    end

    O2 --> Hmin
    O2 --> Hmax
```

## 4.3 Pressure Distribution Around the Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRESS["PRESSURE DISTRIBUTION p vs theta"]
        A0["theta = 0: Maximum Film Thickness"]
        A1["theta = 0 to theta0: Convergent Wedge - Pressure Rises"]
        A2["theta = theta0: Peak Pressure"]
        A3["theta = theta0 to 180: Divergent Wedge - Pressure Falls"]
        A4["theta = 180: Minimum Film Thickness hmin"]
        A5["theta = 180 to 360: Sub-Atmospheric - Cavitation Zone"]
    end

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The sub-atmospheric zone in the divergent region **cavitates** — the oil vapor pressure is reached, and dissolved gases come out of solution. This is why Reynolds' equation uses the **half-Sommerfeld** boundary condition: $p = 0$ from $\theta = \pi + \theta_0$ to $2\pi - \theta_0$. Drawing this correctly earns full marks.
