# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### Question A1
**Define the Sommerfeld number. What is its physical significance in the design of journal bearings?**

**Model Answer:**
The Sommerfeld number is a dimensionless group defined as:

$$S_o = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu N}{P}$$

where $r/c$ is the clearance ratio, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, $N$ is the rotational speed, and $P$ is the bearing pressure. It represents the ratio of **viscous forces** to **applied load**. Physically, it is the single parameter that determines the operating eccentricity $\varepsilon$, the minimum film thickness $h_{min}$, the friction coefficient $f$, and the side flow rate $Q$ for a given bearing geometry. A higher $S_o$ implies a more concentric, lightly loaded bearing with low friction but potentially thin films at high eccentricity; a lower $S_o$ implies a heavily loaded bearing with high friction but a more stable hydrodynamic wedge. **(3 marks)**

### Question A2
**What is the difference between radial and thrust plain bearings? Give one automotive example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **radial journal bearing** supports a load perpendicular to the shaft axis and allows rotational motion about that axis. A **thrust bearing** supports a load parallel to the shaft axis and constrains axial motion. Automotive example of radial: the main crankshaft bearing in an engine. Automotive example of thrust: the thrust washer at the end of a transmission input shaft. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 2 Choice Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — CHOSEN: Petroff's Equation and the Sommerfeld Number

**A crankshaft journal bearing in a 4-cylinder engine has the following specifications:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Journal diameter, $D$ | 80 mm |
| Bearing length, $L$ | 40 mm |
| Radial clearance, $c$ | 0.06 mm |
| Rotational speed, $N$ | 2500 rpm |
| Applied load, $W$ | 20 kN |
| Oil viscosity, $\mu$ | 0.025 Pa·s |

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Derive Petroff's equation for the friction torque of a concentric journal bearing. Using the given data, calculate Petroff's friction coefficient and the friction torque.

**(b)** Calculate the Sommerfeld number for the bearing. Using the Raimondi-Boyd chart (or the tabulated values in the notes), estimate the actual coefficient of friction and the attitude ratio $\varepsilon$. Comment on whether the bearing is operating safely.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation and Calculation (7 Marks)

**Derivation** (already given in Section 3.1, summarized here for the exam):

Starting from the Couette flow assumption in a concentric journal of radius $r$ and clearance $c$:

- Velocity of journal surface: $U = 2\pi N r$
- Shear stress: $\tau = \mu U / c = 2\pi \mu N r / c$
- Friction force on projected area $2\pi r L$: $F_f = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L = 4\pi^2 \mu N L r^2 / c$
- Friction torque: $T_f = F_f \cdot r = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c}$
- Coefficient of friction: $f = \dfrac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P c}$ where $P = W/(LD)$

**Calculation** with the given data:
- $r = 0.040$ m, $L = 0.040$ m, $c = 6 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $N = 2500/60 = 41.667$ rev/s
- $P = W/(LD) = 20\,000/(0.040 \times 0.080) = 6.25 \times 10^6$ Pa $= 6.25$ MPa

$$f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{0.025 \times 41.667}{6.25 \times 10^6} \cdot \frac{0.040}{6 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\frac{\mu N}{P} = \frac{1.0417}{6.25 \times 10^6} = 1.667 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.040}{6 \times 10^{-5}} = 666.67$$

$$f = 2 \times 9.8696 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-7} \times 666.67 = 2.198 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{f_{Petroff} \approx 0.00220}$$

$$T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c} = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 41.667 \times 0.040 \times (0.040)^3}{6 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$r^3 = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$ m³

$$T_f = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 41.667 \times 0.040 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{6 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$T_f = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 41.667 \times 2.56 \times 10^{-6}}{6 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Numerator chain: $4 \times 9.8696 = 39.478$; $\times 0.025 = 0.9870$; $\times 41.667 = 41.12$; $\times 2.56 \times 10^{-6} = 1.053 \times 10^{-4}$.

$$T_f = \frac{1.053 \times 10^{-4}}{6 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.755 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{f,Petroff} \approx 1.76 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(7 marks — full derivation = 3, calculation = 4)**

#### Part (b) — Sommerfeld Number and Raimondi-Boyd (7 Marks)

**Sommerfeld number:**

$$S_o = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} = (666.67)^2 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-7} = 4.444 \times 10^5 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$S_o = 0.0741$$

**Raimondi-Boyd chart reading** (interpolating from the table in Section 3.4):

For $S_o = 0.0741$ (between 0.01 and 0.1, closer to 0.1):
- $\varepsilon \approx 0.96$ (bearing is highly eccentric, nearly concentric)
- $f_{loaded}/f_{Petroff} \approx 0.30$ → $f_{loaded} \approx 0.30 \times 0.00220 = 6.6 \times 10^{-4}$
- $h_{min}/c \approx 0.04$ → $h_{min} = 0.04 \times 6 \times 10^{-5} = 2.4 \times 10^{-6}$ m = 2.4 μm

**Safety comment:** With $h_{min} = 2.4$ μm, the bearing is operating in the **mixed lubrication regime** (full-film requires $h_{min} > 3$–5 μm typically). Surface roughness of bearing materials (typically 0.2–0.8 μm Ra) suggests the peaks of the journal and bearing are *intermittently* contacting. This is acceptable for a modern engine bearing using a babbitt (soft) lining, but is on the borderline of safe operation. The designer should either:
- Increase the clearance to $c = 0.08$ mm (raises $S_o$ → less eccentric, thicker film), or
- Use a higher-viscosity oil (e.g., 0.035 Pa·s) at operating temperature.

**(7 marks — So calculation = 2, chart reading = 3, comment = 2)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE: Hydrodynamic Pressure Buildup & Performance

**(a)** Sketch and explain the pressure distribution in a hydrodynamic journal bearing. Indicate the locations of the maximum pressure, the divergent-region cavitation zone, and the minimum film thickness on your sketch.

**(b)** A bearing has the following parameters: $D = 100$ mm, $L = 50$ mm, $c = 0.075$ mm, $W = 30$ kN, $N = 1800$ rpm, $\mu = 0.018$ Pa·s. Calculate:
- (i) The Sommerfeld number
- (ii) The Petroff coefficient of friction
- (iii) The projected area and specific pressure
- (iv) The Petroff friction power loss (in watts)

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Pressure Distribution Sketch and Explanation (7 Marks)

**Sketch** (to be drawn on the exam paper — text description here):

A polar plot of pressure $p$ versus angular position $\theta$ around the bearing circumference. The pressure curve:
- Starts at zero at $\theta = -\theta_0$ (the leading edge of the high-pressure zone)
- Rises rapidly as the gap converges
- Reaches a peak at $\theta = \theta_0$ (typically 30°–60° ahead of the position of minimum film thickness)
- Falls to zero at the cavitation boundary
- Remains at zero (or atmospheric) from the cavitation boundary to the leading edge of the next cycle

**Locations:**
- **Maximum pressure** at $\theta_0$ (ahead of the line of closest approach)
- **Minimum film thickness** at $\theta = 180°$ from the load line (where the journal is closest to the bearing)
- **Cavitation zone** in the divergent region (where $dp/d\theta > 0$ would require negative pressures, which the oil cannot sustain — dissolved gases come out of solution)

**Physical explanation:** The wedge of oil being dragged into the convergent gap builds pressure (like compressing a spring). Once the gap starts diverging, the pressure would have to go negative to maintain the flow, but the oil cavitates instead, limiting $p$ to zero. This is the **half-Sommerfeld boundary condition** of Reynolds' equation.

**(7 marks — sketch quality = 3, location identification = 2, physical explanation = 2)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 0.100$ m, $r = 0.050$ m
- $L = 0.050$ m
- $c = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $W = 30\,000$ N
- $N = 1800/60 = 30$ rev/s
- $\mu = 0.018$ Pa·s

**(i) Sommerfeld number:**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{30\,000}{0.050 \times 0.100} = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 6 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.050}{7.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 666.67$$

$$S_o = (666.67)^2 \cdot \frac{0.018 \times 30}{6 \times 10^6} = 4.444 \times 10^5 \cdot \frac{0.54}{6 \times 10^6}$$

$$S_o = 4.444 \times 10^5 \times 9 \times 10^{-8} = 0.0400$$

$$\boxed{S_o = 0.040}$$

**(ii) Petroff's coefficient of friction:**

$$f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{0.018 \times 30}{6 \times 10^6} \cdot 666.67$$

$$\frac{\mu N}{P} = \frac{0.54}{6 \times 10^6} = 9 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$f = 2 \times 9.8696 \times 9 \times 10^{-8} \times 666.67 = 2 \times 9.8696 \times 6 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$f = 19.739 \times 6 \times 10^{-5} = 1.184 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{f_{Petroff} \approx 0.00118}$$

**(iii) Projected area and specific pressure:**

$$A_p = L \times D = 0.050 \times 0.100 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 50 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$\boxed{A_p = 50 \text{ cm}^2, \quad P = 6 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(iv) Petroff friction power loss:**

First, the friction force:

$$F_f = f \cdot W = 1.184 \times 10^{-3} \times 30\,000 = 35.52 \text{ N}$$

The journal surface velocity:

$$U = 2\pi N r = 2\pi \times 30 \times 0.050 = 9.425 \text{ m/s}$$

The friction power:

$$P_f = F_f \cdot U = 35.52 \times 9.425 = 334.8 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_f \approx 335 \text{ W}}$$

**(7 marks — 1.5 per sub-part, plus 1 for cross-check of units)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in KTU 2024 Plain Bearing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $c$ and $C_d$** (radial vs. diametral clearance). Worth 1 mark lost per occurrence. **Always state your definition at the start of the solution.**
>
> 2. **Using $W/(2rL)$ vs. $W/(LD)$** for bearing pressure. Petroff uses $2rL$ (cylindrical surface area); modern bearing design uses $LD$ (projected area). The exam will specify which; if not, default to projected area $LD$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rev/s** in the Sommerfeld number formula. A factor-of-60 error is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
>
> 4. **Treating Petroff's $f$ as the actual friction coefficient.** Petroff gives the *concentric, isoviscous* upper bound. The actual friction is **lower** by a factor that depends on $S_o$. Always state this distinction.
>
> 5. **No sketch of pressure distribution** = 2–3 marks lost on Part (a) questions. Even a hand-drawn crude curve showing the wedge, the peak, and the cavitation zone is sufficient.
>
> 6. **Not closing the thermal loop.** In a 14-mark design problem, the final answer should include a thermal-equilibrium check: $\Delta T = fWV/(Q \rho C_p) \le \Delta T_{allowable}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [ ] A **plain bearing** supports load through a self-generated hydrodynamic oil film; no rolling elements, no external pump.
- [ ] **Petroff's equation** applies only to a *concentric, isoviscous* journal: $T_f = 4\pi^2 \mu N L r^3 / c$ and $f = 2\pi^2 (\mu N / P)(r/c)$.
- [ ] **Sommerfeld number** $S_o = (r/c)^2 (\mu N / P)$ is the **master dimensionless group** that determines bearing behaviour.
- [ ] **Raimondi-Boyd charts** relate $S_o$ to: $\varepsilon$, $h_{min}/c$, $\theta_0$ (peak location), $f/(r/c)$, $Q/(rc\omega L)$, and $Q_s/Q$.
- [ ] **Minimum film thickness** $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ — the *most critical* design variable; must exceed the combined surface roughness $R_a$.
- [ ] **Specific bearing pressure** $P = W/(LD)$ is the modern convention for engine bearings.
- [ ] **Cavitation** occurs in the divergent wedge; Reynolds' equation uses the **half-Sommerfeld** boundary condition to handle it.
- [ ] **L/D ratio** typically 0.25 – 1.0; short bearings (L/D < 0.5) use the short-bearing approximation, long bearings (L/D > 1) use the long-bearing approximation, intermediate use the full 2D Reynolds equation.
- [ ] **Thermal equilibrium** requires $H_{generated} = H_{dissipated}$: the iterative loop $T_b \to \mu(T_b) \to S_o \to f \to H_g \to T_b$ must converge.
- [ ] **Petroff over-predicts** friction for real (eccentric) bearings by a factor of 2 to 5 — it is a *safe upper bound*, not the design value.
- [ ] **Typical automotive values:** $S_o$ = 0.1 – 5; $\varepsilon$ = 0.6 – 0.9; $h_{min}$ = 2 – 25 μm; $P$ = 10 – 30 MPa; oil supply temperature = 80 – 110 °C.
- [ ] **Modern bearing materials:** babbitt (tin/lead-based, soft, embeddability), aluminum-tin, copper-lead, polymer overlays — selected to survive boundary lubrication and start-stop conditions.
- [ ] **Thrust bearings** differ from journal bearings: load is axial, film is generated by tapered lands or tilting pads.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrostatic Thrust Bearing - Design, Application, and Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT831 - ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hydrostatic Thrust Bearing - Design, Application, and Selection # 🛢️ SECTION 1: Hydrostatic Thrust Bearing — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Hydrostatic Thrust Bearing** — A thrust bearing in which the load-carrying lubricant film is established by an **external pressurized fluid supply** (pump or accumulator) forced through a restrictor into a recess (pocket) in the bearing pad, rather than being generated by the relative motion of the bearing surfaces themselves. In a hydrostatic thrust bearing, the lubricant film is maintained even when the shaft is **stationary**, because the fluid pressure required to support the axial load is supplied **externally**. The fluid is fed into a machined **recess (pocket)** in the bearing pad, then leaks out across a narrow **land (step)** into a surrounding drain groove or the bearing edge. The pressure drop across the land creates a force that balances the applied axial load. **Distinguishing Feature:** Unlike hydrodynamic bearings, hydrostatic bearings do **not** require relative motion to build pressure — they can carry full load at **zero speed** with extremely low friction (≈ frictionless start). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic falls under the design of externally pressurized bearings. The student is expected to derive the load capacity, flow rate, and pumping power equations, and to identify suitable industrial applications and selection criteria. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation **Real-world analogy — "Floating a heavy table on a water cushion":** Imagine you want to support a **1000 kg marble tabletop** without any mechanical contact with the floor. You can't use rollers because you'd need to keep pushing them. What if you drilled small holes in the tabletop and pumped high-pressure water upward through them? The water would form a thin, pressurized film between the table and the floor, lifting it up just like a hovercraft. This is exactly how a **hydrostatic thrust bearing** works: - The **pump** = your water source (external pressurizer). - The **recess** = the small drilled pocket. - The **land** = the narrow gap through which water leaks out sideways. - The **load** (W) = the weight of the table pushing down. - The **film thickness (h)** = how high the table floats. Because the water is always being pumped in, the table can be **parked** (no rotation) and still float. As soon as you stop the pump, the table sinks — proving the load-carrying ability comes from **external pressure**, not from motion. **Another analogy:** A car on a **hydraulic lift** at a service station — even when the engine is off, the lift holds the car. The hydraulic fluid pressure does all the work. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | |---|---|---| | Lubricant viscosity | $\mu$ | **0.01 – 0.1 Pa·s** (ISO VG 32–100) | | Supply pressure | $P_s$ | **5 – 35 MPa** | | Recess pressure | $P_r$ | **0.3 $P_s$ – 0.6 $P_s$** (with capillary/orifice restrictor) | | Atmospheric/drain pressure | $P_a$ | **0.1 MPa** | | Film thickness | $h$ | **25 – 150 µm** | | Rotational speed | $N$ | **0 – 5000 rpm** | | Load capacity | $W$ | **10² – 10⁶ N** | | Stiffness | $S$ | **10⁸ – 10¹⁰ N/m** | --- ## 1.4 Components of a Hydrostatic Thrust Bearing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory anatomical breakdown (Examiner frequently asks "sketch and label"):** 1. **Thrust collar (runner)** — the rotating disc attached to the shaft, providing the surface to be supported. 2. **Bearing pad(s)** — stationary shoes with a recessed pocket and surrounding land. 3. **Recess (pocket)** — machined depression where pressurized oil is delivered. 4. **Land (step)** — the narrow flat region surrounding the recess through which oil leaks out. 5. **Restrictor** — capillary tube, orifice, or constant-flow valve that meters the supply flow. 6. **Oil supply header / manifold** — distributes high-pressure oil from pump to each pad. 7. **Drain groove** — collects leaked oil and returns it to sump. 8. **Sealing gland** — prevents oil leakage along the shaft. The bearing may consist of a **single circular pad**, **multiple pads** (3, 4, 5, or 6 equally spaced), or a **tilting-pad arrangement** for higher stiffness. --- ## 1.5 Classification of Hydrostatic Thrust Bearings | Type | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Circular flat recess bearing** | Simple disc with one circular pocket | Light-to-medium loads | | **Annular recess bearing** | Ring-shaped pocket | Uniform loading | | **Multi-recess bearing** | Multiple pockets, each fed through separate restrictors | Very high stiffness | | **Sector pad bearing** | Pie-shaped pads | Large thrust collars (turbines) | | **Tilting-pad hydrostatic bearing** | Pad pivots on a spherical seat | Self-aligning, very high precision | | **Step bearing** | Flat thrust with single radial step | Most common geometry | # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Pressurized oil supply:** A high-pressure pump delivers lubricant at pressure $P_s$ to the bearing recess through a restrictor. 2. **Recess pressurization:** The restrictor causes a pressure drop; oil enters the recess at a reduced pressure $P_r$ ($P_r < P_s$). 3. **Film formation:** Oil flows radially outward across the narrow land of thickness $h$ between the recess and the bearing edge. 4. **Pressure drop across land:** Due to viscous resistance, pressure drops from $P_r$ at the recess edge to $P_a$ at the periphery. 5. **Load support:** The integral of pressure over the bearing surface (recess + land) produces an upward force $W$ that balances the applied load. 6. **Self-regulating stiffness:** If the load increases, the film thickness $h$ decreases slightly → land resistance increases → recess pressure $P_r$ rises (less leakage) → load capacity increases until equilibrium. 7. **Continuous leakage:** A small quantity $Q$ of oil continuously leaks out and returns to the sump; this must be replaced by the pump. --- ## 2.2 Why a Restrictor is Mandatory Without a restrictor, $P_r = P_s$, and the bearing would be **unstable** — increasing load would decrease the gap, which would *not* increase the pressure (because pressure is fixed by the pump). The bearing would collapse onto the runner. **With a restrictor:** - Load ↑ → film $h$ ↓ → land resistance ↑ → flow $Q$ ↓ → pressure drop across restrictor ↓ → $P_r$ ↑ → load capacity ↑ → equilibrium restored. This is the **positive stiffness** mechanism that makes hydrostatic bearings exceptionally stiff. --- ## 2.3 Types of Restrictors | Restrictor Type | Flow–Pressure Relation | Stiffness Characteristic | |---|---|---| | **Capillary tube** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi d_c^4}{128 \mu L_c}(P_s - P_r)$ | Linear; moderate stiffness | | **Orifice (sharp-edged)** | $Q = C_d A_o \sqrt{2(P_s - P_r)/\rho}$ | Non-linear; high stiffness at low loads | | **Constant-flow valve** | $Q = $ constant regardless of $P_r$ | Theoretically **infinite stiffness** | | **Diaphragm valve** | $Q \propto (P_s - P_r)$ with compensation | Self-compensating, good stiffness | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the symbols, units, and the structural form.** ### Table 2.1 — Hydrostatic Thrust Bearing Master Formula Table | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Load capacity (general) | $W = P_r A_r + \int_{A_L} P \, dA$ | N | | 2 | Load capacity — single circular step bearing | $W = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\,(P_r - P_a)\,(R^2 - r_r^2)$ | N | | 3 | Recess pressure (capillary restrictor) | $P_r = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + \dfrac{6 \mu Q L_c}{\pi h^3 d_c^4/32} \cdot \dfrac{\ln(R/r_r)}{\pi}}$ → simplified via balance | Pa | | 4 | Pressure in land (radial) | $P(r) = P_r - \dfrac{6 \mu Q}{\pi h^3}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r}{r_r}\right)$ | Pa | | 5 | Volumetric flow rate (radial leakage) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi h^3}{6 \mu} \cdot \dfrac{P_r - P_a}{\ln(R/r_r)}$ | m³/s | | 6 | Pumping power | $H_p = P_s \cdot Q$ | W | | 7 | Friction power (rotating, Couette component) | $H_f = \dfrac{\mu \omega^2}{2}\!\left[\,\dfrac{R^4 - r_r^4}{h}\right]\pi$ | W | | 8 | Total power loss | $H_{total} = H_p + H_f$ | W | | 9 | Bearing stiffness | $S = \dfrac{dW}{dh}$ | N/m | | 10 | Friction torque | $T_f = \dfrac{\mu \omega \pi}{2h}\,(R^4 - r_r^4)$ | N·m | | 11 | Film parameter (Hamrock-Dowson) | $U = \dfrac{\mu \omega R}{P_r}$ | dim'less | | 12 | Optimum recess ratio (single pad) | $r_r / R \approx 0.5$ | – | > **Notation:** > $R$ = outer radius of bearing pad > $r_r$ = radius of recess > $h$ = film thickness over land > $P_s$ = supply pressure, $P_r$ = recess pressure, $P_a$ = ambient pressure > $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $\omega$ = angular velocity > $d_c, L_c$ = capillary diameter & length, $A_o$ = orifice area > $C_d$ = discharge coefficient, $\rho$ = oil density > $A_r$ = recess area, $A_L$ = land area > $Q$ = volumetric leakage flow --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Machine tool spindles** (lathes, milling, grinders) — infinite resolution at low speeds, high stiffness for precision machining. - **Turbine thrust bearings** — backup support, especially at low RPM start-up. - **Heavy rolling mills** — support massive roll-separating forces. - **Gyroscopes & inertial platforms** — near-zero friction, no stick-slip. - **Hydrostatic lift systems** — elevators, vehicle lifts, aircraft launching catapults. - **Precision measuring machines** — coordinate measuring machines (CMM), surface plates. - **Large telescopes & antennae** — hydrostatic pads for altitude-azimuth mounts. --- ## 2.6 Design Constraints & Operating Limits - **Maximum temperature:** Recess oil must stay below **120 °C** to maintain viscosity and prevent oxidation. - **Minimum film thickness:** $h \geq 25\,\mu m$ to prevent asperity contact (roughness of ground surfaces is typically 0.2–0.8 µm). - **Recess ratio:** For optimal load capacity and minimum flow, $r_r / R \approx 0.5$ for single circular pads. - **Stiffness requirement:** For machine tools, $S \geq 10^{9}$ N/m is common. - **Laminar flow assumption:** Reynolds number in the land $Re = \rho U h / \mu < 1000$ (turbulence causes instability). # 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Pressure Distribution in the Land Region **Assumptions:** - Steady-state, incompressible, Newtonian flow. - Laminar flow in the radial land. - Constant film thickness $h$ over the land. - Body forces (centrifugal) neglected for thin films and moderate speeds. - The bearing pad is stationary; the runner rotates at $\omega$. - The recess pressure $P_r$ is uniform across the recess (high supply flow). **Starting point — Modified Reynolds Equation (Hydrostatic, no squeeze, no sliding stretch, negligible inertia):** For a squeeze-free, pure hydrostatic bearing with no rotation: $$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r h^3 \frac{dP}{dr}\right) = 0$$ For constant $h$: $$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \frac{dP}{dr}\right) = 0$$ **Step 1 — First integration:** $$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \frac{dP}{dr}\right) = 0 \implies r \frac{dP}{dr} = C_1$$ **Step 2 — Separation of variables:** $$\frac{dP}{dr} = \frac{C_1}{r}$$ **Step 3 — Second integration:** $$P(r) = C_1 \ln r + C_2$$ **Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions:** At $r = r_r$: $P = P_r$ At $r = R$: $P = P_a$ $$P_r = C_1 \ln r_r + C_2$$ $$P_a = C_1 \ln R + C_2$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the constants:** Subtracting: $$P_r - P_a = C_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{r_r}{R}\right) = -C_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_r}\right)$$ $$\boxed{C_1 = -\frac{P_r - P_a}{\ln(R / r_r)}}$$ **Step 6 — Final pressure distribution:** Substitute back: $$P(r) = P_r - \frac{(P_r - P_a)}{\ln(R / r_r)} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_r}\right)$$ **Physical interpretation:** Pressure falls **logarithmically** with radius in the land, from $P_r$ at the recess edge to $P_a$ at the periphery. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Volumetric Flow Rate (Leakage) **Starting point — Flow rate through an annular slit at radius $r$:** For a thin film of thickness $h$ and width $2\pi r$, the volumetric flow (Poiseuille flow) per unit circumference is: $$q = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{dP}{dr}$$ **Step 1 — Total radial flow (constant for steady incompressible flow):** $$Q = 2\pi r \cdot q = -\frac{\pi r h^3}{6\mu}\frac{dP}{dr}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the pressure gradient from Section 3.1:** $$\frac{dP}{dr} = \frac{C_1}{r} = -\frac{P_r - P_a}{r \ln(R/r_r)}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $Q$:** $$Q = -\frac{\pi r h^3}{6\mu} \cdot \left[-\frac{P_r - P_a}{r \ln(R / r_r)}\right]$$ $$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi h^3}{6\mu} \cdot \frac{P_r - P_a}{\ln(R / r_r)}}$$ **This is the key KTU formula for leakage flow.** --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Load Capacity The total axial load supported equals the integral of pressure over the **recess** (uniform pressure $P_r$) **plus** the integral of pressure over the **land** (variable pressure $P(r)$): $$W = P_r \cdot \pi r_r^2 + \int_{r_r}^{R} P(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$ **Step 1 — Substitute the pressure distribution:** $$W = P_r \pi r_r^2 + 2\pi \int_{r_r}^{R}\!\left[P_r - \frac{(P_r - P_a)\ln(r/r_r)}{\ln(R/r_r)}\right] r \, dr$$ **Step 2 — Split the integral:** $$W = P_r \pi r_r^2 + 2\pi P_r \int_{r_r}^{R} r\,dr - \frac{2\pi(P_r - P_a)}{\ln(R/r_r)} \int_{r_r}^{R} r \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_r}\right) dr$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the first integral:** $$\int_{r_r}^{R} r\,dr = \frac{1}{2}(R^2 - r_r^2)$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the second integral** (use $\int r \ln(r/r_r)dr = \tfrac{1}{2}r^2 \ln(r/r_r) - \tfrac{1}{4}r^2$): $$\int_{r_r}^{R} r \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_r}\right) dr = \left[\frac{r^2}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_r}\right) - \frac{r^2}{4}\right]_{r_r}^{R}$$ $$= \frac{R^2}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_r}\right) - \frac{R^2}{4} - \left(0 - \frac{r_r^2}{4}\right)$$ $$= \frac{R^2}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_r}\right) - \frac{R^2}{4} + \frac{r_r^2}{4}$$ **Step 5 — Combine (after algebraic simplification):** After lengthy but straightforward algebra, the result reduces to: $$\boxed{W = \frac{\pi}{2}\,(P_r - P_a)\,(R^2 - r_r^2)}$$ > **NOTE on approximation:** This compact form is exact **only when the recess pressure $P_r$ acts on the entire recess** and the land pressure profile is logarithmic. It is the standard KTU formula used for single circular step bearings. --- ## 3.4 Derivation — Pumping Power The pump must continuously supply oil at pressure $P_s$ at flow rate $Q$. The hydraulic pumping power is: $$\boxed{H_p = P_s \cdot Q}$$ For a capillary-restricted system, $P_s$ is much greater than $P_r$ due to the restrictor drop: $$P_s = P_r + \Delta P_{restrictor} = P_r + \frac{128 \mu L_c Q}{\pi d_c^4}$$ Substituting the $Q$ from §3.2 into the pumping power gives the **complete pumping loss equation**, which KTU often asks in numerical problems. --- ## 3.5 Derivation — Friction Torque and Friction Power (Rotating Runner) The runner rotates at angular velocity $\omega$. The shear stress in the Newtonian film is: $$\tau = \mu \frac{\omega r}{h}$$ **Step 1 — Friction torque** (force × moment arm, integrated over the annular land): $$T_f = \int_{r_r}^{R} \tau \cdot (2\pi r\,dr) \cdot r = \frac{2\pi \mu \omega}{h}\int_{r_r}^{R} r^3 \, dr$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate:** $$T_f = \frac{2\pi \mu \omega}{h} \cdot \frac{R^4 - r_r^4}{4} = \frac{\pi \mu \omega}{2h}(R^4 - r_r^4)$$ **Step 3 — Friction power:** $$\boxed{H_f = T_f \cdot \omega = \frac{\pi \mu \omega^2}{2h}(R^4 - r_r^4)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison insight:** For a hydrostatic bearing at zero or low speed, $H_f \approx 0$ and the dominant loss is $H_p$. This is why hydrostatic bearings excel in **low-speed, high-load** applications. --- ## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Format) **Problem:** A hydrostatic thrust bearing has the following data: Outer radius $R = 75\,\text{mm}$, recess radius $r_r = 40\,\text{mm}$, film thickness $h = 0.1\,\text{mm}$, oil viscosity $\mu = 0.05\,\text{Pa·s}$, recess pressure $P_r = 4\,\text{MPa}$, ambient pressure $P_a = 0.1\,\text{MPa}$. **Find:** (a) Load capacity, (b) Leakage flow rate, (c) Pumping power (assuming $P_s = 6\,\text{MPa}$). **Solution (a) — Load capacity:** Using $W = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(P_r - P_a)(R^2 - r_r^2)$: $$W = \frac{\pi}{2}(4\times 10^6 - 0.1\times 10^6)\big[(0.075)^2 - (0.040)^2\big]$$ $$W = \frac{\pi}{2}(3.9\times 10^6)(5.625\times 10^{-3} - 1.6\times 10^{-3})$$ $$W = \frac{\pi}{2}(3.9\times 10^6)(4.025\times 10^{-3}) \approx 24{,}650\,\text{N} \approx \mathbf{24.65\,kN}$$ **Solution (b) — Leakage flow rate:** $$Q = \frac{\pi h^3}{6\mu} \cdot \frac{P_r - P_a}{\ln(R/r_r)} = \frac{\pi (1\times 10^{-4})^3}{6(0.05)} \cdot \frac{3.9\times 10^6}{\ln(75/40)}$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \times 10^{-12}}{0.3} \cdot \frac{3.9\times 10^6}{0.6286} = 6.5\times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx \mathbf{6.5\,cm^3/s}$$ **Solution (c) — Pumping power:** $$H_p = P_s Q = (6\times 10^6)(6.5\times 10^{-6}) = \mathbf{39\,W}$$ --- ## 3.7 Design Optimization Equations | Design Goal | Governing Equation | Design Action | |---|---|---| | Maximum stiffness | $S_{opt} = \dfrac{3(P_s - P_a)\pi(R^2 - r_r^2)}{2h_0}\cdot f(r_r/R)$ | Choose $r_r/R \approx 0.5$, minimize $h_0$ | | Minimum flow | $Q_{min}$ at $r_r/R \approx 0.7$ | Increase recess size reduces flow | | Maximum load capacity | $W_{max}$ at $r_r/R \approx 0.5$ | Balanced optimum | For a single recess bearing with capillary restrictor, the **optimum load capacity** occurs when: $$\boxed{\frac{r_r}{R} \approx 0.5}$$ --- ## 3.8 Multi-Pad Bearing Load Summation For an $n$-pad bearing (typical $n = 3, 4, 6$), each pad is fed through an independent restrictor. The total load capacity is: $$W_{total} = n \cdot W_{pad}$$ Each pad must be designed such that the pressure distribution does not interfere with neighbors (pad angular spacing > $30°$). # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Hydrostatic Thrust Bearing Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[External Pump / Accumulator
Supply Pressure Ps] --> B[High-Pressure Manifold
Distribution Block] B --> C1[Restrictor 1
Capillary / Orifice] B --> C2[Restrictor 2
Capillary / Orifice] B --> C3[Restrictor n
Capillary / Orifice] C1 --> D1[Recess 1
Pressure Pr] C2 --> D2[Recess 2
Pressure Pr] C3 --> D3[Recess n
Pressure Pr] D1 --> E1[Land Region 1
Film Thickness h] D2 --> E2[Land Region 2
Film Thickness h] D3 --> E3[Land Region n
Film Thickness h] E1 --> F1[Drain Groove 1
Pressure Pa] E2 --> F2[Drain Groove 2
Pressure Pa] E3 --> F3[Drain Groove n
Pressure Pa] F1 --> G[Sump / Reservoir] F2 --> G F3 --> G G --> A H[Axial Load W] --> I[Thrust Collar Runner
Rotating Shaft] I -.Applies pressure to.-> D1 I -.Applies pressure to.-> D2 I -.Applies pressure to.-> D3 ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Selection Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Select Hydrostatic Thrust Bearing] --> B{Load Magnitude?} B -->|Light 10 N - 1 kN| C[Single Circular Pad
Capillary Restrictor] B -->|Medium 1 kN - 50 kN| D[Multi-Pad 3 or 4
Orifice Restrictor] B -->|Heavy 50 kN - 1 MN| E[Multi-Pad 6 or Sector
Constant-Flow Valve] C --> F{Speed Range?} D --> F E --> F F -->|0 - 100 rpm| G[Capillary OK
Low Friction Priority] F -->|100 - 5000 rpm| H[Orifice or Membrane
Balance Pump and Friction] F -->|5000 rpm| I[Tilting-Pad Hydrostatic
Thermal Management Critical] G --> J{Environment?} H --> J I --> J J -->|Precision / Clean Room| K[Recessed-Pad with Sealed Drain] J -->|Heavy Industry| L[Open Drain with Sump] K --> M[Final Selection] L --> M[Final Selection] ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Pressure Profile in the Land (Conceptual) ```mermaid graph LR A[Recess Center
Uniform Pressure Pr] -->|Constant across recess| B[Recess Edge r equals rr
Pressure Pr] B -->|Logarithmic Drop| C[Mid Land r equals R plus rr over 2
Pressure Pr minus delta] C -->|Logarithmic Drop| D[Bearing Edge r equals R
Pressure Pa] style A fill:#fff4cc style B fill:#ffd966 style C fill:#ffe599 style D fill:#cccccc ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Failure Modes Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hydrostatic Thrust Bearing Failure Modes] --> B[Thermal Failure
Oil Overheating 120 C] A --> C[Mechanical Failure
Asperity Contact h below 25 micron] A --> D[Hydraulic Failure
Pump Failure or Restrictor Blockage] A --> E[Cavitation
Pressure below Vapor Pressure at Drain] B --> F[Solution: Cooler / Better Viscosity Grade] C --> G[Solution: Increase h0 or Improve Surface Finish] D --> H[Solution: Redundant Pump / Filter] E --> I[Solution: Increase Pa or Add Pressurized Drain] ``` # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define a hydrostatic thrust bearing. Mention any two advantages over hydrodynamic bearings.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A hydrostatic thrust bearing is an externally pressurized bearing in which the load-supporting lubricant film is generated by an external pump supplying oil at high pressure through a restrictor into a recess in the bearing pad. *(1 mark)* > **Advantages over hydrodynamic bearings:** > 1. **Zero-speed load capacity** — can support full load even when the shaft is stationary, since pressure is supplied externally. *(1 mark)* > 2. **Very low starting friction** — no stick-slip; ideal for precision machinery. *(1 mark)* > 3. *(Optional additional point)* **Higher stiffness and damping** — system stiffness can be tuned via restrictor selection. > 4. *(Optional)* **Long life** — full-film lubrication eliminates wear. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Differentiate between hydrostatic and hydrodynamic thrust bearings (any four points).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Sl. | Hydrostatic Thrust Bearing | Hydrodynamic Thrust Bearing | |---|---|---| | 1 | Load is supported by **externally pressurized** fluid | Load is supported by fluid pressure generated by **relative motion** | | 2 | Can carry load at **zero speed** | Cannot carry load at zero speed (needs minimum speed) | | 3 | Requires an **external pump** and **restrictor** | Self-contained; only sump and rotating shaft needed | | 4 | Friction is **very low** at all speeds | Friction is high at low speeds; minimum near design speed | | 5 | Power loss = pumping power + viscous friction | Power loss = viscous friction only | | 6 | **Stiffness** tunable via restrictor | Stiffness depends on film geometry and speed | *(Any 4 points × 0.75 = 3 marks)* --- ## 🅱️ PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** A hydrostatic thrust bearing has the following specifications: Outer radius $R = 100\,\text{mm}$, recess radius $r_r = 50\,\text{mm}$, film thickness $h = 0.15\,\text{mm}$, oil viscosity $\mu = 0.04\,\text{Pa·s}$, recess pressure $P_r = 5\,\text{MPa}$, ambient $P_a = 0.1\,\text{MPa}$, and supply pressure $P_s = 8\,\text{MPa}$. Calculate: 1. Load capacity $W$ 2. Leakage flow rate $Q$ 3. Pumping power $H_p$ 4. Friction torque if the shaft rotates at $N = 600\,\text{rpm}$ **(b)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a **multi-recess hydrostatic thrust bearing**. What are the advantages of using multiple recesses over a single recess? --- #### ✅ Solution A(a) — Step-by-Step (7 marks) **Given:** $R = 0.1\,\text{m}$, $r_r = 0.05\,\text{m}$, $h = 1.5\times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$, $\mu = 0.04\,\text{Pa·s}$, $P_r = 5\times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$, $P_a = 0.1\times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$, $P_s = 8\times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$, $N = 600\,\text{rpm}$, so $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi(600)}{60} = 62.83\,\text{rad/s}$. **Part 1 — Load Capacity (2 marks):** $$W = \frac{\pi}{2}(P_r - P_a)(R^2 - r_r^2)$$ $$W = \frac{\pi}{2}(5 - 0.1)\times 10^{6}\left[(0.1)^2 - (0.05)^2\right]$$ $$W = \frac{\pi}{2}(4.9\times 10^{6})(0.01 - 0.0025) = \frac{\pi}{2}(4.9\times 10^{6})(7.5\times 10^{-3})$$ $$\boxed{W = 57{,}730\,\text{N} \approx 57.73\,\text{kN}}$$ **Part 2 — Leakage Flow Rate (2 marks):** $$Q = \frac{\pi h^3}{6\mu} \cdot \frac{P_r - P_a}{\ln(R/r_r)}$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi (1.5\times 10^{-4})^3}{6(0.04)} \cdot \frac{4.9\times 10^{6}}{\ln(0.1/0.05)}$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \times 3.375\times 10^{-12}}{0.24} \cdot \frac{4.9\times 10^{6}}{\ln 2} = (4.416\times 10^{-11})\cdot \frac{4.9\times 10^{6}}{0.6931}$$ $$Q = (4.416\times 10^{-11})(7.07\times 10^{6}) = 3.12\times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 312\,\text{cm}^3/\text{s} \approx 18.7\,\text{L/min}}$$ **Part 3 — Pumping Power (1.5 marks):** $$H_p = P_s \cdot Q = (8\times 10^{6})(3.12\times 10^{-4}) = 2496\,\text{W}$$ $$\boxed{H_p \approx 2.5\,\text{kW}}$$ **Part 4 — Friction Torque (1.5 marks):** $$T_f = \frac{\pi \mu \omega}{2h}(R^4 - r_r^4)$$ $$T_f = \frac{\pi (0.04)(62.83)}{2(1.5\times 10^{-4})}\left[(0.1)^4 - (0.05)^4\right]$$ $$T_f = \frac{\pi (0.04)(62.83)}{3\times 10^{-4}}(1\times 10^{-4} - 6.25\times 10^{-6})$$ $$T_f = 26{,}320 \times 9.375\times 10^{-5} = 2.47\,\text{N·m}$$ $$\boxed{T_f \approx 2.47\,\text{N·m}}$$ **Note:** Friction power $H_f = T_f \cdot \omega = 2.47 \times 62.83 = 155\,\text{W}$, which is much smaller than $H_p$, confirming that pumping loss dominates at low speeds. --- #### ✅ Solution A(b) — Multi-Recess Hydrostatic Thrust Bearing (7 marks) **Construction (3 marks):** - A multi-recess hydrostatic thrust bearing consists of a circular thrust pad divided into **3, 4, 6, or more pockets** arranged symmetrically around the center. - Each pocket is connected to the high-pressure oil supply through its **own individual restrictor** (typically a capillary tube or orifice). - The pockets are separated by **land regions** of width $b$ over which oil leaks radially outward to a peripheral drain groove. - A separate **drain** returns the leaked oil to the sump. **Working (2 marks):** - High-pressure oil enters each pocket through its restrictor, creating a recess pressure $P_r$. - The pressure drops logarithmically across the surrounding land, generating load. - Each pocket acts as an independent load-supporting element; the recess pressures may differ slightly depending on local film thickness (self-compensating). **Advantages of multi-recess over single-recess (2 marks):** 1. **Higher static stiffness** — each pocket acts as an independent spring, and the overall stiffness is the sum. 2. **Greater load capacity** — total load is the sum of all pocket contributions. 3. **Better load distribution** — accommodates misalignment and uneven loading. 4. **Improved damping** — fluid squeeze in multiple pockets dissipates vibration. --- ### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary, 2024]` **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive the expression for the **load capacity** of a circular stepped hydrostatic thrust bearing. State clearly the assumptions used. **(b)** Discuss the **selection criteria** for hydrostatic thrust bearings in machine tool spindles, with emphasis on load, speed, stiffness, and lubrication requirements. --- #### ✅ Solution B(a) — Derivation (7 marks) **Sketch description (1 mark):** A circular pad of outer radius $R$ with a central circular recess of radius $r_r$. The film thickness over the land is $h$ (constant). Recess pressure is $P_r$ (uniform), peripheral pressure is $P_a$. **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Steady, laminar, incompressible Newtonian flow. 2. Constant film thickness $h$ over the land. 3. No body forces, no inertia, no centrifugal effects. 4. Recess pressure $P_r$ is uniform (high supply flow rate). 5. The bearing is axisymmetric. **Derivation (5 marks):** Starting from the Reynolds equation in cylindrical coordinates for pure radial hydrostatic flow: $$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dP}{dr}\right) = 0$$ First integration: $r\dfrac{dP}{dr} = C_1$ Second integration: $P(r) = C_1 \ln r + C_2$ Applying BCs: $P(r_r) = P_r$, $P(R) = P_a$: $$P(r) = P_r - \frac{(P_r - P_a)}{\ln(R/r_r)} \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_r}\right)$$ **Load capacity:** $$W = P_r \pi r_r^2 + 2\pi \int_{r_r}^{R} P(r) r\, dr$$ Carrying out the integration: $$W = P_r \pi r_r^2 + 2\pi\!\left[\frac{P_r(R^2 - r_r^2)}{2} - \frac{(P_r - P_a)}{\ln(R/r_r)} \cdot \left\{\frac{R^2}{2}\ln\!\frac{R}{r_r} - \frac{R^2 - r_r^2}{4}\right\}\right]$$ After algebraic simplification: $$\boxed{W = \frac{\pi}{2}(P_r - P_a)(R^2 - r_r^2)}$$ --- #### ✅ Solution B(b) — Selection Criteria (7 marks) | Criterion | Selection Guideline | |---|---| | **1. Load magnitude** | Single circular pad for $W \leq 10\,\text{kN}$; multi-pad (3–6 pads) for $10 < W \leq 100\,\text{kN}$; sector pads for $W > 100\,\text{kN}$. | | **2. Speed range** | At $N < 100\,\text{rpm}$ (machine tool spindles), hydrostatic is the natural choice; capillary restrictor preferred. At $N > 1000\,\text{rpm}$, add thermal management (oil cooler). | | **3. Stiffness requirement** | Precision grinding requires $S \geq 10^9\,\text{N/m}$. Use orifice or constant-flow restrictors; select recess ratio $r_r/R \approx 0.5$ for optimum stiffness. | | **4. Lubricant** | ISO VG 32 or 46 for spindles; viscosity must be stable over operating temperature. | | **5. Filtration** | Filter rating $\leq 10\,\mu\text{m}$ (absolute); cleanliness per NAS 1638 Class 6 or better. | | **6. Thermal** | Oil inlet temperature controlled to $40\pm 2\,^\circ\text{C}$; cooler sized for $1.5\times$ calculated $H_{total}$. | | **7. Material & surface** | Bearing pad: bronze or hardened steel; runner: hardened steel ($>$ 60 HRC); surface finish $\leq 0.4\,\mu\text{m Ra}$. | | **8. Safety** | Dual redundant pumps; pressure switches and flow sensors with alarm interlocks. | --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks):** > > 1. **Forgetting to subtract $P_a$** — Examiners frequently deduct **2 marks** when students write $W = \frac{\pi}{2} P_r (R^2 - r_r^2)$ instead of $\frac{\pi}{2}(P_r - P_a)(R^2 - r_r^2)$. The ambient pressure does NOT contribute to net load. > > 2. **Confusing $\ln(R/r_r)$ with $\log_{10}$** — The formula uses **natural logarithm** (base $e$). Many calculators default to base 10. > > 3. **Mixing up units of viscosity** — $\mu = 0.05\,\text{Pa·s}$ is **NOT** the same as $0.05\,\text{cP}$. $1\,\text{cP} = 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$. > > 4. **Using $P_s$ instead of $P_r$ in load formula** — Load capacity depends on **recess pressure**, not supply pressure. Use $P_s$ **only** for pumping power calculation. > > 5. **Neglecting units conversion** — $R$ and $r_r$ MUST be in metres for SI units to yield $W$ in Newtons. > > 6. **Skipping derivation steps** — In 7-mark derivation questions, showing intermediate integration steps is mandatory. **Each skipped integration step = −1 mark.** --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] Hydrostatic thrust bearing → load supported by **external pressurized fluid**, NOT by relative motion. - [x] **Recess pressure $P_r < $ supply pressure $P_s$** due to restrictor pressure drop. - [x] Restrictors are **mandatory** for positive stiffness — without them, the bearing is unstable. - [x] Four restrictor types: **capillary, orifice, constant-flow valve, diaphragm** — constant flow gives highest stiffness. - [x] Optimum **recess ratio $r_r / R \approx 0.5$** for a single circular pad bearing. - [x] Load capacity: $\boxed{W = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(P_r - P_a)(R^2 - r_r^2)}$ - [x] Leakage flow: $\boxed{Q = \dfrac{\pi h^3 (P_r - P_a)}{6\mu \ln(R/r_r)}}$ - [x] Pumping power: $\boxed{H_p = P_s Q}$ - [x] Friction torque (rotating runner): $\boxed{T_f = \dfrac{\pi \mu \omega (R^4 - r_r^4)}{2 h}}$ - [x] Pressure distribution in land is **logarithmic**, not linear. - [x] Total power loss = pumping power + friction power; at low speeds, $H_p$ dominates. - [x] Hydrostatic bearings can carry load at **zero speed** — main advantage over hydrodynamic. - [x] Applications: **machine tool spindles, turbines, rolling mills, CMMs, telescopes, gyroscopes**. - [x] Typical film thickness: **25 – 150 µm**; oil viscosity **0.01 – 0.1 Pa·s**. - [x] Filtration: **≤ 10 µm absolute**; cleanliness NAS 1638 Class 6 or better. - [x] Multi-recess bearings have **higher stiffness and load capacity** than single recess. - [x] Tilting-pad hydrostatic bearings are used for **self-alignment** under misaligned loads. - [x] Cavitation at the drain edge is prevented by maintaining $P_a$ above oil vapor pressure, or using **pressurized drain**. - [x] Always subtract **$P_a$ (ambient)** from $P_r$ when calculating load — examiners are strict.

topics.title: Nanomaterials - Synthesis: Physical and Chemical Methods
Characterization Techniques: X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and Scanning Tunnelling Microscopy (STM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT831 - ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characterization Techniques: X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and Scanning Tunnelling Microscopy (STM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Nano-Scale Characterization in Tribology

In the modern automotive industry, the quest for ultra-low friction, anti-wear coatings, and high-performance **nano-lubricants** has pushed material characterization far beyond traditional mechanical testing. Engine components — piston rings, cylinder liners, camshafts, ball bearings, and gearbox synchronizers — are increasingly coated with **nanocomposite films**, **DLC (Diamond-Like Carbon)** layers, and **MoS₂/WS₂ nanoparticles dispersed in base oils**. To validate the crystalline phase, grain size, surface morphology, and atomic arrangement of these engineered materials, four advanced characterization tools form the backbone of any tribology research laboratory:

1. **X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)** — for crystal phase and grain size
2. **Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** — for surface topography and micro-morphology
3. **Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)** — for internal lattice imaging and atomic-scale defects
4. **Scanning Tunnelling Microscopy (STM)** — for atomic-resolution surface mapping and electronic structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** These four techniques collectively cover the complete analytical workflow needed to characterize nano-lubricant additives (graphene, h-BN, MoS₂, TiO₂ nanoparticles) and tribological coatings. Expect theory questions on operating principle, schematic, resolution limits, and comparison among techniques.

---

## 1.2 X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)

### Formal Definition
**X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)** is a non-destructive analytical technique used to identify the crystalline phases, lattice parameters, crystallite size, and internal strain of materials by measuring the diffraction angles and intensities of X-rays scattered by the periodic atomic planes of the sample.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** XRD exploits the wave nature of X-rays (wavelength ≈ atomic spacing, ~0.5–2.5 Å) and their interaction with the regular, repeating atomic planes in a crystal. The output is a plot of **Intensity (I) vs. 2θ**, called a *diffractogram*, which acts as a unique "fingerprint" for every crystalline phase (matched against the **JCPDS-ICDD** database).

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Stadium Wave & The Crystal Lattice"
Imagine a packed cricket stadium where every row of spectators is sitting at perfectly equal spacing (the *crystal planes*). When a cheer (the *incident X-ray wave*) propagates across the crowd, every row simultaneously raises hands. If the timing is perfect (i.e., the spacing between rows equals an integer multiple of the wave's wavelength), the applause echoes back constructively — a loud "diffracted echo." This constructive echo is what the XRD detector records. If the rows are unevenly spaced (an *amorphous material*), no echo returns — only noise. This is exactly why **amorphous materials produce broad humps while crystalline materials produce sharp peaks** in XRD.

---

## 1.3 Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)

### Formal Definition
**Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** is a high-resolution surface imaging technique that uses a focused beam of accelerated electrons (typically 1–30 kV) raster-scanned across the specimen surface, producing topographical and compositional images by detecting **secondary electrons (SE)**, **backscattered electrons (BSE)**, and **characteristic X-rays (EDX/EDS)** emitted from the interaction volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** SEM provides magnifications from **10× to 500,000×** with a resolution of **~1–10 nm**, far surpassing optical microscopy's diffraction limit (~200 nm). Its large **depth of field** makes it ideal for examining rough tribological surfaces — wear scars, scuffing marks, abrasive grooves, and the morphology of nanoparticle agglomerates in used engine oil.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Flashlight on a Mountain at Night"
Think of shining a high-powered flashlight onto a rugged mountain at night. As you sweep the beam across ridges, valleys, and peaks, the reflected light lets you build a detailed 3-D mental map of the topography. SEM does the same — but instead of photons it uses **electrons** (which have a wavelength 100,000× shorter than visible light, ~0.005 nm at 30 kV), and the "echoes" come back as secondary electrons knocked off the surface atoms. The rocky mountain is your **wear scar on a piston ring**.

---

## 1.4 Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)

### Formal Definition
**Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)** is the highest-resolution imaging technique available, in which a beam of high-energy electrons (60–300 kV, wavelength ≈ 0.001–0.004 nm) is transmitted through an ultra-thin specimen (<100 nm) to produce both a real-space magnified image and a reciprocal-space diffraction pattern, enabling direct observation of atomic structure, dislocations, grain boundaries, and nano-precipitates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** TEM achieves **atomic-scale resolution (<0.1 nm)** and magnifications up to **~50,000,000×**. It is the only technique that allows direct visualization of individual crystal lattice planes, stacking faults, and the internal structure of a single nanoparticle (e.g., a 5 nm MoS₂ sheet used as a lubricant additive).

### Intuitive Analogy — "X-Ray Vision Through a Frosted Glass Window"
Imagine you have a piece of frosted glass and you want to see the tiny air bubbles trapped inside it. You slice it paper-thin and hold it up to a powerful backlight — you can clearly see each bubble, its shape, and its arrangement. TEM operates on the same principle: the specimen is sliced to **<100 nm thinness** (using an *ultra-microtome* or *ion-beam milling*) and electrons are passed *through* it. The transmitted electrons form a shadow image revealing the internal architecture — dislocations, grain boundaries, and lattice fringes — of the material.

---

## 1.5 Scanning Tunnelling Microscopy (STM)

### Formal Definition
**Scanning Tunnelling Microscopy (STM)** is a quantum-mechanical imaging technique that uses a bias-voltage-induced **tunnelling current** flowing between an atomically sharp metallic tip and a conducting sample surface (separated by a vacuum gap of ~0.3–1 nm) to map the topography and local density of electronic states with **sub-Å vertical resolution** and **atomic lateral resolution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** Invented by **Gerd Binnig and Heinrich Rohrer in 1981** (Nobel Prize in Physics 1986), STM exploits the **quantum tunnelling effect**, in which electrons have a finite probability to traverse a classically forbidden potential barrier. The exponential dependence of tunnelling current on tip-sample distance provides extreme sensitivity (~1% change in current per 0.01 nm change in gap). **Pre-requisite:** Sample must be electrically conducting or semi-conducting.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Whispering Through a Wall"
Imagine you and your friend are standing on either side of a thick concrete wall. Classically, your whispers cannot pass through. But quantum-mechanically, there's a tiny probability your voice "tunnels" through and reaches your friend's ear. If your friend moves closer (even by a millimeter), the whisper becomes dramatically louder. STM works identically: a quantum "whisper" (tunnelling electrons) flows between tip and sample. As the tip scans across atomic bumps and valleys, the tunnelling current changes exponentially, allowing reconstruction of the **atomic-scale 3-D topography** of the surface.

---

## 1.6 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Mandatory Bold Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants for KTU Calculations:**
> - **Speed of light (c): 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - **Planck's constant (h): 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - **Wavelength of Cu Kα X-ray: λ = 1.5406 Å** (most commonly used in XRD)
> - **Wavelength of Mo Kα X-ray: λ = 0.7107 Å**
> - **Acceleration voltage in SEM: 1–30 kV** (typical 5–20 kV)
> - **Acceleration voltage in TEM: 60–300 kV**
> - **STM tunnelling current: 0.1–10 nA**
> - **STM tip-sample gap: 0.3–1.0 nm**
> - **Electron charge (e): 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - **Electron mass (mₑ): 9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg**

---

## 1.7 Comparative Overview of the Four Techniques

| Property | XRD | SEM | TEM | STM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Probe used** | X-ray photons | Electron beam (scanned) | Electron beam (transmitted) | Tunnelling electrons |
| **Probe wavelength** | 1.5406 Å (Cu Kα) | ~0.0086 nm @ 20 kV | ~0.0017 nm @ 300 kV | Quantum wavefunction |
| **Resolution** | Bulk averaged (~μm³) | 1–10 nm | <0.1 nm (atomic) | 0.01 nm (vertical), 0.1 nm (lateral) |
| **Magnification** | Not applicable | 10× to 500,000× | 1,000× to 50,000,000× | Atomic-scale direct imaging |
| **Sample type** | Crystalline powder/bulk | Solid (conductive/non-conductive) | Ultra-thin foil (<100 nm) | Conducting/semi-conducting surface |
| **Information obtained** | Phase, lattice, grain size | Surface morphology, topography | Internal structure, defects | Atomic topography, electronic states |
| **Environment** | Ambient | High vacuum | High vacuum | UHV or ambient |
| **Key equation** | Bragg's Law: nλ = 2d sin θ | – | de Broglie: λ = h/p | I ∝ e^(–κd) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) — Deep Theory

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
A beam of monochromatic X-rays (produced by bombarding a copper target with high-energy electrons) is directed at the sample. The X-rays interact with the **periodic electron density** of the crystal lattice. Each set of atomic planes (denoted by Miller indices hkl) acts as a partial mirror, reflecting X-rays. Constructive interference occurs only when the path difference between reflections from successive planes equals an integer multiple of the wavelength.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of XRD Operation
- **Step 1:** X-ray tube generates Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) by electron bombardment of a copper anode.
- **Step 2:** The X-ray beam is collimated by slits and directed onto the flat sample surface.
- **Step 3:** The sample is rotated by angle θ, while the detector is rotated by 2θ (Bragg–Brentano geometry).
- **Step 4:** At specific angles satisfying **Bragg's Law**, constructive interference occurs and a peak is recorded.
- **Step 5:** The plot of Intensity vs. 2θ is the *diffractogram*.
- **Step 6:** Peak positions are matched to the **JCPDS/ICDD powder diffraction database** for phase identification.

### 2.1.3 The Bragg's Law Equation

Bragg's Law governs the condition for constructive interference:

$$n\lambda = 2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta$$

where:
- $n$ = order of diffraction (usually = 1)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of incident X-ray
- $d_{hkl}$ = interplanar spacing of (hkl) planes
- $\theta$ = angle of incidence (Bragg angle)

### 2.1.4 The Scherrer Equation (Grain Size)

The broadening of an XRD peak is inversely proportional to the crystallite size, as described by the Scherrer equation:

$$D = \frac{K \lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}$$

where:
- $D$ = average crystallite size (nm)
- $K$ = Scherrer constant (shape factor, typically 0.9 for spherical crystallites)
- $\lambda$ = X-ray wavelength
- $\beta$ = full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the peak in **radians**
- $\theta$ = Bragg angle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters in Tribology:** The Scherrer equation allows estimation of the grain size of nano-additives (e.g., CuO nanoparticles added to engine oil) directly from the FWHM of their XRD peaks. This avoids the need for direct electron microscopy and provides statistically averaged information over millions of crystallites.

### 2.1.5 Williamson-Hall Equation (Strain + Size Broadening)

Total peak broadening $\beta_{total}$ has contributions from crystallite size and microstrain:

$$\beta_{total} \cos\theta = \frac{K \lambda}{D} + 4 \varepsilon \sin\theta$$

A plot of $\beta_{total}\cos\theta$ vs. $\sin\theta$ gives a straight line: slope = microstrain $\varepsilon$, intercept = $K\lambda/D$.

### 2.1.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of XRD
- Phase purity verification of commercial MoS₂ and WS₂ nano-lubricants
- Identification of oxide phases (Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄) on worn tribo-surfaces
- Determination of residual stress in DLC coatings
- Crystallinity assessment of polymer nanocomposite gears

---

## 2.2 Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) — Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle
A field-emission or thermionic electron gun emits electrons, which are accelerated (1–30 kV) and demagnified by a system of **electromagnetic lenses** (condenser and objective) into a fine probe (spot size ~1–10 nm). Scan coils deflect this probe in a **raster pattern** across the sample. The interactions generate signals (SE, BSE, X-rays) collected by detectors and synchronized to build a 2-D image pixel by pixel.

### 2.2.2 Signal Types Generated in SEM
- **Secondary Electrons (SE):** Low energy (<50 eV), generated from surface layers (~5–50 nm depth) → provides **topographic contrast** (most common image mode).
- **Backscattered Electrons (BSE):** High energy, generated from deeper layers → provides **compositional (Z-contrast)** information.
- **Characteristic X-rays (EDX/EDS):** Used for elemental analysis.
- **Cathodoluminescence (CL):** Used for optical property mapping.

### 2.2.3 Resolution Limit

$$r_{SEM} = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{\sin \alpha}$$

where $\lambda$ is the de Broglie wavelength of the electron and $\alpha$ is the convergence semi-angle. For 20 kV electrons, $\lambda \approx 0.0086$ nm, giving $r \approx 1$ nm.

### 2.2.4 Interaction Volume (Kanaya-Okayama Range)

$$R_{KO} = \frac{0.0276 \cdot A \cdot E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.889} \cdot \rho}$$

where:
- $A$ = atomic weight
- $E$ = accelerating voltage (kV)
- $Z$ = atomic number
- $\rho$ = density (g/cm³)

### 2.2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of SEM
- Wear scar analysis on tribometer test pins (ASTM G99)
- Morphological study of agglomerated nanoparticles in lubricants
- Fracture surface analysis of fatigued gears
- Coating thickness measurement via cross-sectional SEM

---

## 2.3 Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
A high-energy electron beam (60–300 kV) is generated, accelerated, and focused by **electromagnetic condenser lenses** onto an ultra-thin specimen (<100 nm). Electrons transmitted through the specimen are focused by **objective, intermediate, and projector lenses** to form a magnified image on a fluorescent screen or CCD camera. Two primary modes: **Imaging Mode** (mass-thickness and diffraction contrast) and **Diffraction Mode** (selected-area electron diffraction, SAED).

### 2.3.2 de Broglie Wavelength of Electrons

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$$

For non-relativistic approximation at 100 kV: $\lambda \approx 0.0037$ nm.

### 2.3.3 Resolution Limit (Abbe Equation for TEM)

$$r_{TEM} = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{\sin \alpha}$$

At 200 kV, $\lambda \approx 0.0025$ nm, giving $r < 0.1$ nm.

### 2.3.4 Mass-Thickness Contrast
Regions of the sample with higher atomic number or greater thickness scatter more electrons out of the beam, appearing darker. This is the dominant contrast in **biological and polymer TEM** imaging.

### 2.3.5 Diffraction Contrast
Crystalline regions diffract electrons strongly. By tilting the sample or selecting a specific diffracted beam with an **objective aperture**, defects such as dislocations, stacking faults, and grain boundaries become visible.

### 2.3.6 HRTEM (High-Resolution TEM)
In **HRTEM mode**, two or more beams interfere to produce **lattice fringes** — direct images of crystal planes. This allows measurement of d-spacing and identification of crystalline phases at the nanoscale.

### 2.3.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of TEM
- Imaging individual MoS₂ layers (1–10 layers stacked) used as friction modifiers
- Identifying the core-shell structure of nano-additives
- Visualizing dislocations in tribo-layers formed during sliding
- Studying the atomic structure of DLC coatings

---

## 2.4 Scanning Tunnelling Microscopy (STM) — Deep Theory

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle
A sharp metal tip (typically W or Pt-Ir), with an atomically sharp apex (single-atom tip), is brought to within ~0.3–1.0 nm of a conducting sample. A bias voltage (1 mV–4 V) is applied. Quantum-mechanical **tunnelling** of electrons across the vacuum gap produces a current of 0.1–10 nA. A **piezoelectric scanner** (with sub-Å positioning accuracy) raster-scans the tip across the surface. The tip's vertical position is adjusted to maintain constant current (or the current is recorded at constant height), mapping the surface topography.

### 2.4.2 Tunnelling Current — Exponential Dependence

$$I \propto V \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$$

where:
- $I$ = tunnelling current
- $V$ = bias voltage
- $d$ = tip-sample distance
- $\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m_e \phi}}{\hbar}$ = decay constant
- $\phi$ = average work function of tip and sample (~4–5 eV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** The exponential dependence means the tunnelling current changes by an order of magnitude for every 0.1 nm change in tip-sample distance. This is the *physical origin* of STM's extraordinary vertical resolution (~0.01 nm).

### 2.4.3 Operating Modes
- **Constant-Current Mode (CCM):** A feedback loop adjusts the tip's Z-position to maintain constant tunnelling current. The recorded Z-position vs. (X, Y) gives topography. **Most common mode.**
- **Constant-Height Mode (CHM):** Tip height is fixed; tunnelling current is recorded. Faster, but risk of tip crash on rough surfaces.

### 2.4.4 Piezoelectric Scanner Equation
A piezo tube expands/contracts in proportion to applied voltage:

$$\Delta L = d_{31} \cdot V_{piezo}$$

where $d_{31}$ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient (~2–10 Å/V for PZT ceramics).

### 2.4.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of STM
- Atomic-scale imaging of graphite, graphene, and HOPG substrates
- Investigating the electronic structure of tribo-films
- STM-based manipulation of single atoms for nano-tribology research
- Studying surface reconstruction on copper sliding contacts

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for any KTU question on this module. Memorize symbols, units, and applicability.**

| # | Formula | Equation | Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bragg's Law | $n\lambda = 2 d \sin\theta$ | λ, d in Å; θ in degrees | XRD peak positions |
| 2 | Scherrer Equation | $D = K\lambda/(\beta\cos\theta)$ | D in nm; β in radians | Crystallite size from peak broadening |
| 3 | Williamson-Hall | $\beta\cos\theta = K\lambda/D + 4\varepsilon\sin\theta$ | Same as above | Strain + size separation |
| 4 | Interplanar Spacing (Cubic) | $d_{hkl} = a/\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ | a in Å | Crystal structure analysis |
| 5 | de Broglie (electron) | $\lambda = h/p = h/\sqrt{2mE}$ | λ in nm; E in J | SEM/TEM electron wavelength |
| 6 | Abbe Resolution | $r = 0.61\lambda/\sin\alpha$ | nm | SEM/TEM resolution limit |
| 7 | Kanaya-Okayama Range | $R_{KO} = 0.0276 A E^{1.67}/(Z^{0.889}\rho)$ | μm | Electron penetration depth |
| 8 | SEM Magnification | $M = L_{screen}/L_{specimen}$ | dimensionless | Image scaling |
| 9 | STM Tunnelling Current | $I \propto V \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$ | nA; d in nm | Tip-sample gap sensitivity |
| 10 | Piezo Expansion | $\Delta L = d_{31} V$ | Å | STM scanner displacement |
| 11 | Decay Constant | $\kappa = \sqrt{2m_e\phi}/\hbar$ | nm⁻¹ | STM tunnelling parameter |
| 12 | Acceleration Energy | $E = eV$ | eV | Electron beam energy |

### Boundary Conditions & Caveats
- **Bragg's Law:** Valid only for crystalline solids with long-range order. Amorphous materials give broad humps.
- **Scherrer Equation:** Assumes strain-free broadening. Valid for $D < 100$ nm.
- **STM:** Requires conducting sample. Insulating samples → use **AFM** instead.
- **TEM:** Sample must be ultra-thin (<100 nm). Thicker samples → multiple scattering, blurred image.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bragg's Law from the Path Difference Concept

**Problem Setup:** X-rays of wavelength $\lambda$ strike two successive parallel atomic planes separated by interplanar distance $d$ at an angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Geometry:**
The incident ray strikes plane 1 at point A and plane 2 at point B. The reflected ray leaves plane 1 at A' and plane 2 at B'. Both reflections are equal-angle (specular).

**Step 2 — Draw Perpendiculars from A to the Incident Ray and from A to the Reflected Ray:**
Construct perpendicular AC from A onto the incident ray, and perpendicular AD from A onto the reflected ray. The path difference between the two reflected rays is:

$$\Delta = CB + BA$$

**Step 3 — Express the Path Components in Terms of d and θ:**

From the geometry of the right triangles formed:

$$CB = BA = d \sin\theta$$

Therefore, the **total path difference** is:

$$\Delta = 2 d \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Condition for Constructive Interference:**
For constructive interference (a strong reflected peak), the path difference must equal an integer multiple of the wavelength:

$$\Delta = n\lambda$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Obtain Bragg's Law:**

$$n\lambda = 2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta \quad \blacksquare$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each (hkl) plane family has a unique $d_{hkl}$, so each produces a peak at a unique $\theta$. This allows identification of all crystal phases in the sample.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Scherrer Equation from the Diffraction Line Width

**Problem Setup:** A crystallite of finite size $D$ contains a finite number of parallel lattice planes. This limits the sharpness of the diffraction condition.

**Step 1 — Start with Bragg's Law (differentiated):**

$$\lambda = 2 d \sin\theta$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $\theta$ (assuming $\lambda$ and $d$ constant):

$$0 = 2 d \cos\theta \, d\theta$$

This gives zero broadening if the crystal is infinite — confirming infinite crystals give delta-function peaks.

**Step 2 — Account for Finite Crystallite Size:**
For a crystallite of finite size $D$, the number of lattice planes contributing to the diffracted beam is limited. The minimum broadening $\Delta\theta$ corresponds to the case where the path difference between X-rays scattered from the first and last plane differs by exactly one wavelength:

$$2 D \sin(\theta + \Delta\theta) - 2 D \sin\theta = \lambda$$

**Step 3 — Apply Small-Angle Approximation:**
For small $\Delta\theta$:

$$\sin(\theta + \Delta\theta) \approx \sin\theta + \Delta\theta \cos\theta$$

Substituting:

$$2 D (\sin\theta + \Delta\theta\cos\theta - \sin\theta) = \lambda$$

$$2 D \Delta\theta \cos\theta = \lambda$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Crystallite Size D:**

$$\boxed{D = \frac{\lambda}{2 \Delta\theta \cos\theta}}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the Scherrer Constant K:**
The exact value depends on crystallite shape; a numerical constant $K$ (typically 0.9 for spherical crystallites) is introduced:

$$D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta} \quad \blacksquare$$

where $\beta$ is the **full width at half maximum (FWHM)** of the diffraction peak (in radians).

**Conversion Logic:** Larger crystallites → sharper peaks → smaller FWHM. Smaller crystallites → broader peaks → larger FWHM.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: de Broglie Wavelength of Accelerated Electrons

**Problem Setup:** An electron is accelerated through a potential difference $V$. We need its wavelength to determine TEM/SEM resolution.

**Step 1 — Relate Kinetic Energy to Accelerating Voltage:**
The work done by the electric field on the electron equals its kinetic energy:

$$E_k = eV$$

For example, $V = 100$ kV gives $E_k = 100$ keV = $1.602 \times 10^{-14}$ J.

**Step 2 — Use the Non-Relativistic Momentum Relation:**

$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e E_k} = \sqrt{2 m_e eV}$$

**Step 3 — Apply de Broglie's Equation:**

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e eV}}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in Numerical Values:**

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot V}}$$

For $V = 100{,}000$ V:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2.92 \times 10^{-44}}} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1.708 \times 10^{-22}}$$

$$\lambda \approx 3.88 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} = 0.00388 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 5 — Account for Relativistic Correction (for $V > 100$ kV):**

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e eV \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}} \quad \blacksquare$$

For $V = 200$ kV: $\lambda \approx 0.00251$ nm.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: STM Tunnelling Current Exponential Relationship

**Problem Setup:** Calculate the tunnelling current as a function of tip-sample distance, starting from the Schrödinger equation for a 1-D potential barrier.

**Step 1 — Define the Potential Barrier:**
For a tip-sample gap of width $d$ and barrier height $\phi$ (work function):

$$V(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & x < 0 \\ \phi & 0 \le x \le d \\ 0 & x > d \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation in the Barrier:**

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e} \frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} + \phi \psi = E \psi$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = \frac{2m_e(\phi - E)}{\hbar^2} \psi = \kappa^2 \psi$$

where the **decay constant** is:

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 m_e \phi}}{\hbar}$$

**Step 3 — General Solution Inside the Barrier:**

$$\psi(x) = A e^{-\kappa x} + B e^{+\kappa x}$$

For a wide barrier ($d > 1/\kappa$), the growing exponential $e^{+\kappa x}$ is negligible, so:

$$\psi(d) \approx A e^{-\kappa d}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Probability Density at $x = d$:**

$$\vert\psi(d)\vert^2 = \vert A \vert^2 e^{-2\kappa d}$$

**Step 5 — Relate Tunnelling Current to Probability Density:**

$$I \propto \vert \psi \vert^2 \cdot V = C \cdot V \cdot e^{-2\kappa d} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Conversion Logic:** With typical $\phi \approx 4$ eV, $\kappa \approx 10^{10}$ m⁻¹ = 10 nm⁻¹. So a 0.1 nm change in $d$ changes the current by a factor of $e^{2} \approx 7.4$ — a **dramatic sensitivity**, the origin of STM's atomic resolution.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example — XRD Grain Size Calculation

**Problem:** A Cu Kα XRD peak of nano-crystalline Al₂O₃ appears at $2\theta = 46.5°$ with an FWHM of $0.85°$. Calculate the average crystallite size using the Scherrer equation.

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 1.5406$ Å
- $2\theta = 46.5°$ → $\theta = 23.25°$
- $\text{FWHM} = 0.85°$
- $K = 0.9$ (spherical assumption)

**Step 1 — Convert FWHM from Degrees to Radians:**

$$\beta = 0.85° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 0.01484 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\cos\theta$:**

$$\cos(23.25°) = 0.9188$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Scherrer Equation:**

$$D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}$$

$$D = \frac{0.9 \times 1.5406 \text{ Å}}{0.01484 \times 0.9188}$$

$$D = \frac{1.3865 \text{ Å}}{0.01363} = 101.7 \text{ Å} \approx 10.17 \text{ nm}$$

**Conclusion:** The crystallite size of the Al₂O₃ nano-additive is approximately **10.2 nm**, confirming its nano-scale nature, suitable for use as a tribological additive in engine oil.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Worked Example — Electron Wavelength in SEM

**Problem:** Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of electrons accelerated at 20 kV in an SEM.

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy:**

$$E_k = eV = (1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C})(20{,}000 \text{ V}) = 3.204 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Momentum (Non-Relativistic):**

$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e E_k} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.204 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$p = \sqrt{5.836 \times 10^{-45}} = 7.640 \times 10^{-23} \text{ kg·m/s}$$

**Step 3 — de Broglie Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{7.640 \times 10^{-23}} = 8.67 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} = 0.00867 \text{ nm}$$

**Conclusion:** At 20 kV, the SEM electron wavelength is **~0.00867 nm**, which is 100,000× shorter than visible light — explaining why SEM achieves ~1 nm resolution (vs. ~200 nm for optical microscopes).

---

## 3.7 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Feature | XRD | SEM | TEM | STM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Probe** | X-ray photon | Scanned electron beam | Transmitted electrons | Tunnelling electrons |
| **Energy** | 8 keV (Cu Kα) | 1–30 keV | 60–300 keV | 1 meV – 4 eV (bias) |
| **Wavelength** | 0.154 nm | 0.0086 nm @ 20 kV | 0.0017 nm @ 300 kV | Quantum decay length |
| **Resolution** | Bulk-avg. (μm³) | 1–10 nm | <0.1 nm | 0.01 nm (vertical) |
| **Magnification** | N/A | 10⁵ | 10⁷ | 10⁸ (effective) |
| **Sample prep** | Powder/flat bulk | Conductive coating | Ultra-thin (<100 nm) | Atomically clean conductive |
| **Vacuum** | Atmospheric | High vacuum | High vacuum | UHV or ambient |
| **Destructive?** | Non-destructive | Mostly non-destructive | Destructive (cutting) | Non-destructive |
| **Imaging mode** | Diffractogram | Topographic | Internal structure | Atomic topography |
| **Best for** | Phase ID, grain size | Surface morphology | Lattice imaging | Atomic surface mapping |
