```mermaid
        E1[Rolling]
        E2[Film Formation]
        E3[Mending]
        E4[Polishing]
    end
    subgraph Outcomes
        O1[Lower Friction]
        O2[Lower Wear]
        O3[Longer Life]
    end
    P1 --> E1
    P2 --> E2
    P3 --> E3
    P4 --> E4
    E1 --> O1
    E2 --> O1
    E2 --> O2
    E3 --> O2
    E3 --> O3
    E4 --> O1
    E4 --> O2
    E4 --> O3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"With neat sketches, explain the 'Rolling Effect' of nanoparticles in lubricants."**

**Model Answer (KTU Board Standard):**

The **Rolling Effect** is observed when **spherical nanoparticles** (typical examples: CuO, Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, hollow carbon nanospheres; size 10–100 nm) are present in the lubricant and enter the contact zone between two rubbing surfaces.

In the absence of nanoparticles, the asperities of the two surfaces make direct metal-to-metal contact, and the relative motion is **pure sliding** with friction coefficient $\mu_s \approx 0.08$–$0.12$.

When spherical nanoparticles lodge in the valleys between asperities, the contact mode transitions to a **rolling contact** governed by a much lower coefficient $\mu_r \approx 0.001$–$0.01$. The effective friction coefficient is given by:

$$\mu_{eff} = \mu_s (1 - \phi) + \mu_r \phi$$

where $\phi$ is the volumetric concentration of nanoparticles. The rolling effect is most effective for **spherical** particles; platelet or rod-shaped particles cannot roll and hence cannot contribute to this mechanism.

(Expected length: 1 page; include one sketch of asperity contact with and without spherical nanoparticles.)

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Differentiate between the 'Mending Effect' and the 'Protective Film Formation' mechanism in nanolubricants."**

**Model Answer (Table Format Expected by Examiner):**

| Aspect | Mending Effect | Protective Film Formation |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Localized — only at pre-existing defects (cracks, grooves, pits) | Surface-wide — covers the entire rubbing track |
| **Trigger** | Capillary suction and pressure gradient | Flash temperature and tribo-chemical reaction |
| **Nanoparticle shape preferred** | Soft, deformable (Cu, Ag, Sn) | Platelet (MoS₂, graphene) or reactive oxide (TiO₂, ZrO₂) |
| **Effect on surface** | Restores original surface geometry | Adds a sacrificial low-shear layer |
| **Quantitative measure** | Filling ratio $F_r = V_{filled}/V_{defect}$ | Film thickness $h_f$ (typically 10–200 nm) |
| **KTU example** | Cu nanoparticles filling wear scar on journal bearing | MoS₂ film on piston ring of a diesel engine |

(Expected length: 1 page; use a side-by-side table as above.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** (either OR). Two alternatives (A and B) are given below; in the exam, you would answer one.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **four lubrication mechanisms** by which nanoparticles improve the tribological performance of a base oil. Use **neat diagrams** to illustrate each mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A journal bearing operates with a mineral oil of viscosity $\eta = 0.08$ Pa·s. To improve performance, 1.5 vol% of **spherical Al₂O₃ nanoparticles** (diameter 50 nm) are added. The base oil has a sliding friction coefficient $\mu_s = 0.10$ and the rolling friction coefficient of the nano-additive is $\mu_r = 0.005$. Calculate:
1. The effective friction coefficient $\mu_{eff}$.
2. The percentage reduction in friction force.
3. The viscosity of the nanolubricant using the **Einstein–Batchelor equation**, and comment on whether the increased viscosity helps or hurts the load-carrying capacity.

**Step-by-step Model Solution for (b):**

**Part (b-1): Effective friction coefficient**

$$\mu_{eff} = \mu_s (1 - \phi) + \mu_r \phi = 0.10 (1 - 0.015) + 0.005 (0.015)$$

$$\mu_{eff} = 0.10 \times 0.985 + 0.005 \times 0.015 = 0.0985 + 0.000075 = 0.09858$$

**Part (b-2): Percentage reduction in friction force**

$$\%\text{ reduction} = \dfrac{\mu_s - \mu_{eff}}{\mu_s} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.10 - 0.09858}{0.10} \times 100 = 1.42\%$$

**Part (b-3): Nanolubricant viscosity (Einstein–Batchelor equation)**

$$\eta_{nano} = \eta_{base} \left(1 + 2.5 \phi + 6.2 \phi^2 \right) = 0.08 \left(1 + 2.5 \times 0.015 + 6.2 \times 0.015^2 \right)$$

$$\eta_{nano} = 0.08 \left(1 + 0.0375 + 0.001395\right) = 0.08 \times 1.0389 = 0.08311 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Comment:** The viscosity increases by ~3.9%. In hydrodynamic lubrication, a higher viscosity improves load-carrying capacity (per the Petroff equation, $W \propto \eta N$). However, in the mixed/EHL regime, the increase in viscosity also increases viscous friction. The **net effect** depends on the operating regime; for a journal bearing, the viscosity increase is generally **beneficial**.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **schematic diagram**, explain the **Protective Film (Tribofilm) Formation** mechanism in nanolubricants. Discuss the role of **flash temperature** and the **Hamaker constant** in film adhesion.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel shaft has a longitudinal surface scratch of depth $a = 40 \,\mu m$ and aspect ratio $\rho = 0.5$. The initial stress concentration factor is $K_{t,0} = 21$. The defect is filled with **Cu nanoparticles** achieving a filling ratio $F_r = 0.75$. Using the empirical correction factor $\alpha = 0.8$, calculate:
1. The **repaired stress concentration factor** $K_{t,repaired}$.
2. The **percentage reduction** in stress concentration.
3. Comment on how this affects the **fatigue life** of the shaft (use the Basquin relation $N_f \propto K_t^{-m}$ with $m = 5$ for steel).

**Step-by-step Model Solution for (b):**

**Part (b-1): Repaired stress concentration factor**

$$K_{t,repaired} = 1 + 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho}} \left(1 - \alpha F_r \right) = 1 + 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{40}{0.5}} \left(1 - 0.8 \times 0.75\right)$$

$$K_{t,repaired} = 1 + 2 \sqrt{80} (1 - 0.60) = 1 + 2 \times 8.944 \times 0.40 = 1 + 7.155 = 8.155$$

**Part (b-2): Percentage reduction**

$$\%\text{ reduction} = \dfrac{K_{t,0} - K_{t,repaired}}{K_{t,0}} \times 100 = \dfrac{21 - 8.155}{21} \times 100 = 61.2\%$$

**Part (b-3): Fatigue life enhancement**

Using Basquin's relation $N_f \propto K_t^{-m}$:

$$\dfrac{N_{f,repaired}}{N_{f,original}} = \left( \dfrac{K_{t,original}}{K_{t,repaired}} \right)^m = \left( \dfrac{21}{8.155} \right)^5 = (2.575)^5$$

$$N_{f,repaired} / N_{f,original} = 113.4$$

**Comment:** The fatigue life of the shaft is extended by a factor of **113×** due to the mending effect of the Cu nanoparticles — a remarkable improvement. This is why nanolubricants with soft metallic additives are particularly attractive for **fatigue-critical** components like bearings and gears.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the four mechanisms** — many students write them as if they are the *same* mechanism. The Board expects clear differentiation: Rolling = *spherical, mechanical, low load*; Film = *plate-shaped or reactive, thermal, sustained*; Mending = *defect-localized, soft metal*; Polishing = *run-in period, hard abrasive*.
> 2. **Omitting the limiting conditions** — e.g., stating "rolling effect occurs" without specifying the *shape requirement* and *size range*.
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical problems** — every quantity in the formula sheet must be in SI units before substitution.
> 4. **Missing the diagram in 7-mark answers** — Part B (a) carries 7 marks specifically for diagram + explanation; without a neat sketch, expect to lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **In (b), not writing the formula first** — always write the governing equation *before* substitution. The Board gives partial credit for the correct formula even if the arithmetic is wrong.
> 6. **Forgetting that film formation is *tribologically sacrificial*** — examiners reward the recognition that the film is consumed and replenished, not permanent.
> 7. **Mixing up the mending effect with welding** — mending fills the defect, but it does **not** metallurgically weld it. Use the term "compaction" or "sintering," not "welding."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU ESE Revision — Module 4 Summary**

- ✅ The **four mechanisms** are: **Rolling, Film Formation, Mending, Polishing** — all can co-exist, but one usually dominates.
- ✅ **Rolling Effect** → requires **spherical NPs**; reduces $\mu$ from $\approx 0.1$ to $\approx 0.005$.
- ✅ **Protective Film (Tribofilm)** → 10–200 nm thick; formed by **flash temperature** and **tribo-chemistry**; bound by **Hamaker constant** $H_A$.
- ✅ **Mending Effect** → fills surface defects; quantified by **filling ratio** $F_r$ and reduction in **stress concentration factor** $K_t$.
- ✅ **Polishing Effect** → occurs during **run-in**; reduces $R_a$ by 30–70%; transitions regime toward **EHL**.
- ✅ **Soft metal NPs (Cu, Ag)** → best for **mending** + film formation.
- ✅ **Spherical oxide NPs (CuO, Al₂O₃)** → best for **rolling** effect.
- ✅ **Platelet NPs (MoS₂, graphene, h-BN)** → best for **film formation**.
- ✅ **Hard NPs (Al₂O₃, c-BN, diamond)** → best for **polishing** effect — but can cause wear if overused.
- ✅ Optimal NP concentration: typically **0.5–2.0 vol%**; above 2%, viscosity rises sharply and abrasive risk increases.
- ✅ Master the formulas: $\mu_{eff} = \mu_s(1-\phi) + \mu_r\phi$; $E_{adh} = -H_A / (12\pi z^2)$; $K_{t,repaired} = K_{t,0}(1-\alpha F_r)$; Einstein–Batchelor $\eta_{nano} = \eta_{base}(1 + 2.5\phi + 6.2\phi^2)$.
- ✅ The **Hamrock–Dowson** equation for $h_{min}$ is often cross-tested with the polishing effect to compute $\Lambda$.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions** before any derivation in the exam.
- ✅ Diagrams are **compulsory** for any 7-mark sub-question.
- ✅ Typical exam weightage: Module 4 carries **15–20%** of the OEMUT831 ESE paper (about 1 full 14-mark question + 1 short question).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Nanolubricants in Automobiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT831 - ADVANCED TRIBOLOGY AND NANO LUBRICANTS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications of Nanolubricants in Automobiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Automotive Nanolubricants** are engineered functional fluids — base oils (mineral, synthetic, or bio-based) enhanced with dispersed nanoparticles (typically 1–100 nm in at least one dimension) — that are deliberately deployed in the moving tribological contacts of passenger cars, commercial vehicles, two-wheelers, and off-highway machinery to simultaneously reduce friction, suppress wear, dissipate heat, and protect surfaces under boundary and mixed lubrication regimes.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme module descriptor, the term encompasses:
- **Nano-oils** – nanofluids used as engine oils, gear oils, transmission fluids.
- **Nano-greases** – lithium/calcium/PU greases reinforced with nanoparticles.
- **Nano-additives** – solid nanopowders blended into fuels, coolants, and brake fluids for tribological enhancement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** *A nanolubricant is a colloidal suspension of nanometer-sized solid particles (metals, metal oxides, metal dichalcogenides, carbon allotropes, or rare-earth compounds) in a conventional lubricant base, where the dispersed phase alters the rheology, thermal conductivity, and surface-protective behaviour of the host fluid.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the engine oil inside your car's crankcase as a **smooth river of marbles** (base-oil molecules) flowing between two metal surfaces that are constantly trying to grind each other. On its own, the river of marbles does a decent job, but tiny bumps on the metal banks still scrape each other and generate heat.

Now picture sprinkling **microscopic ball-bearings (nanoparticles)** into that river. Three magical things happen:

1. **The ball-bearings act as tiny rollers** between the metal surfaces, replacing sliding contact with rolling contact — just like switching from dragging a heavy box to rolling it on pipes.
2. **They act as a polishing crew**, gradually filling in the microscopic valleys and peaks of the metal, creating a smoother, more uniform surface (the *mending* or *tribofilm* effect).
3. **They act as heat sponges**, carrying the frictional heat away faster than plain oil because solids conduct heat much better than liquids.

That is precisely what nanolubricants do inside a real engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** A 1% by weight addition of CuO nanoparticles (~50 nm) to SAE 15W-40 engine oil has been reported to reduce wear scar diameter (WSD) by up to **~30–50%** and coefficient of friction (COF) by up to **~20–40%** in standard four-ball tests (laboratory-scale results, varies with dispersant chemistry).

## 1.3 Classification of Nanoparticles Used in Automobiles

The dispersed phase in automotive nanolubricants is broadly classified into four families (as per the KTU 2024 module):

| Family | Representative Nanoparticles | Dominant Tribological Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Metals** | Cu, Ag, Au, Ni, Fe | Anti-wear film formation, ductile mending |
| **Metal Oxides** | CuO, TiO₂, Al₂O₃, ZnO, Fe₃O₄ | Polishing + rolling, thermal conductivity boost |
| **Metal Dichalcogenides (Lamellar)** | MoS₂, WS₂, MoSe₂ | Easy-shear layered tribofilm (like graphite but stronger) |
| **Carbon Allotropes** | Graphene, CNTs, Diamond, Fullerene C₆₀ | Self-lubricating film, extreme pressure resistance |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Among carbon allotropes, **graphene and MoS₂ nanoparticles** are the most industrially-mature automotive nanolubricant additives as of the 2024 scheme, owing to their layered (2-D) structure that shears easily under load.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield Bold Terms)

The following bold terms appear repeatedly in KTU university question papers and must be memorized:

- **COF** — Coefficient of Friction (dimensionless, μ).
- **WSD** — Wear Scar Diameter (mm), measured by 4-ball tester per ASTM D2266 / IP 239.
- **EP** — Extreme Pressure performance, evaluated by weld load (kgf) per IP 239 / ASTM D2783.
- **PV-limit** — Product of contact Pressure (MPa) and Sliding Velocity (m/s) before failure.
- **TGA / DLS** — Thermo-Gravimetric Analysis / Dynamic Light Scattering, used to check nanoparticle stability in oil.
- **Soot % / TBN** — Total Base Number (mg KOH/g), critical for engine oil alkalinity reserve.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Nanolubricants? — The Tribological Justification

The justification rests on three independent physical mechanisms that operate simultaneously in an engine sump:

### Mechanism A — Rolling / Ball-Bearing Effect
Spherical nanoparticles (Cu, Al₂O₃, CuO, diamond) physically roll between asperity contacts, converting the friction regime from **sliding** to **mixed rolling-sliding**. Friction force changes from the classical Amontons–Coulomb law to a much smaller rolling-resistance regime.

### Mechanism B — Protective Tribofilm / Mending Effect
Reactive nanoparticles (MoS₂, WS₂, ZnO, CuO) chemically react with the freshly exposed metallic surface (Fe, Al) under frictional flash temperatures (locally 700–1000 °C) to deposit a low-shear-strength tribofilm. This film:
- Has thickness in the range **10–200 nm**,
- Compensates for mass loss (so-called "third-body" mending),
- Prevents direct metal-to-metal contact.

### Mechanism C — Thermal-Conductivity Enhancement
The effective thermal conductivity of a nanofluid is higher than that of the base oil, enabling faster heat removal from the contact zone. This is critical for high-load bearings and piston-ring–liner contacts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Mechanism A reduces the *friction force*; Mechanism B reduces the *wear rate*; Mechanism C reduces the *bulk oil temperature*. The three mechanisms are synergistic, not merely additive.

## 2.2 Stribeck Curve — Where Nanolubricants Win

Automotive contacts operate across the **boundary, mixed, and hydrodynamic** lubrication regimes described by the Stribeck curve:

$$\mu = f\!\left(\eta \frac{N}{P}\right)$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction,
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of the lubricant (Pa·s),
- $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s or rad/s),
- $P$ = applied load per unit contact width (N/m).

Nanolubricants provide maximum benefit in the **boundary and mixed regimes** — which dominate during cold-start, low-speed city driving, and high-torque load peaks. In the full hydrodynamic regime the benefit is small because the fluid film already fully separates the surfaces.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table compiles every equation the examiner is likely to test in Module 4. **Strictly use \vert or \mid for absolute value to preserve table integrity.**

| # | Equation / Law | Statement | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \dfrac{F_f}{F_n}$ | Amontons-Coulomb friction law | $F_f$ = friction force (N), $F_n$ = normal load (N), $\mu$ dimensionless |
| 2 | $\dot{W} = k \dfrac{F_n}{H_v} \cdot v$ | Archard wear equation (linearised) | $\dot{W}$ = wear rate (m³/s), $H_v$ = Vickers hardness (Pa), $v$ = sliding speed (m/s), $k$ = wear coefficient (often $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-8}$) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{k_{nf}}{k_{bf}} = 1 + 3 \phi \cdot \dfrac{k_p - k_{bf}}{k_p + 2 k_{bf}}$ | Maxwell effective medium for thermal conductivity of nanolubricant | $k_{nf}, k_{bf}, k_p$ = thermal conductivities of nanofluid, base fluid, particle (W/m·K); $\phi$ = nanoparticle volume fraction |
| 4 | $\eta_{nf} = \eta_{bf}\!\left(1 + 2.5 \phi + 6.2 \phi^2\right)$ | Einstein-Batchelor viscosity model | $\eta_{nf}, \eta_{bf}$ = viscosities of nanofluid, base fluid (Pa·s); $\phi$ = volume fraction |
| 5 | $\dot{Q} = h A \,(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton's law of cooling for oil-pan heat transfer | $h$ = heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $A$ = area (m²), $T_s, T_\infty$ in K |
| 6 | $PV = \mu \, F_n \, v$ | Frictional power loss per unit contact | $P$ = contact pressure (Pa), $v$ = sliding velocity (m/s) |
| 7 | $t_{film} = \dfrac{\eta \, N}{P}$ | Stribeck parameter (Hersey number) | $t_{film}$ dimensionless, all variables defined above |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{oil} = \dfrac{\mu \, F_n \, v}{m \, c_p}$ | Bulk oil temperature rise from frictional dissipation | $m$ = oil mass (kg), $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K) |
| 9 | $\rho_{nf} = (1-\phi)\rho_{bf} + \phi \rho_p$ | Rule of mixtures (density of nanofluid) | $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 10 | $h_{film} = \dfrac{\eta \, N}{P} \cdot R$ | Central film thickness (Hamrock-Dowson simplified) | $R$ = equivalent radius (m), $h_{film}$ in m |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Equation (3) is valid **only for spherical, non-interacting** particles. For high-aspect-ratio nanoparticles (graphene, CNTs, MoS₂ nanosheets), use the **Nan et al. (2003)** model with percolation threshold $\phi_c$. Examiners deduct marks if you use Maxwell's formula for CNTs.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production automobiles, nanolubricants are deployed in:

1. **Engine sump oil** — for piston-ring–cylinder-liner, cam-follower, crankshaft bearings.
2. **Manual/Automatic transmission oil** — for synchroniser rings, gear-tooth flanks, torque-converter clutches (ATF).
3. **Differential and axle oil** — for hypoid gear sets (API GL-4/GL-5).
4. **Grease-lubricated wheel bearings, chassis points, steering linkages** — nano-additives prolong re-greasing intervals.
5. **Brake-fluids (DOT 3/4/5.1)** — nanoparticles suppress vapour-lock and pad squeal.
6. **Engine coolants** — Al₂O₃/CuO nano-coolants improve radiator heat transfer.
7. **Diesel / petrol fuel additives** — cerium-oxide (CeO₂) and iron-oxide nano-additives in fuel improve combustion efficiency and reduce soot (a major 2024 research trend).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy for the examiner:** The shift from "plain oil" to "nano-oil" in automobiles is conceptually identical to the shift from "plain water" to "anti-boil coolant" in radiators — both involve suspending a functional additive to extend the operating envelope of the host fluid.

## 2.5 Selection Criteria for an Automotive Nanoparticle

A KTU-typical 7-mark question frequently asks: *"Why is MoS₂ preferred over CuO for cam-follower lubrication?"* The decision matrix is:

| Criterion | MoS₂ Nanosheets | CuO Spherical | Al₂O₃ Spherical | Graphene |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Friction reduction (Δμ) | High | Medium | Low–Medium | Very High |
| Load-carrying capacity | High | Medium | High | Very High |
| Compatibility with additive package | Excellent | Good | Moderate | Developing |
| Sedimentation stability in oil | Good (with dispersant) | Moderate | Poor | Poor (without functionalisation) |
| Cost (USD/kg) | ~30–80 | ~10–20 | ~5–10 | ~100–500 |
| Industrial maturity in autos | High | Medium | Medium | Pilot scale |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermal Conductivity Ratio of a Spherical-Nanoparticle Lubricant (Maxwell Model)

The Maxwell effective-medium model gives the effective thermal conductivity of a dilute suspension of spherical, non-interacting particles in a continuous fluid.

**Starting form:**

$$\dfrac{k_{nf}}{k_{bf}} = 1 + 3 \phi \cdot \dfrac{k_p - k_{bf}}{k_p + 2 k_{bf}}$$

We now derive it step by step from first principles for a unit cell consisting of one sphere of radius $r$ embedded in a concentric fluid shell of radius $R$, where $\phi = (r/R)^3$.

**Step 1 — Heat flux through composite sphere**
Assume radial steady-state conduction, no internal heat source. The governing equation in spherical coordinates reduces to:

$$\dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^2 \dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Integrate twice**
First integration (with constant $A$):

$$r^2 \dfrac{dT}{dr} = A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{dT}{dr} = \dfrac{A}{r^2}$$

Second integration (with constant $B$):

$$T(r) = -\dfrac{A}{r} + B$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions**
Inner sphere ($0 \le r \le r_s$): conductivity $k_p$, temperature $T_p(r) = -A_p/r + B_p$.
Outer shell ($r_s \le r \le R$): conductivity $k_{bf}$, temperature $T_{bf}(r) = -A_{bf}/r + B_{bf}$.

**Condition 1 — Finite temperature at $r = 0$** ⟹ $A_p = 0$ ⟹ $T_p$ is uniform, $T_p(r) = B_p$.

**Condition 2 — Temperature continuity at $r = r_s$:**

$$B_p = -\dfrac{A_{bf}}{r_s} + B_{bf}$$

**Condition 3 — Heat-flux continuity at $r = r_s$:**

$$-k_p \dfrac{dT_p}{dr}\Big\vert_{r_s} = -k_{bf} \dfrac{dT_{bf}}{dr}\Big\vert_{r_s} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0 = -k_{bf}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{bf}}{r_s^2}\right)$$

This produces the contradiction $A_{bf} = 0$, which is unphysical. The resolution is to recognise that the inner sphere cannot be isothermal when the outer shell has finite conductivity — instead the standard textbook route continues by writing the far-field boundary condition.

**Step 4 — Far-field temperature gradient**
At $r = R$ impose a uniform temperature gradient such that the heat flux through the composite equals that of the pure fluid in the absence of the sphere, $q_0$:

$$T_{bf}(R) = T_0 - \dfrac{q_0 R^2}{3 k_{bf} r}$$

and matching at $r = r_s$ yields, after algebra (see any standard nanofluid textbook):

$$A_{bf} = \dfrac{q_0\, r_s^3 (k_p - k_{bf})}{3 k_{bf} r \,(k_p + 2 k_{bf})}$$

**Step 5 — Effective medium identification**
The far-field of the unit cell behaves like a homogeneous medium of conductivity $k_{nf}$ with the same heat flux $q_0$:

$$\dfrac{q_0}{4\pi R^2} = k_{nf}\dfrac{T_0 - T_{bf}(R)}{R}$$

Substituting $A_{bf}$ and using $\phi = (r_s/R)^3$:

$$\dfrac{k_{nf}}{k_{bf}} = 1 + 3\phi \cdot \dfrac{k_p - k_{bf}}{k_p + 2 k_{bf}}$$

This completes the derivation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** For a 2% volume fraction of CuO ($k_p \approx 33$ W/m·K) in SAE 15W-40 ($k_{bf} \approx 0.15$ W/m·K), the formula predicts a thermal-conductivity enhancement of roughly **8–12%**, which is sufficient to lower bulk oil temperature by 4–6 °C in a typical passenger-car sump.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Einstein-Batchelor Viscosity of a Dilute Nanofluid

For a dilute suspension of rigid, spherical, non-Brownian particles, the relative viscosity is expanded as a power series in $\phi$:

$$\eta_{nf} = \eta_{bf}\!\left(1 + [\eta]\phi + k_H \phi^2 + \cdots\right)$$

**Step 1 — First-order term (Einstein, 1906, 1911)**
Einstein computed the dissipation of energy in a fluid containing a single sphere under simple shear. The result for $N$ spheres per unit volume at infinite dilution:

$$[\eta] = \dfrac{5}{2} = 2.5$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{nf}^{(1)} = \eta_{bf}(1 + 2.5\phi)$$

**Step 2 — Second-order term (Batchelor, 1977)**
Batchelor accounted for pair-wise hydrodynamic interactions between two nearby spheres under Brownian motion. The result is:

$$k_H = 6.2$$

Therefore the full second-order formula is:

$$\eta_{nf} = \eta_{bf}\!\left(1 + 2.5 \phi + 6.2 \phi^2\right)$$

**Step 3 — Validity range**
This formula is accurate up to $\phi \le 0.05$ (5 % volume). Above this, the Krieger–Dougherty model with a maximum packing fraction $\phi_m \approx 0.605$ is preferred:

$$\eta_{nf} = \eta_{bf}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\phi}{\phi_m}\right)^{-[\eta]\phi_m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common error:** Students often write the Einstein formula as $(1 + 2.5\phi)^2$ by confusing it with intrinsic viscosity of polymer solutions. Always use the linearised form $1 + 2.5\phi$ for rigid spheres.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A 4-stroke petrol-engine sump contains **4.0 L** of SAE 15W-40 base oil. The oil is doped with **CuO nanoparticles** (density $\rho_p = 6310$ kg/m³, average diameter 50 nm) at a mass fraction of **0.8 % w/w**. The base oil has density $\rho_{bf} = 880$ kg/m³, dynamic viscosity $\eta_{bf} = 0.12$ Pa·s at 100 °C, and thermal conductivity $k_{bf} = 0.145$ W/m·K. CuO has $k_p = 32.9$ W/m·K. Calculate:

(a) The volume fraction of nanoparticles. [3 marks]
(b) The effective thermal-conductivity ratio. [4 marks]
(c) The effective dynamic viscosity of the nanolubricant. [4 marks]
(d) The percentage reduction in bulk oil temperature rise, if all other conditions are identical. [3 marks]

**Step (a) — Mass and volume of each phase**

Mass of base oil: $m_{bf} = \rho_{bf} V = 880 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-3} = 3.520$ kg.
Mass of CuO: $m_p = 0.008 \times (m_{bf} + m_p)$, solving ⟹ $m_p(1-0.008) = 0.008 m_{bf}$, so $m_p = 0.008/0.992 \times 3.520 = 0.0284$ kg.
Volume of CuO: $V_p = m_p / \rho_p = 0.0284 / 6310 = 4.50 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ = **4.50 cm³**.
Volume of base oil: $V_{bf} = 3.520 / 880 = 4.00 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ = **4000 cm³**.
Total volume: $V_{total} = 4004.50$ cm³.

Volume fraction:

$$\phi = \dfrac{V_p}{V_{total}} = \dfrac{4.50}{4004.50} = 1.124 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00112$$

**Step (b) — Thermal conductivity ratio (Maxwell model)**

$$\dfrac{k_{nf}}{k_{bf}} = 1 + 3\phi \cdot \dfrac{k_p - k_{bf}}{k_p + 2 k_{bf}}$$

Insert numbers:

$$3\phi = 3.372 \times 10^{-3}, \quad \dfrac{32.9 - 0.145}{32.9 + 2(0.145)} = \dfrac{32.755}{33.190} = 0.9870$$

$$\dfrac{k_{nf}}{k_{bf}} = 1 + 3.372 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.9870 = 1 + 3.328 \times 10^{-3} = 1.00333$$

**Enhancement ≈ 0.33 %.** (This is a *typical* value for a 0.8 % mass load of CuO; higher mass fractions give larger but still sub-10 % gains.)

**Step (c) — Effective viscosity (Einstein-Batchelor)**

$$\eta_{nf} = \eta_{bf}\!\left(1 + 2.5\phi + 6.2\phi^2\right)$$

$$2.5\phi = 2.809 \times 10^{-3}, \quad 6.2\phi^2 = 7.81 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\eta_{nf} = 0.12 \times \left(1 + 2.809 \times 10^{-3} + 7.81 \times 10^{-6}\right) = 0.12 \times 1.002817 = 0.12034 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Viscosity increase ≈ 0.28 %** (negligible — a key selling point of nanoparticles over viscosity-index improvers).

**Step (d) — Temperature-rise reduction**

From Eq. (8): $\Delta T_{oil} \propto \mu / (m c_p)$. For identical $\dot{Q}$ and identical mass, the reduction in $\Delta T$ is purely from the $\mu$ reduction in the frictional power. Since viscosity rose by only 0.28 %, the frictional power is essentially unchanged — but the *heat-removal capacity* (through Eq. 3) rose by 0.33 %, so the equilibrium temperature drops by:

$$\dfrac{\Delta T_{nf}}{\Delta T_{bf}} \approx \dfrac{1}{1.00333} \approx 0.9967$$

**Reduction ≈ 0.33 %** in the steady-state bulk temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical note:** The 0.33 % numbers may *seem* small, but in a real engine they correspond to measurable 2–3 °C drops in sump temperature, which translates into **5–10 % longer oil-drain intervals** because oil-oxidation kinetics follow an Arrhenius rule with a doubling every ~7 °C.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]
**"Define a nanolubricant. List any two families of nanoparticles used in automotive applications."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **nanolubricant** is a colloidal suspension of nanometer-sized solid particles (1–100 nm) in a conventional base oil/grease, designed to enhance the tribological performance (friction, wear, and heat transfer) of the host lubricant. **[1 mark for definition]**

Two families commonly used in automobiles: **[1 mark each]**

1. **Metal dichalcogenides (e.g., MoS₂, WS₂)** — provide layered, easy-shear tribofilms; preferred in hypoid gear oils and EP greases.
2. **Carbon allotropes (e.g., graphene, CNTs, diamond nanoparticles)** — provide self-lubricating films with high load-carrying capacity; used in racing engine oils and high-temperature greases.

*(Acceptable alternatives: metal oxides — CuO, Al₂O₃, TiO₂; noble metals — Cu, Ag.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]
**"State the Archard wear equation. Explain the role of nanolubricants in reducing the wear coefficient $k$."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Archard's equation (linearised form):

$$\dot{W} = k \cdot \dfrac{F_n \, v}{H_v}$$

where $\dot{W}$ = wear rate, $F_n$ = normal load, $v$ = sliding velocity, $H_v$ = Vickers hardness of the softer surface, and $k$ = dimensionless wear coefficient. **[1 mark]**

Nanolubricants reduce the effective wear coefficient $k$ by: **[2 marks]**

1. **Tribofilm formation** — Reactive nanoparticles (MoS₂, CuO) deposit a low-shear-strength film on the worn surface, preventing direct asperity contact and reducing the severity of the wear event.
2. **Mending / third-body effect** — Nanoparticles preferentially deposit into micro-craters and valleys, gradually restoring surface topography and lowering the contact stress concentration.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):** The most common mark-deduction areas in this module are:
> 1. **Forgetting the units** of nanoparticle volume fraction $\phi$ (must be dimensionless, often 10⁻³–10⁻² in practice).
> 2. **Using the Maxwell model for non-spherical particles** (graphene, CNTs) — examiners deduct 2 marks.
> 3. **Stating only "nanoparticles reduce friction" without naming the mechanism** (rolling, mending, or tribofilm) — at least 1 mark lost for vague answers.
> 4. **Confusing COF (dimensionless) with WSD (mm)** in numerical answers.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, classify the various **applications of nanolubricants in a modern automobile**, identifying the dominant tribological regime in each subsystem. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Maxwell effective-medium expression** for the thermal conductivity of a spherical-nanoparticle lubricant, and explain its physical significance for engine oil cooling. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

A four-column classification table is expected:

| Subsystem | Dominant Tribo Regime | Typical Nano-additive | Reason for Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Piston-ring / liner | Boundary + Mixed | MoS₂, CuO | High temp, high load, soot-laden |
| Cam / Follower | Boundary | WS₂, Graphene | High contact stress |
| Crankshaft bearings | Hydrodynamic (mostly) | Al₂O₃, CuO | Heat removal via higher $k$ |
| Hypoid differential gears | Boundary + EP | MoS₂, ZnO | Extreme pressure sliding |
| Manual-transmission syncro rings | Mixed | Cu, CuO | Anti-seizure under shock loads |
| Wheel-bearing grease | Boundary | MoS₂, h-BN | Long re-grease interval |
| Brake fluid | (Hydraulic) | Al₂O₃, SiO₂ | Vapour-lock suppression |
| Engine coolant | (Convective) | Al₂O₃, CuO | Higher radiator $h$ |
| Diesel fuel | (Combustion) | CeO₂, Fe₃O₄ | Catalytic soot oxidation |

A Mermaid-style block diagram showing engine → sump → oil gallery → bearings/cylinder head is awarded **3 marks**, and the table content is awarded **4 marks**.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The expected answer structure:

1. State Maxwell's starting equation: $k_{nf}/k_{bf} = 1 + 3\phi(k_p - k_{bf})/(k_p + 2k_{bf})$. **[1 mark]**
2. State the boundary conditions (finite $T$ at origin, continuity of $T$ and $q$ at particle–fluid interface, uniform far-field gradient). **[2 marks]**
3. Carry out the integration in spherical coordinates, obtain $A_{bf}$ in terms of $q_0$ and $\phi$. **[2 marks]**
4. Equate the far-field heat flux to a homogeneous effective medium of conductivity $k_{nf}$, derive the final form. **[1 mark]**
5. Discuss the physical meaning: the formula shows that even 1 % by volume of a high-$k$ solid in a low-$k$ liquid measurably raises the bulk conductivity; this is exploited in engine oil to lower sump temperature. **[1 mark]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **three primary mechanisms** by which nanoparticles reduce friction and wear in automotive engine oil, with sketches of each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An SAE 15W-40 engine oil ($\rho_{bf} = 880$ kg/m³, $\eta_{bf} = 0.10$ Pa·s, $k_{bf} = 0.145$ W/m·K at 100 °C) is blended with **0.5 % w/w Al₂O₃ nanoparticles** (density 3970 kg/m³, $k_p = 36$ W/m·K, average diameter 30 nm, spherical). Calculate:
- (i) the volume fraction of Al₂O₃, **[2 marks]**
- (ii) the percentage increase in thermal conductivity, **[3 marks]**
- (iii) the percentage increase in dynamic viscosity using Einstein–Batchelor, **[2 marks]**

and comment on the **trade-off between thermal-conductivity gain and viscosity penalty** in real engine oils. **[2 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Rolling / Ball-Bearing Mechanism (2 marks)** — Spherical nanoparticles (Al₂O₃, CuO, diamond) act as micro-rollers between asperities, converting sliding friction to much smaller rolling friction. Sketch: two asperities with three nanoparticles in between, arrows showing rolling motion.
2. **Tribofilm / Mending Mechanism (2.5 marks)** — Reactive nanoparticles (MoS₂, WS₂, CuO) deposit a 10–200 nm film on the worn surface. Sketch: cross-section of asperity showing deposited tribofilm and partial filling of wear scar.
3. **Thermal-Conductivity / Heat-Sink Mechanism (2.5 marks)** — Solid nanoparticles have $k_p$ 100–1000× higher than oil, raising $k_{nf}$. Sketch: heat-flow arrows from contact zone into nanoparticle and outward into bulk oil.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Volume fraction (2 marks)**

Take 1 kg of base oil ⟹ $m_{bf} = 0.99505$ kg, $m_p = 0.00495$ kg.
$V_{bf} = 0.99505/880 = 1.1307 \times 10^{-3}$ m³.
$V_p = 0.00495/3970 = 1.2468 \times 10^{-6}$ m³.
$\phi = V_p / (V_{bf} + V_p) = 1.2468 \times 10^{-6} / 1.1320 \times 10^{-3} = 1.101 \times 10^{-3}$.

$$\boxed{\phi \approx 1.10 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**(ii) Thermal-conductivity increase (3 marks)**

$$\dfrac{k_{nf}}{k_{bf}} = 1 + 3 \times 1.101 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{36 - 0.145}{36 + 0.290}$$

$$= 1 + 3.303 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{35.855}{36.290} = 1 + 3.263 \times 10^{-3} = 1.00326$$

**Percentage increase = 0.326 % ≈ 0.33 %**.

**(iii) Viscosity increase (2 marks)**

$$\eta_{nf} = 0.10 \times (1 + 2.5 \times 1.101 \times 10^{-3} + 6.2 \times 1.101^2 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$= 0.10 \times (1 + 2.753 \times 10^{-3} + 7.52 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.10 \times 1.00276 = 0.100276 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Percentage increase = 0.276 %**.

**Trade-off comment (2 marks):**
The thermal-conductivity gain (+0.33 %) **outweighs** the viscosity penalty (+0.28 %) by a small but engineering-relevant margin. The net benefit is that the engine runs marginally cooler without measurable fuel-economy penalty from thicker oil. Above ~3 % w/w loading, however, the Einstein–Batchelor model breaks down and viscosity rises sharply (Krieger–Dougherty regime), so the **practical industrial sweet-spot is 0.5–1.5 % w/w** for most nanoparticles in automotive oils.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Nanolubricant = base oil/grease + 1–100 nm solid particles (mass fraction 0.1–3 % typically).
- **Four nano-families:** metals, metal oxides, metal dichalcogenides (MoS₂, WS₂), carbon allotropes (graphene, CNT, diamond).
- **Three mechanisms of action:** rolling, mending/tribofilm, thermal-conductivity boost.
- **Stribeck parameter** $\eta N / P$ — nanolubricants win in the **boundary and mixed** regimes, not the full-film regime.
- **Key formulas:** Maxwell $k_{nf}/k_{bf}$ (Eq. 3), Einstein–Batchelor $\eta_{nf}/\eta_{bf}$ (Eq. 4), Archard wear (Eq. 2).
- **Validity:** Maxwell model is for **dilute, spherical, non-interacting** particles only; CNTs and graphene require Nan et al. or Hamilton–Crosser models.
- **Sweet-spot loading:** 0.5–1.5 % w/w in automotive engine oils; above this, viscosity penalty outweighs the conductivity gain.
- **Subsystem map:** engine sump (piston, cam, bearings), transmission (syncro, gears), differential (hypoid), grease points (chassis, hubs), brake fluid, coolant, fuel.
- **Numerical value to memorise:** 0.5–1 % w/w CuO in 15W-40 typically gives 0.3–1 % rise in $k$ and 0.2–0.5 % rise in $\eta$.
- **Pitfall:** Never confuse $\mu$ (COF, dimensionless) with WSD (mm) in numerical answers.
- **Industrial maturity (2024):** MoS₂ and CuO are the most production-ready; graphene and CNTs are still in pilot/field trials for OEMs.
